title: Big-O vs Big-Omega vs Big-Theta
body: Big-O is an upper bound, Big-Omega is a lower bound, and Big-Theta is a tight bound that holds both ways. Saying an algorithm is O(n squared) only promises it grows no faster than that — technically, binary search is also O(n squared).

title: What interviewers really mean by Big-O
body: When interviewers say Big-O, they almost always mean the tight bound, Big-Theta. Give the tightest bound you can prove, and never pad an answer upward just to be safe — calling linear search O(n squared) sounds like you do not understand it.

title: Why constants disappear
body: Asymptotic notation describes growth as n goes to infinity, so constants and lower-order terms vanish: 3n squared plus 50n is just O(n squared). Constants still matter in practice, which is why insertion sort beats merge sort on tiny inputs.

title: Quiz: Tight bounds
body: Which asymptotic notation expresses a tight bound — the function is squeezed both above and below?
payload: {"options": ["Big-O", "Big-Omega", "Big-Theta", "Little-o"], "correct": "Big-Theta", "explain": "Big-Theta means the runtime is bounded above AND below by the same growth rate. Big-O is only an upper bound and Big-Omega only a lower bound."}

title: Best, average, and worst case
body: The same algorithm can have wildly different cases: quicksort averages O(n log n) but degrades to O(n squared) on adversarial pivots. In interviews, state which case you are quoting — hash lookups are O(1) average but O(n) worst case.

title: Amortized is not average case
body: Amortized analysis averages cost over a worst-case sequence of operations on a data structure. Average case averages over random inputs. A dynamic array append is amortized O(1) even though some individual appends cost O(n) — no randomness involved.

title: Quoting the right case out loud
body: A strong interview habit: after coding, say the average case and worst case explicitly, and note when the worst case is realistic. For hash-heavy solutions, mention that pathological collisions exist but are not a practical concern in the problem.

title: Quiz: Dynamic array append
body: What is the amortized time complexity of appending to a dynamic array like a Python list or Java ArrayList?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(1)", "explain": "Occasional resizes cost O(n), but doubling capacity makes those rare enough that the total cost of n appends is O(n), i.e. O(1) amortized per append."}

title: Aggregate method
body: The simplest amortized technique: compute the total cost of n operations, then divide by n. For a dynamic array, n appends cost at most n plus the geometric resize sum 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + n, which is under 3n — so O(1) each.

title: Accounting method
body: The accounting method overcharges cheap operations and banks the difference as credit. Each cheap array append is charged 3 units: one to insert, two saved to pay for copying itself and an older element during the next resize.

title: Potential method
body: The potential method defines a potential function on the data structure state — like stored energy. An operation's amortized cost is its real cost plus the change in potential. Expensive operations drain potential that cheap ones built up.

title: Quiz: Banking credits
body: Which amortized analysis technique explicitly saves credits on cheap operations to pay for expensive ones later?
payload: {"options": ["Aggregate method", "Accounting method", "Potential method", "Master theorem"], "correct": "Accounting method", "explain": "The accounting method overcharges cheap operations and stores the surplus as credit on elements, spending it when an expensive operation occurs."}

title: The complexity ladder
body: Memorize the growth ladder: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n squared) < O(n cubed) < O(2 to the n) < O(n factorial). Everything up to n log n scales to huge inputs; quadratic tops out around n = 10000; exponential dies past n = 25 or so.

title: Recognizing O(log n)
body: If each step throws away a constant fraction of the input — halving in binary search, halving in balanced BST descent — you are in O(log n) territory. Log base does not matter asymptotically since changing base is a constant factor.

title: Exponential and factorial in practice
body: O(2 to the n) means enumerating subsets and O(n factorial) means enumerating permutations — both scream backtracking. They are acceptable only when constraints are tiny, typically n up to about 20 for subsets and 10 to 12 for permutations.

title: Quiz: Halving pattern
body: An algorithm does constant work, then recurses on half of the input. What is its time complexity?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(log n)", "explain": "Discarding half the input each step means only about log2(n) steps before the input shrinks to size 1 — the binary search pattern."}

title: Space vs auxiliary space
body: Total space complexity includes the input; auxiliary space counts only the extra memory your algorithm allocates. When interviewers ask for space complexity of an in-place algorithm, they usually want auxiliary space — which is O(1).

title: The recursion stack counts
body: Recursion is not free space-wise: every stack frame is memory. Recursive DFS on a degenerate tree of n nodes uses O(n) auxiliary space, and even the elegant recursive reversal of a linked list costs O(n) stack.

title: Classic space costs to know
body: Merge sort needs O(n) auxiliary space for its merge buffer; quicksort needs only O(log n) expected stack; heapsort is O(1) auxiliary. Knowing these lets you answer the follow-up question — can you sort with less memory — instantly.

title: Quiz: Merge sort memory
body: What is the auxiliary space complexity of a standard merge sort on an array?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(n)", "explain": "Merging requires a temporary buffer the size of the array. The recursion stack adds O(log n), but the O(n) buffer dominates."}

title: Reading a recurrence
body: Divide-and-conquer runtimes are written as recurrences: T(n) = a T(n/b) + f(n), where a is the number of subproblems, n/b their size, and f(n) the split-and-combine cost. Merge sort is T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n).

title: Master theorem in one line
body: The master theorem compares f(n) against n raised to log base b of a. If the subproblem work dominates, that power wins; if f(n) dominates, f(n) wins; if they tie, multiply by log n. Merge sort ties: n times log n.

title: Recurrences you should solve on sight
body: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) is binary search, O(log n). T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) is merge sort, O(n log n). T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1) is tree traversal, O(n). T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) is a simple loop in disguise, O(n).

title: Quiz: Solve the recurrence
body: What does T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) solve to?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(n log n)", "O(n squared)", "O(log n)"], "correct": "O(n log n)", "explain": "Each of the log n levels of the recursion tree does O(n) total work — the merge sort recurrence."}

title: The recursion tree method
body: Draw the recursion as a tree: cost per node, nodes per level, number of levels. Total runtime is the sum across levels. A branching factor of b and depth d gives roughly b to the d leaves — the source of exponential blowups.

title: Why naive Fibonacci is exponential
body: Naive recursive Fibonacci branches twice per call with depth n, giving O(2 to the n) — the same subproblems recomputed endlessly. Memoize and each of the n distinct subproblems is solved once: O(n). This gap is the whole pitch for dynamic programming.

title: Counting recursion work honestly
body: For backtracking, complexity is roughly the number of nodes in the decision tree times the work per node. Generating all subsets touches 2 to the n nodes with O(n) copy work each: O(n times 2 to the n). Say the per-node cost — interviewers check for it.

title: Quiz: Naive Fibonacci
body: What is the time complexity of the naive recursive Fibonacci with no memoization?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(n squared)", "O(2 to the n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(2 to the n)", "explain": "Each call spawns two more with depth about n, so the call tree has exponentially many nodes, most recomputing the same values."}

title: The 10 to the 8 rule
body: Rough rule: about 10 to the 8 simple operations per second. Given a constraint of n up to 10 to the 5, an O(n squared) solution is 10 to the 10 operations — dead. You need O(n log n) or better. Constraints are the problem telling you the intended complexity.

title: Reading constraints like a menu
body: n up to 10 to the 6: aim for O(n) or O(n log n). n up to a few thousand: O(n squared) is fine. n up to about 20: exponential subsets or bitmask DP is expected. n up to 10 to 12: permutations are on the table.

title: Use constraints to pick your approach
body: Before solving, glance at the input bounds and work backwards to the target complexity. If n is 10 to the 5 and your idea is O(n squared), do not code it — the constraint just told you a better pattern exists, usually sorting, hashing, or two pointers.

title: Quiz: Constraint reading
body: The input size is n = 100000 and the time limit is one second. Which target complexity is the safest bet?
payload: {"options": ["O(n squared)", "O(n log n)", "O(2 to the n)", "O(n cubed)"], "correct": "O(n log n)", "explain": "O(n log n) is about 1.7 million operations here — comfortable. O(n squared) is 10 billion, far beyond the roughly 10 to the 8 ops-per-second budget."}

title: Trading space for time
body: The most common interview optimization is spending memory to buy speed: two sum drops from O(n squared) to O(n) with a hash map, and memoization turns exponential recursion polynomial. When stuck, ask yourself what lookup you keep repeating.

title: Precompute vs on-demand
body: If a value will be queried many times, precompute it: prefix sums cost O(n) once and answer every range-sum query in O(1). One-off queries do not justify the setup. State the query-count assumption when you choose.

title: Volunteer the trade-off
body: Strong candidates narrate trade-offs unprompted: this uses O(n) extra memory to cut time to O(n), or we could save memory with sorting at O(n log n). It signals you see the design space, not just one solution.

title: Quiz: Range sum queries
body: After an O(n) prefix-sum precomputation, how fast is each subsequent range-sum query?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(1)", "explain": "A range sum is just prefix[r+1] minus prefix[l] — two lookups and a subtraction, regardless of range length."}

title: Core structure cheat sheet: lookup
body: Array by index: O(1). Hash map get and put: O(1) average. Balanced BST or TreeMap: O(log n) but keeps keys sorted. Linked list search: O(n). Choosing the structure IS choosing the complexity of your solution.

title: Core structure cheat sheet: insert and delete
body: Array insert or delete in the middle: O(n) shifting. Linked list splice with a pointer in hand: O(1). Hash set add and remove: O(1) average. Heap push and pop: O(log n). Balanced BST insert and delete: O(log n).

title: Core structure cheat sheet: heaps and sorting
body: Heap peek at min or max: O(1); push and pop: O(log n); building a heap from n items: O(n), not n log n. Comparison sorting: O(n log n) is the floor. These four facts settle a surprising number of interview follow-ups.

title: Quiz: Heap peek
body: What is the time complexity of reading the minimum element of a min-heap without removing it?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(1)", "explain": "The minimum sits at the root of a min-heap, so peeking is a single array access. Removal is what costs O(log n)."}

title: Static vs dynamic arrays
body: Static arrays have fixed capacity set at creation; dynamic arrays wrap one and resize automatically. When a dynamic array fills, it allocates a bigger block — typically 1.5x to 2x — and copies everything over in O(n).

title: Why doubling makes append cheap
body: Growing capacity geometrically means resizes get exponentially rarer as the array grows. The copy costs across n appends sum to a geometric series under 2n, so each append is amortized O(1). Growing by a fixed amount instead would make appends amortized O(n).

title: Know your language's dynamic array
body: Python's list, Java's ArrayList, and C++'s vector are all dynamic arrays: O(1) index, O(1) amortized append, O(n) insert or delete in the middle because elements must shift. If you need cheap front insertion, that is a deque, not a list.

title: Quiz: Middle insertion
body: What is the cost of inserting an element into the middle of a dynamic array of n elements?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(n)", "explain": "Every element after the insertion point must shift right by one slot, which is O(n) in the worst case regardless of amortization."}

title: Row-major memory layout
body: A 2D array is stored as one flat block in row-major order: cell (r, c) of an m-by-n grid lives at flat index r times n plus c. This mapping lets you treat a grid as a 1D array and back — handy for binary searching a sorted matrix.

title: Grids are just arrays of arrays
body: In most languages a 2D structure is an array of row arrays. Iterate rows in the outer loop and columns inner — it matches memory layout and cache behavior. Watch for the classic bug of swapping rows and cols bounds on non-square grids.

title: Directions array trick
body: For grid problems, define dirs = ((0,1),(1,0),(0,-1),(-1,0)) and loop over it to visit neighbors, checking bounds each time. It replaces four copy-pasted if blocks and extends cleanly to 8 directions or knight moves.

title: Quiz: Flattening a grid
body: In an m-by-n grid, what is the flat 1D index of cell (r, c) in row-major order?
payload: {"options": ["r * m + c", "r * n + c", "c * n + r", "r + c"], "correct": "r * n + c", "explain": "Each full row before row r contributes n elements, so skip r*n elements, then move c into the current row."}

title: In-place reversal
body: Reverse an array in place with two pointers: swap the ends and walk inward until they meet. O(n) time, O(1) space. This tiny primitive builds bigger tricks like rotation and word reversal.

title: Rotation by triple reversal
body: To rotate an array right by k in place: reverse the whole array, then reverse the first k elements, then reverse the rest. Three O(n) passes, O(1) extra space. Remember to take k modulo n first.

title: Swap-to-partition
body: Many in-place problems reduce to swapping elements into position: move zeroes, sort colors (Dutch national flag), partitioning around a pivot. Keep a write pointer for where the next qualifying element belongs and swap into it.

title: Quiz: In-place rotation
body: Which technique rotates an array by k positions using O(1) extra space?
payload: {"options": ["Copy to a new array", "Triple reversal", "Sorting", "Prefix sums"], "correct": "Triple reversal", "explain": "Reverse the entire array, then reverse the first k and the remaining n-k separately. The double reversal restores order within each block while blocks stay swapped."}

title: String immutability
body: Strings are immutable in Java and Python: every concatenation builds a brand-new string. Appending in a loop copies the growing result each time, turning what looks like O(n) code into O(n squared).

title: String builders
body: The fix for repeated concatenation: Java's StringBuilder, or in Python append parts to a list and join once at the end. Both make n appends O(n) total. In interviews, reaching for the builder unprompted is a fluency signal.

title: Mutable string workaround
body: Need to modify characters? Convert to a char array or Python list, edit in place, and join back at the end. C++ users get a pass — std::string is mutable — but should say so out loud when leaning on it.

title: Quiz: Loop concatenation
body: In Python, what is the total complexity of building a string by doing result += ch inside a loop over n characters?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(n log n)", "O(n squared)", "O(1)"], "correct": "O(n squared)", "explain": "Strings are immutable, so each += copies the entire result so far. The copies sum to 1+2+...+n, which is O(n squared). Use a list and join instead."}

title: ASCII and frequency arrays
body: ASCII covers 128 characters, so for lowercase-only input an int array of size 26 indexed by c minus the character a is a faster, cleaner hash map. For general ASCII use size 128 or 256.

title: Unicode caution
body: Unicode characters can take multiple bytes, and some glyphs are multiple code points. Asking is the input ASCII or Unicode is a classic clarifying question — it decides whether a fixed-size array is safe or a hash map is required.

title: Character arithmetic
body: Characters are numbers underneath: c - 'a' gives a 0-based letter index, and c - '0' converts a digit character to its value. These conversions power frequency counting, radix tricks, and manual atoi implementations.

title: Quiz: Frequency array size
body: The input is guaranteed lowercase English letters. What frequency array size do you need?
payload: {"options": ["26", "52", "128", "256"], "correct": "26", "explain": "There are 26 lowercase letters; index with c minus the character code of a. It beats a hash map on both speed and cleanliness for this constraint."}

title: Subarray vs substring vs subsequence
body: Subarrays and substrings are contiguous slices; subsequences keep relative order but may skip elements. The word in the problem statement is a giant hint: contiguous suggests sliding window or prefix sums, subsequence usually means DP.

title: How many are there
body: An array of length n has n(n+1)/2 nonempty subarrays but 2 to the n subsequences. Any brute force over subsequences is exponential — a red flag that the intended solution is dynamic programming or clever counting.

title: Misreading costs interviews
body: A classic failure: solving longest common substring when the problem said subsequence, or vice versa. Restate the term during clarification — do you mean contiguous — before writing a line of code.

title: Quiz: Counting subarrays
body: How many nonempty contiguous subarrays does an array of length n have?
payload: {"options": ["n", "n(n+1)/2", "2 to the n", "n squared"], "correct": "n(n+1)/2", "explain": "A subarray is defined by its start and end positions: n choices for a single element, plus every pair, giving n(n+1)/2 total."}

title: Prefix sums
body: Precompute prefix[i] as the sum of the first i elements; then any range sum from l to r is prefix[r+1] minus prefix[l] in O(1). One O(n) pass converts repeated range-sum questions from O(n) each to constant time.

title: Prefix sum plus hash map
body: Count subarrays summing to k by walking the array, tracking the running sum, and counting how often running-sum minus k has appeared before in a hash map. O(n) time — and it gracefully handles negative numbers where sliding window fails.

title: Difference arrays
body: To apply many range updates cheaply, add the value at index l and subtract it at r+1 in a difference array, then take a prefix sum at the end to materialize final values. Each update is O(1) instead of O(range length).

title: Quiz: Subarray sum equals k
body: Which technique counts subarrays with sum exactly k in O(n), including with negative numbers?
payload: {"options": ["Sliding window", "Prefix sum with a hash map", "Sorting", "Binary search"], "correct": "Prefix sum with a hash map", "explain": "Track the running sum and count occurrences of runningSum minus k seen so far. Sliding window breaks with negatives because the sum is no longer monotonic as the window grows."}

title: Naive string matching
body: Checking a pattern of length m at every position of a text of length n costs O(n times m). It is fine to start there in an interview — state it as the brute force, then name the faster options.

title: Rabin-Karp rolling hash
body: Rabin-Karp hashes the pattern and slides a window over the text, updating the window hash in O(1) per step: subtract the outgoing character's contribution, multiply by the base, add the incoming one. Average O(n + m); verify matches to handle hash collisions.

title: KMP at awareness level
body: KMP precomputes a failure function over the pattern so the scan never re-reads text characters, guaranteeing O(n + m) worst case. For Google interviews, know the idea and the complexity — full implementation is rarely demanded.

title: Quiz: Rolling hash update
body: In Rabin-Karp, what is the cost of updating the hash when the window slides one character?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(m)", "O(log n)", "O(n)"], "correct": "O(1)", "explain": "Remove the leading character's contribution, shift by the base, add the new character — constant work, which is the entire point of a rolling hash."}

title: Anagrams are frequency counts
body: Two strings are anagrams if their character frequency counts match. Counting is O(n) with a 26-slot array — beating the sort-and-compare approach at O(n log n) — and the same idea powers grouped-anagram hashing by count signature.

title: Palindrome checks
body: Check a palindrome with two pointers walking inward from both ends. For palindromic substrings, expand around each of the 2n minus 1 centers — each character and each gap — giving O(n squared) total, which usually passes.

title: Frequency map as first instinct
body: When a string problem mentions counts, duplicates, or rearrangement, the first tool to reach for is a frequency map. Most easy and medium string questions are one frequency map plus one clean pass away from solved.

title: Quiz: Fastest anagram check
body: What is the optimal way to check whether two strings are anagrams?
payload: {"options": ["Sort both and compare", "Compare frequency counts", "Check every permutation", "Use two pointers"], "correct": "Compare frequency counts", "explain": "Counting characters is O(n) time with O(1) space for a fixed alphabet, beating sorting's O(n log n). Permutation checking is factorial — never."}

title: Spiral traversal
body: Spiral order is boundary bookkeeping: maintain top, bottom, left, right; walk each edge and shrink the boundary inward after consuming it. The classic bug is double-printing the last row or column — guard with boundary checks before the reverse passes.

title: Rotate a matrix in place
body: Rotating a square matrix 90 degrees clockwise equals transposing it, then reversing each row. Both steps are in place, giving O(1) extra space. Counterclockwise is transpose then reverse each column.

title: Diagonal identities
body: Cells on the same top-left-to-bottom-right diagonal share r minus c; cells on the same anti-diagonal share r plus c. Group by those keys in a hash map and diagonal traversal problems collapse to bookkeeping.

title: Quiz: 90-degree rotation
body: Which two operations rotate a square matrix 90 degrees clockwise in place?
payload: {"options": ["Reverse rows, then columns", "Transpose, then reverse each row", "Reverse each row, then transpose... twice", "Swap diagonals only"], "correct": "Transpose, then reverse each row", "explain": "Transposing flips the matrix over its main diagonal; reversing each row then lands every element exactly where a clockwise rotation puts it."}

title: How hash maps work inside
body: A hash map runs a key through a hash function, takes it modulo the bucket count, and stores the entry in that bucket. The load factor — entries divided by buckets — controls how crowded buckets get; keeping it low keeps operations O(1).

title: A good hash function
body: A good hash function is fast, deterministic, and spreads keys uniformly across buckets. Clustering is the enemy: if many keys land in one bucket, that bucket becomes a linear scan and O(1) quietly becomes O(n).

title: Set is a map without values
body: A hash set is a hash map that stores only keys. Same internals, same O(1) average operations. Use a set when you only care about membership and a map when each key carries a payload like a count or an index.

title: Quiz: Load factor
body: What does the load factor of a hash map measure?
payload: {"options": ["Entries divided by buckets", "Buckets divided by entries", "Collisions per lookup", "Memory in bytes"], "correct": "Entries divided by buckets", "explain": "Load factor is occupancy: how full the table is. When it crosses a threshold like 0.75, the map resizes to keep buckets sparse and operations O(1)."}

title: Chaining
body: Chaining resolves collisions by making each bucket a small list of entries; colliding keys just append. Lookups scan the chain. Java's HashMap chains, and since Java 8 it converts long chains into red-black trees to cap worst-case lookups at O(log n).

title: Open addressing
body: Open addressing stores everything in the array itself: on collision, probe for the next open slot — linearly, quadratically, or via a second hash. Python dicts do this. Deletions need tombstones so probe chains do not break.

title: Probing trade-offs
body: Linear probing is cache-friendly but suffers primary clustering, where filled runs grow and snowball. Quadratic probing and double hashing spread probes out to fight clustering at the cost of locality. Know the terms; implementation is rarely asked.

title: Quiz: Collision by chaining
body: In a hash map using chaining, where does a new entry go when its bucket already holds another key?
payload: {"options": ["It replaces the old entry", "It is appended to that bucket's list", "It probes the next bucket", "The insert fails"], "correct": "It is appended to that bucket's list", "explain": "Chaining keeps a list per bucket, so colliding entries coexist in the same bucket and lookups walk the short list comparing keys."}

title: Rehashing on resize
body: When the load factor crosses its threshold, the map allocates roughly double the buckets and re-inserts every entry, because each key's bucket index depends on the table size. That pass is O(n), but doubling makes it rare — inserts stay amortized O(1).

title: Pre-sizing a map
body: If you know you will insert n entries, constructing the map with capacity for n avoids every intermediate rehash. In interviews it is a nice micro-optimization to mention; in production code it is a real win on hot paths.

title: Why order changes after resize
body: Iteration order of a plain hash map can change after a resize because entries move to new buckets. Never depend on hash map order — if order matters, that is a different structure, like a LinkedHashMap or an ordered dict.

title: Quiz: Resize cost
body: A hash map resize re-inserts all n entries, yet insertion is still called amortized O(1). Why?
payload: {"options": ["Resizes never happen", "Doubling makes resizes exponentially rare", "Rehashing is O(1) per entry total", "The OS handles it"], "correct": "Doubling makes resizes exponentially rare", "explain": "Geometric growth means the total work of all resizes over n inserts is O(n), spreading to O(1) per insert — the same argument as dynamic arrays."}

title: When O(1) breaks
body: Hash map operations are O(1) on average but O(n) worst case, when many keys collide into one bucket. Adversarial inputs crafted to collide are a real attack; that is why languages randomize string hash seeds per process.

title: Java's treeified buckets
body: Since Java 8, a HashMap bucket that grows past 8 entries converts its chain into a red-black tree, capping worst-case lookup at O(log n) instead of O(n). A nice detail to drop when an interviewer pushes on worst cases.

title: State both cases
body: When your solution leans on a hash map, quote O(1) average and add that the worst case is O(n) under pathological collisions but is not a practical concern here. That one sentence preempts the standard follow-up.

title: Quiz: Worst-case lookup
body: What is the worst-case lookup time in a classic chained hash map with no treeification?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(n)", "explain": "If every key hashes to the same bucket, lookup degenerates into scanning one list holding all n entries."}

title: The equals and hashCode contract
body: The contract: equal objects must produce equal hash codes. Unequal objects may share a hash — that is just a collision. Break the contract and equal keys land in different buckets, making stored values unfindable.

title: Overriding one without the other
body: The classic Java bug: overriding equals without hashCode. Two logically equal objects get different default hashes, so a map lookup with an equal key misses. Python has the same rule with eq and hash; tuples of fields make easy keys.

title: Hashing a composite key
body: Need a pair or coordinate as a key? Use a tuple in Python, or in Java combine fields like 31 times x plus y, or encode as r times cols plus c for grid cells. Immutable keys only — mutating a key after insert strands the entry.

title: Quiz: The contract
body: Two objects are equal according to equals(). What does the hashing contract require?
payload: {"options": ["They must have equal hash codes", "They must be the same object", "Their hashes must differ", "Nothing is required"], "correct": "They must have equal hash codes", "explain": "Equal objects must hash identically or they land in different buckets and lookups fail. The reverse is not required — unequal objects may collide."}

title: HashMap vs TreeMap
body: HashMap gives O(1) average operations with no ordering; TreeMap is a red-black tree giving O(log n) operations with keys always sorted. TreeMap also answers range and neighbor queries — floorKey, ceilingKey — that a hash map simply cannot.

title: When order matters
body: Reach for an ordered map when you need smallest or largest key, the closest key to a value, or in-order iteration — think calendar booking or a time-based key-value store. If you only ever look up exact keys, the hash map's O(1) wins.

title: Python's dict ordering caveat
body: Python dicts preserve insertion order, which is not sorted order. For a sorted-key structure Python has no built-in TreeMap; interviews allow assuming a sorted container or using the sortedcontainers library — say the assumption out loud.

title: Quiz: Closest key query
body: You need the largest key less than or equal to a query value. Which structure answers this in O(log n)?
payload: {"options": ["HashMap", "TreeMap", "ArrayList", "HashSet"], "correct": "TreeMap", "explain": "A TreeMap keeps keys sorted in a balanced BST, so floor and ceiling queries are O(log n) tree descents. Hash maps scatter keys and cannot answer neighbor queries."}

title: Complement lookup
body: Two sum in O(n): for each number, check whether target minus it is already in your hash map, then insert the current number. One pass, and the pattern — look up the thing that would complete the answer — generalizes everywhere.

title: Frequency maps
body: Count occurrences with map[x] += 1 while scanning. Frequency maps drive anagram grouping, top-k frequent elements, majority element checks, and character-window problems. If a problem says count or most common, start here.

title: Index maps
body: Map each value to the index where it appeared to answer where did I last see this in O(1). This powers two sum returning indices, detecting duplicates within distance k, and finding the first repeated character.

Checking before inserting also prevents pairing an element with itself."}

title: Deduplication in one line
body: Dropping an array into a set removes duplicates in O(n). Comparing the set's size to the original length is the instant has-duplicates check. Sorting also dedupes adjacent values but costs O(n log n).

title: Set operations
body: Intersection, union, and difference of two collections are all O(n + m) with hash sets: build a set from one side and probe with the other. This beats the nested-loop O(n times m) comparison every time.

title: Seen sets in traversal
body: A visited set is the guard rail of graph and grid traversal — without it, cycles loop forever. The same seen-set instinct detects duplicates in streams and cycles in linked structures like the happy number problem.

title: Quiz: Fast intersection
body: What is the time complexity of intersecting two arrays of sizes n and m using a hash set?
payload: {"options": ["O(n times m)", "O(n + m)", "O(n log m)", "O(1)"], "correct": "O(n + m)", "explain": "Build a set from one array in O(n), then probe each of the m elements in O(1) average — linear total, versus quadratic for nested loops."}

title: Rolling hash idea
body: A rolling hash treats a string window as a number in some base: slide the window and update the hash in O(1) by removing the outgoing character's term and adding the incoming one. It turns substring comparison into number comparison.

title: Where rolling hashes shine
body: Rolling hashes power Rabin-Karp matching, detecting repeated substrings of fixed length like repeated DNA sequences, and comparing many same-length windows cheaply. Store window hashes in a set and duplicates surface in O(n).

title: Collisions and verification
body: Different strings can share a hash, so a rolling-hash match is a candidate, not a proof. Verify by direct comparison, or use a large prime modulus — or two independent hashes — to make false positives vanishingly rare. Mention this caveat unprompted.

Pairwise comparison is quadratic."}

title: Singly vs doubly vs circular
body: A singly linked list has next pointers only; doubly linked nodes also point back, buying O(1) deletion when you hold the node; circular lists loop the tail back to the head. Doubly linked plus a hash map is the backbone of an LRU cache.

title: Why linked lists exist
body: Linked lists trade the array's O(1) indexing for O(1) insertion and deletion at a known position — no shifting. Access by position is O(n) because you must walk. If a problem needs both fast index and fast splice, you likely need a hybrid.

title: The null-check discipline
body: Most linked list bugs are null dereferences: advancing past the tail, or assuming a next exists. Before writing node.next.next, ask what happens on an empty list, a single node, and exactly two nodes — those three cases catch nearly everything.

title: Quiz: Deleting a known node
body: You hold a direct reference to a node in a doubly linked list. What does deleting it cost?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)"], "correct": "O(1)", "explain": "Rewire node.prev.next and node.next.prev and it is gone — no traversal needed. In a singly linked list you would need the predecessor, which costs O(n) to find."}

title: Dummy head nodes
body: A dummy or sentinel node placed before the real head means the head is no longer a special case: insertions and deletions at the front use the same code as anywhere else. Return dummy.next at the end and the edge cases evaporate.

title: When to reach for a dummy
body: Any problem that might modify or remove the head — remove elements, merge lists, partition list — is a dummy node problem. Interviewers read the instinct to start with dummy = Node(0); dummy.next = head as a sign of experience.

title: Sentinels beyond lists
body: The sentinel idea generalizes: a phantom element that absorbs boundary logic. Dummy heads and tails in an LRU cache's internal list mean insert-at-front and evict-from-back never touch a null — the same trick, twice in one structure.

title: Quiz: Dummy node purpose
body: What problem does a dummy head node primarily solve?
payload: {"options": ["It makes traversal faster", "It removes special-case handling for the head", "It reduces memory usage", "It enables O(1) indexing"], "correct": "It removes special-case handling for the head", "explain": "With a sentinel before the head, deleting or inserting at position zero uses the same pointer surgery as any other position, and you return dummy.next."}

title: Pointer surgery order
body: Insertion between a and b: set newNode.next to b first, then a.next to newNode. Do it in the wrong order and you lose the rest of the list. The rule: connect the new node outward before breaking any existing link.

title: Deletion means bypassing
body: Deleting a node from a singly linked list means making its predecessor skip it: prev.next = curr.next. You always need a handle on the node before the one you remove — which is exactly why deletion loops track prev and curr together.

title: Splicing sublists
body: To splice a sublist out or in, capture the boundary nodes before disconnecting anything: the node before the segment and the node after it. Reversal of a middle section, k-group operations, and reordering all reduce to careful boundary capture.

Reversing the order in a singly linked list drops your only reference to b and everything after it."}

title: Iterative reversal
body: Reverse a list with three pointers: prev, curr, next. Save next, point curr.next at prev, advance both. When curr hits null, prev is the new head. O(n) time, O(1) space — this must be muscle memory for a Google loop.

title: Recursive reversal
body: Recursive reversal unwinds from the tail: reverse the rest, then set head.next.next = head and head.next = null. Elegant, but it costs O(n) stack space — say that trade-off out loud when you choose it.

title: Partial and k-group reversal
body: Reversing positions m to n, or every k nodes, is full reversal plus boundary splicing: locate the node before the segment, reverse the segment, reconnect both ends. K-group adds a count-ahead check so a short final group stays untouched.

The recursive version holds one stack frame per node, so its call stack is O(n)."}

title: Floyd's cycle detection
body: Run a slow pointer one step at a time and a fast pointer two steps. If the list has a cycle, they must meet inside it; if fast hits null, the list is clean. O(n) time, O(1) space — no visited set needed.

title: Finding the cycle entrance
body: After slow and fast meet inside the cycle, reset one pointer to the head and advance both one step at a time — they meet exactly at the cycle's entry node. That is the linked list cycle II result, and it also solves find-the-duplicate-number.

title: Middle of the list
body: Fast and slow pointers find the middle in one pass: when fast reaches the end, slow is halfway. This seeds palindrome checking, list splitting for merge sort, and reorder-list — all of which start with find the middle, then act.

title: Quiz: Detecting a cycle

A visited set also works but costs O(n) space."}

title: Merging two sorted lists
body: Merge two sorted lists with a dummy head and a tail pointer: repeatedly attach the smaller head and advance that list. O(n plus m) time, O(1) space. This is the merge step of merge sort and the base case of merge k lists.

title: Splitting a list
body: Split a list by finding the middle with fast and slow pointers, then cutting: second = slow.next; slow.next = null. Forgetting to sever the link is the classic bug — the halves stay connected and recursion never terminates.

title: Merge sort is the list sort
body: Merge sort is the natural O(n log n) sort for linked lists: splitting and merging need no random access, and the merge is O(1) extra space. Quicksort's index-based partitioning is exactly what lists are bad at.

title: Quiz: Sorting a linked list

title: Intersection by length alignment
body: To find where two lists intersect, compute both lengths, advance the longer list's pointer by the difference, then walk both together until the pointers are equal. Compare node references, not values — different nodes can share a value.

title: The two-pointer switcheroo
body: The elegant intersection trick: walk pointer A through list A then list B, and pointer B through list B then list A. Both travel the same total distance, so they meet at the intersection node or at null together. No length counting needed.

title: Intersection means shared tail
body: Two singly linked lists that intersect share every node from the intersection onward — a Y shape, never an X — because each node has exactly one next pointer. Realizing this shape is what makes the alignment tricks valid.

title: Quiz: Detecting intersection

title: Copying a list with random pointers
body: Each node has a next and a random pointer to any node. The hash map solution: first pass clones every node into a map from original to copy; second pass wires copy.next and copy.random via the map. O(n) time, O(n) space.

title: The interleaving trick
body: The O(1) space version interleaves: insert each clone right after its original, so original.random.next is exactly the clone's random target. Set randoms in a second pass, then unzip the two lists in a third. Three clean passes.

title: Why this problem is asked
body: Copy list with random pointer tests whether you can map an arbitrary object graph — the same idea as clone graph, just linear. The insight to remember: you need a correspondence from original nodes to their copies, stored in a map or in the structure itself.

title: LRU cache anatomy
body: An LRU cache is a hash map pointing into a doubly linked list. The map gives O(1) key lookup; the list keeps recency order with most-recent at the front. Get and put both run in O(1) — the whole point of the design.

title: LRU operations
body: On get: find the node via the map and move it to the front. On put: insert or update at the front; if capacity is exceeded, remove the back node and delete its key from the map. Dummy head and tail sentinels keep every case uniform.

title: Why a doubly linked list
body: Moving a node to the front requires removing it from the middle in O(1), which needs a prev pointer — hence doubly linked. A singly linked list would make each move-to-front O(n), destroying the design. Expect exactly this follow-up question.

This is a Google classic — know it cold."}

title: Stacks: last in, first out
body: A stack supports push, pop, and peek in O(1). It models anything that unwinds in reverse order: the call stack, undo history, backtracking, and matching nested structures like brackets and directory paths.

title: The call stack is a stack
body: Every recursive algorithm secretly uses a stack — the call stack. That is why any recursion can be rewritten iteratively with an explicit stack, and why deep recursion overflows: each frame is a push that never popped.

title: Spotting a stack problem
body: Reach for a stack when the most recent unresolved thing matters: unmatched brackets, the previous smaller element, un-collapsed folders in a path, or pending operators in an expression. Recency plus nesting equals stack.

title: Quiz: Stack ordering

payload: {"options": ["1, 2, 3", "3, 2, 1", "2, 1, 3", "Random order"], "correct": "3, 2, 1", "explain": "LIFO: last in, first out. The most recently pushed element sits on top and pops first — the defining property of a stack."}

title: Queues: first in, first out
body: A queue enqueues at the back and dequeues at the front, both O(1). It preserves arrival order, which is why BFS uses one: nodes are processed in the order discovered, giving level-by-level exploration.

title: Ring buffers
body: A circular queue or ring buffer wraps a fixed array with head and tail indices advancing modulo capacity. No shifting, no reallocation — O(1) operations in constant memory, which is why it backs real-world producer-consumer pipes.

title: The full-versus-empty ambiguity
body: In a ring buffer, head equals tail could mean empty or full. Standard fixes: keep an explicit count, or sacrifice one slot so full means tail plus one equals head. Design-a-circular-queue interview questions are testing exactly this detail.

title: Quiz: BFS's data structure

title: Deques
body: A deque supports O(1) push and pop at both ends — a stack and a queue in one structure. Python's collections.deque and Java's ArrayDeque are the standard implementations, and ArrayDeque is Java's recommended stack over the legacy Stack class.

title: When you need both ends
body: Sliding window maximum needs to evict stale elements from the front while pruning dominated elements from the back — only a deque does both in O(1). Palindrome checking by comparing both ends is another natural deque fit.

title: Deque as the default
body: In Python, use deque for any queue: popping from the front of a list is O(n) because everything shifts. deque.popleft is O(1). Mentioning this instead of using list.pop(0) is a small but real fluency signal.

title: Quiz: Python queue pitfall

title: Queue from two stacks
body: Implement a queue with an in-stack and an out-stack: enqueue pushes onto in; dequeue pops from out, refilling it by dumping all of in when empty. Each element moves at most twice, so operations are amortized O(1).

title: Stack from queues
body: The reverse — a stack from queues — is clumsier: on push, enqueue the new element then rotate the rest of the queue behind it, making push O(n) and pop O(1). The asymmetry itself is a worthwhile talking point.

title: Why interviewers ask this
body: These conversions test whether you understand the structures rather than just call them. The key insight: two LIFO reversals compose into FIFO order. Say that sentence and the design explains itself.

title: Monotonic stacks
body: A monotonic stack keeps its contents sorted by popping any element the newcomer would violate. Each element pushes and pops at most once, so a full scan is O(n). It answers nearest-greater and nearest-smaller questions in one pass.

title: Next greater element
body: Next greater element: scan the array; while the stack top is smaller than the current value, pop it — the current value is its answer — then push the current index. Daily temperatures is this exact pattern with distances.

title: The hard classics
body: Largest rectangle in histogram and trapping rain water are monotonic stack problems: the stack tracks bars whose span is not yet resolved, and each pop computes an area using the popped bar's height and the width to the new stack top.

The nested loop appearance hides a linear bound."}

title: Monotonic deque
body: Sliding window maximum in O(n): keep a deque of indices with values in decreasing order. Pop the back while the new value dominates it, pop the front when it slides out of the window — the front is always the current maximum.

title: Why dominated elements can go
body: If a newer element is larger than an older one, the older can never be a future window's maximum — the newer element outlives and outranks it. Discarding dominated elements is what keeps every element's total work O(1).

title: Deque versus heap for windows
body: A max-heap also solves sliding window maximum but costs O(n log n) with lazy deletion of expired entries. The monotonic deque is strictly better here at O(n). Comparing the two unprompted is exactly the trade-off talk interviewers want.

title: Quiz: Window maximum

title: Min-stack
body: A min-stack returns the minimum in O(1) by pairing the main stack with an auxiliary stack that records the minimum so far at each depth. Push compares against the aux top; pop pops both. Same trick mirrored gives a max-stack.

title: The one-stack variant
body: Space-tighter version: push onto the aux stack only when the new value is less than or equal to the current min, and pop it only when the popped value equals the aux top. The less-than-or-equal detail is what makes duplicates safe.

title: Auxiliary structure thinking
body: Min-stack teaches a general design move: when a structure must answer an extra query in O(1), attach a shadow structure maintaining that answer at every state. The pattern reappears in max-queue and in O(1) getRandom designs.

title: Balanced brackets
body: Valid parentheses: push every opener; on a closer, the stack top must be its matching opener — pop it or fail. Finish with an empty stack. Nearly every parsing problem is this skeleton with more cases layered on.

title: Postfix evaluation
body: Postfix or RPN expressions evaluate with one stack: push numbers; on an operator, pop two operands, apply, push the result. No precedence rules needed — the notation encodes them — which is exactly why compilers like it.

title: The calculator pattern
body: Basic calculator problems handle infix with a stack: track a running number, a current sign, and on parentheses push the accumulated result and sign, then restore on close. Work one careful example by hand before coding — sign bugs hide everywhere.

Mind the order: the second pop is the left operand for subtraction and division."}

title: Tree vocabulary
body: Depth is the distance from the root down to a node; height is the distance from a node down to its deepest leaf. A complete tree fills levels left to right; a full tree gives every node zero or two children; a perfect tree is full with all leaves level.

title: Balanced means log n
body: A balanced binary tree keeps left and right subtree heights within one of each other at every node, guaranteeing height O(log n). That height bound is why BST operations, heap operations, and TreeMap queries all run in O(log n).

title: Why definitions get tested
body: Interviewers probe terminology because sloppy definitions cause real bugs: computing depth when the problem wants height, or assuming complete when the tree is merely balanced. Restate the definition you are using before you code with it.

Mixing them up flips recursions upside down."}

title: The three DFS orders
body: Preorder visits node, left, right — good for copying and serializing. Inorder visits left, node, right — sorted order in a BST. Postorder visits left, right, node — children before parent, so use it when a node's answer depends on its subtrees.

title: Iterative traversal
body: Iterative preorder is a stack: pop a node, visit it, push right then left. Iterative inorder walks left pushing everything, then pops and steps right. Being able to write inorder without recursion is a common Google warm-up.

title: Choosing an order
body: Ask what information flows which way: top-down (pass state into children, like path sums so far) suggests preorder thinking; bottom-up (combine children's answers, like height or diameter) is postorder. Most tree bugs are order mismatches.

title: Quiz: Sorted traversal

title: Level order with a queue
body: BFS on a tree uses a queue: pop a node, visit it, enqueue its children. To process level by level, snapshot the queue size at the start of each level and loop exactly that many pops — that count is the entire trick.

title: Level-order variants
body: Zigzag order alternates a reverse flag per level; right side view keeps each level's last node; average of levels sums per level. All are the same size-snapshot BFS loop with two lines changed — recognize the family and they are free points.

title: BFS vs DFS on trees
body: BFS finds the shallowest anything first — minimum depth, first node meeting a condition — and its queue can hold an entire level, O(n) wide. DFS is O(height) space and suits path and subtree problems. Choose by what the question rewards.

Loop that many dequeues and everything you enqueue belongs to the next level. Null markers work but are clunkier."}

title: The BST invariant
body: In a binary search tree, every node in the left subtree is smaller than the node and every node in the right subtree is larger — recursively, for the entire subtrees, not just the immediate children. Search follows one comparison per level: O(height).

title: BST insert and delete
body: Insert descends like a search and attaches at the null it reaches. Delete has three cases: a leaf just unlinks; one child gets promoted; two children means replace the node's value with its inorder successor, then delete that successor from the right subtree.

title: O(log n) has fine print
body: BST operations are O(height), which is O(log n) only when balanced. Insert sorted data into a naive BST and it degenerates into a linked list with O(n) operations. That caveat is the standard follow-up — volunteer it first.

The inorder predecessor works symmetrically."}

title: Validating a BST correctly
body: The classic wrong answer checks only each node against its children. A right-subtree node can violate a grandparent while satisfying its parent. Correct validation passes down a (min, max) range that tightens at every step.

title: Validation by inorder
body: Alternative: inorder traversal of a valid BST is strictly increasing, so walk inorder and compare each value to the previous one. Same O(n), and the strictly matters — decide with the interviewer how duplicates are defined before coding.

title: Inorder-is-sorted as a tool
body: The inorder-equals-sorted property solves more than validation: kth smallest element is the kth inorder visit, recover-swapped-BST finds the inversion in the inorder sequence, and BST-to-sorted-list is just inorder with pointer stitching.

title: Quiz: The validation trap

Passing (min, max) bounds down enforces the invariant over whole subtrees."}

title: LCA in a BST
body: In a BST, the lowest common ancestor of p and q is the first node where they split: descend while both values are on the same side, and the first node between them (or equal to one) is the answer. O(height), no extra space.

title: LCA in a general tree
body: Without BST ordering, recurse: if the current node is null, p, or q, return it; otherwise ask both subtrees. If both sides return non-null, the current node is the LCA; if one side does, pass it up. One elegant postorder pass.

title: Why LCA matters
body: LCA is the meeting point for distance-between-nodes (dist p to q equals dist to LCA from each) and appears inside harder problems like burning a tree from a leaf. Google asks both variants — know why the BST version cannot be used generally.

title: Quiz: General LCA logic
body: In general-tree LCA recursion, both left and right calls return non-null. What does that mean?
payload: {"options": ["The tree is invalid", "The current node is the LCA", "p and q are duplicates", "Keep recursing upward"], "correct": "The current node is the LCA", "explain": "Non-null from both sides means p and q were found in different subtrees, so they split exactly here — the definition of the lowest common ancestor."}

title: Height in one recursion
body: Height of a tree: one plus the max of the children's heights, with null returning minus one (or zero, depending on your edge-versus-node convention — state which you use). This four-line recursion is the template for half of all tree problems.

title: Diameter
body: The diameter — the longest path between any two nodes — at each node is left height plus right height plus the connecting edges. Compute height postorder and update a global max as you go: O(n), one pass. The path need not pass through the root.

title: Balance checking
body: Checking balance naively recomputes heights at every node: O(n squared). The O(n) version returns height and a balanced flag together — or returns minus one as a sentinel for unbalanced — so each node is visited once. Interviewers watch for exactly this upgrade.

title: Quiz: Diameter at a node
body: When computing tree diameter, what quantity do you evaluate at each node?
payload: {"options": ["Left height plus right height", "Max of children's diameters only", "Node count in its subtree", "Depth from the root"], "correct": "Left height plus right height", "explain": "The longest path through a node stitches its deepest left descent to its deepest right descent. Track the max of this over all nodes while computing heights postorder."}

title: Root-to-leaf path sums
body: Path sum questions pass state downward: carry the remaining target into each child and check it at leaves. Collecting all paths adds a backtracking list — append on the way down, pop on the way back up.

title: Max path sum
body: Binary tree maximum path sum is the hard classic: each node returns its best downward gain — node value plus the larger child gain, clamped at zero — while the global answer considers node plus both gains. The clamp-negative-to-zero step is the crux.

title: Paths not through the root
body: Path-counting problems like path sum III use a prefix-sum map over the current root-to-node path: count how many ancestors have prefix sum equal to current sum minus target. Increment entering a node, decrement leaving — backtracking a hash map.

title: Quiz: Max path sum clamp
body: In maximum path sum, why clamp a child's contribution at zero?
payload: {"options": ["To avoid overflow", "A negative subtree path only hurts, so exclude it", "Zero is the base case", "To keep the recursion balanced"], "correct": "A negative subtree path only hurts, so exclude it", "explain": "If the best path down a child sums negative, attaching it lowers any path through the node. Taking max(gain, 0) models the choice to simply not extend that way."}

title: Rebuilding from preorder and inorder
body: Preorder's first element is the root; find it in inorder — everything left of it is the left subtree, everything right is the right subtree. Recurse on the slices. A value-to-index hash map for inorder keeps the build O(n).

title: Which traversal pairs work
body: Preorder plus inorder or postorder plus inorder uniquely rebuild a binary tree. Preorder plus postorder alone does not — a single-child node is ambiguous between left and right. Knowing why earns the follow-up point.

title: Index arithmetic over slicing
body: Passing array copies at each recursion level costs O(n squared) in the worst case. Pass index boundaries into the recursion instead and the construction is a clean O(n). Interviewers specifically watch whether you slice or index.

title: Quiz: Reconstruction roles
body: Rebuilding a tree from preorder and inorder, what does the preorder array contribute?
payload: {"options": ["The subtree sizes", "The root of each subtree, in construction order", "The sorted values", "The leaf order"], "correct": "The root of each subtree, in construction order", "explain": "Preorder is root-first, so consuming it left to right always hands you the next subtree's root; inorder then splits values into that root's left and right subtrees."}

title: Serializing a tree
body: Preorder serialization with explicit null markers — like 1,2,#,#,3 — captures a binary tree's exact structure in one pass. Deserialization consumes tokens in the same order, building the node then recursing left and right. The null markers do all the work.

title: BFS serialization
body: Level-order serialization, like LeetCode's bracket format, rebuilds with a queue: attach the next two tokens as children of each dequeued node. Either style is acceptable in interviews — pick one, state the format, and stay consistent.

title: Serialization special cases
body: BSTs serialize without null markers: preorder alone suffices because value bounds determine structure during rebuild. And design-wise, remember delimiters matter — multi-digit and negative values break character-by-character parsing. Split on a separator.

title: Quiz: Why null markers
body: Why does preorder serialization of a general binary tree include null markers?
payload: {"options": ["To save space", "Preorder values alone cannot pin down the structure", "They speed up parsing", "They mark the tree's height"], "correct": "Preorder values alone cannot pin down the structure", "explain": "Without nulls, you cannot tell where a left subtree ends and a right begins. Explicit null leaves make the preorder sequence a unique, replayable construction script."}

title: Why self-balancing trees exist
body: AVL and red-black trees rotate on insert and delete to keep height O(log n), preventing the sorted-input degeneration of naive BSTs. They are why TreeMap and C++ std::map guarantee O(log n) — awareness of that is what interviews require, not rotation code.

title: AVL vs red-black
body: AVL balances strictly (heights within one), giving faster lookups but more rotation work per update. Red-black tolerates looser balance for cheaper updates, which is why standard libraries chose it. One sentence each is all the depth you need.

title: The interview move
body: When a problem needs sorted order with dynamic inserts and O(log n) queries, say: I would use a self-balancing BST, in Java a TreeMap. Then use its API. Nobody expects you to implement rotations in 45 minutes — reaching for the right tool is the test.

title: Quiz: The guarantee
body: What do self-balancing trees like AVL and red-black fundamentally guarantee?
payload: {"options": ["O(1) lookups", "Height stays O(log n) under inserts and deletes", "Sorted arrays", "No duplicate keys"], "correct": "Height stays O(log n) under inserts and deletes", "explain": "Rotations repair balance after each update, so operations never degrade to the O(n) of a degenerate BST — regardless of insertion order."}

title: N-ary trees
body: N-ary tree nodes hold a list of children instead of left and right. Traversals generalize directly: preorder visits the node then each child in order; BFS enqueues all children. File systems, org charts, and the DOM are all n-ary trees.

title: Adapting binary techniques
body: Most binary-tree logic ports to n-ary by replacing the left-right pair with a loop over children: max depth is one plus the max over children, serialization records a child count per node. If you know the binary version, say the generalization out loud.

title: Left-child right-sibling
body: Any n-ary tree encodes as a binary tree: each node's left pointer holds its first child and its right pointer holds its next sibling. A niche but classic conversion — worth recognizing if an interviewer steers toward it.

title: Quiz: N-ary depth
body: How does maximum-depth recursion change from binary to n-ary trees?
payload: {"options": ["It becomes iterative only", "Take one plus the max over all children instead of two", "It requires a heap", "Depth is no longer defined"], "correct": "Take one plus the max over all children instead of two", "explain": "The structure of the recursion is identical; the two-way max simply becomes a max over the children list. Most binary tree patterns generalize this way."}

title: Trie structure
body: A trie stores strings character by character down a tree: each node holds children (a 26-slot array or a map) and an end-of-word flag. Insert, search, and startsWith all cost O(word length) — independent of how many words are stored.

title: Prefix queries are the point
body: Hash sets check exact membership but cannot answer does any word start with pre without scanning everything. Tries make prefix queries native — which is why autocomplete, typeahead, and word-search puzzles all reach for one.

title: Trie plus backtracking
body: Word Search II — find dictionary words in a letter grid — is the trie showcase: walk the grid with DFS while simultaneously descending the trie, pruning any path the trie says no word continues. The trie turns exponential search into feasible search.

title: Quiz: Trie search cost
body: What does searching for a word of length L cost in a trie holding n words?
payload: {"options": ["O(L)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)", "O(L times n)"], "correct": "O(L)", "explain": "Search descends one child edge per character, touching L nodes regardless of dictionary size — the property that makes tries ideal for prefix-heavy workloads."}

title: Segment trees in one breath
body: A segment tree stores aggregates like sum, min, or max over halved ranges, answering range queries and point updates in O(log n) each after an O(n) build. Awareness level for Google: know when it applies, not the full implementation.

title: Fenwick trees
body: A binary indexed tree (Fenwick) handles prefix sums with point updates in O(log n) using clever index arithmetic on an array — smaller and simpler than a segment tree but limited to invertible operations like sums, not min or max.

title: When to name them
body: The trigger: many range queries interleaved with updates. A static array wants plain prefix sums; updates break them. Saying a segment tree or BIT would give O(log n) per operation here — and then coding the simpler expected solution — is the right interview calibration.

title: Quiz: Choosing the structure
body: You face millions of range-sum queries interleaved with point updates. Which structure fits?
payload: {"options": ["Plain prefix sums", "A segment tree or Fenwick tree", "A hash map", "A sorted array"], "correct": "A segment tree or Fenwick tree", "explain": "Prefix sums answer queries in O(1) but an update invalidates O(n) of them. Segment and Fenwick trees balance both at O(log n) per operation."}

title: Tree DP in plain terms
body: Tree DP computes each node's answer from its children's answers in postorder: return small summaries upward, combine at the parent. Height, diameter, and counting nodes are all baby tree DP — the pattern just scales up.

title: Returning tuples upward
body: Harder tree DP returns multiple values per node — house robber III returns (best if robbed, best if skipped) per subtree; the parent combines them under the no-adjacent constraint. When one number is not enough, return a tuple of states.

title: Global versus returned value
body: A recurring tree DP shape: the value a node returns to its parent (a path going up) differs from the value used to update the global answer (a path bending through the node). Diameter and max path sum both hinge on that distinction — name it explicitly.

title: Quiz: Tree DP direction
body: Tree DP naturally computes answers in which traversal order?
payload: {"options": ["Preorder, top down", "Postorder, children before parent", "Level order", "Reverse inorder"], "correct": "Postorder, children before parent", "explain": "A node's DP value depends on its children's values, so children must finish first — which is exactly what postorder guarantees."}

title: Heaps live in arrays
body: A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored flat in an array: node i has children at 2i+1 and 2i+2 and parent at (i-1)/2. Completeness means no gaps, so the array encoding wastes nothing and needs no pointers.

title: The heap property
body: A min-heap keeps every parent less than or equal to its children, so the global minimum sits at the root — but siblings have no order, and the array is not sorted. A heap answers what is the smallest instantly, and nothing else.

title: Priority queue equals heap
body: A priority queue is the abstract interface — insert, and extract the highest priority — and a binary heap is its standard implementation: O(log n) push and pop, O(1) peek. Python heapq, Java PriorityQueue, C++ priority_queue.

title: Quiz: Heap array math
body: In a zero-indexed array heap, where are the children of node i?
payload: {"options": ["2i and 2i+1", "2i+1 and 2i+2", "i+1 and i+2", "i/2 and i/2+1"], "correct": "2i+1 and 2i+2", "explain": "Zero-indexed heaps place children at 2i+1 and 2i+2 with the parent at (i-1)/2. The 2i and 2i+1 version is the one-indexed convention."}

title: Sift up and sift down
body: Insertion appends at the end and sifts up, swapping with the parent while it violates the heap property. Extraction moves the last element to the root and sifts down, swapping with the smaller child. Both walk one path of height log n.

title: Build-heap is O(n)
body: Building a heap from n items by inserting one at a time is O(n log n) — but heapifying in place, sifting down from the last parent backwards, is O(n). Most nodes sit near the bottom with tiny sift distances, and the level-weighted sum converges to linear.

title: Where O(n) build matters
body: The linear build shows up in heapsort's first phase and any solve that starts with heapify everything. Quoting build-heap as O(n) instead of O(n log n) is a small correctness detail interviewers genuinely notice.

title: Quiz: Heapify cost
body: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an unsorted array of n elements using bottom-up heapify?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(n log n)", "O(log n)", "O(n squared)"], "correct": "O(n)", "explain": "Sift-down work is proportional to node height, and most nodes are near the leaves with height near zero — the weighted sum across levels is bounded by O(n)."}

title: Min vs max heap
body: A min-heap surfaces the smallest element; a max-heap the largest. The counterintuitive pairing: keep the k LARGEST items with a MIN-heap of size k — the root is the weakest member, evicted whenever something bigger arrives.

title: Faking a max-heap
body: Python heapq is min-only: push negated values and negate again on pop, or push tuples with a negated priority first. Java and C++ accept a comparator instead. Doing this smoothly, without ceremony, is a fluency tell.

title: Tuples as heap entries
body: Heaps compare whole entries, so push tuples with the priority first: (distance, node) for Dijkstra, (count, char) for frequency problems. Watch for ties — if second elements are not comparable, add a tiebreaker index.

title: Quiz: K largest, which heap
body: To maintain the k largest elements of a stream, which heap do you keep at size k?
payload: {"options": ["A max-heap", "A min-heap", "Two heaps", "A sorted array"], "correct": "A min-heap", "explain": "The min-heap root is the smallest of your current top k — the element on the chopping block. If a newcomer beats it, pop and push; otherwise ignore."}

title: Top-K pattern
body: Top k of n elements: keep a size-k heap and stream everything through it — O(n log k), much better than sorting at O(n log n) when k is small. Works for k largest numbers, k most frequent words, k closest points to origin.

title: K most frequent
body: K most frequent elements is a two-step: build a frequency map, then heap the (count, item) pairs — size-k min-heap for O(n log k). Bucket sort by count achieves O(n) if the interviewer pushes past the heap answer.

title: Say the log k
body: The point of the size-k heap is that operations cost log k, not log n. Saying O(n log k) — and noting it beats sorting when k is much smaller than n — is precisely the complexity nuance top-K questions exist to test.

title: Quiz: Top-K complexity
body: What does finding the k largest of n elements cost with a size-k min-heap?
payload: {"options": ["O(n log n)", "O(n log k)", "O(k log n)", "O(n + k)"], "correct": "O(n log k)", "explain": "Each of the n elements does at most one push and pop on a heap that never exceeds k entries, so each operation is O(log k)."}

title: Kth largest, two ways
body: Kth largest element: a size-k min-heap gives O(n log k) guaranteed; quickselect gives O(n) average but O(n squared) worst case. Presenting both and letting the interviewer pick the trade-off is the strongest move.

title: Quickselect in one line
body: Quickselect partitions like quicksort but recurses into only the side containing the kth position, halving expected work each round: n + n/2 + n/4 + ... = O(n) average. Random pivots make the worst case vanishingly unlikely.

title: Which to pick when
body: Streaming input or need the top k continuously? Heap. One-shot query on an array you may mutate? Quickselect. Guaranteed worst-case bound demanded? Heap again, or mention median-of-medians exists and move on.

title: Quiz: Quickselect average
body: What is quickselect's average time complexity for the kth largest element?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(n log n)", "O(k log n)", "O(log n)"], "correct": "O(n)", "explain": "Each partition discards one side, so expected work is a geometric series n + n/2 + n/4 + ... which sums to O(n). The worst case is O(n squared) with bad pivots."}

title: Median of a stream
body: Find median from a data stream: a max-heap holds the lower half, a min-heap the upper half. The median is a root (or the average of both roots). This two-heap split is one of the most-asked heap designs at Google.

title: Keeping the halves balanced
body: The invariant: sizes differ by at most one, and every lower-half element is at most every upper-half element. Standard insert: push onto one heap, pop its root onto the other, then rebalance sizes if needed — three O(log n) steps, no case analysis.

title: Two heaps beyond medians
body: The pattern generalizes to any running percentile and to problems needing both the max of one group and the min of another simultaneously — like sliding window median, which adds lazy deletion on top.

title: Quiz: Stream median layout
body: In the two-heap median design, which heap holds which half of the data?
payload: {"options": ["Min-heap lower half, max-heap upper half", "Max-heap lower half, min-heap upper half", "Both max-heaps", "Both min-heaps"], "correct": "Max-heap lower half, min-heap upper half", "explain": "You need instant access to the LARGEST of the small half and the SMALLEST of the large half — the two values adjacent to the median — so the heaps face each other."}

title: K-way merge
body: Merge k sorted lists with a min-heap of each list's current head: pop the smallest overall, append it to the output, push that list's next element. Every element passes through the heap once: O(n log k) for n total elements.

title: What goes in the heap entry
body: Push (value, listIndex, elementIndex) so a popped entry knows where its successor lives. In Java, a comparator on the node value lets you push list nodes directly. Forgetting the successor bookkeeping is the common stumble.

title: K-way merge in disguise
body: Kth smallest in a sorted matrix, smallest range covering k lists, and ugly numbers are all k-way merges: several sorted streams, repeatedly take the global minimum. If sorted sequences must interleave, think heap of heads.

title: Quiz: Merging k lists
body: What is the complexity of merging k sorted lists totaling n elements using a min-heap?
payload: {"options": ["O(n log k)", "O(n log n)", "O(nk)", "O(k log n)"], "correct": "O(n log k)", "explain": "The heap only ever holds one head per list — k entries — so each of the n elements pays O(log k) to pass through."}

title: Meeting rooms II
body: Minimum meeting rooms: sort intervals by start, keep a min-heap of end times. For each meeting, if the earliest-ending room frees up before it starts, pop; always push the new end. The heap's peak size is the answer.

title: Task scheduler thinking
body: Task scheduler with cooldowns pairs a max-heap of remaining counts with a cooldown queue: greedily run the most frequent available task, park it until its cooldown expires, re-add it. Heap picks the best now; queue enforces the constraint.

title: Greedy plus heap
body: The general shape: a greedy strategy needs the current best candidate at every step, and a heap serves it in O(log n). CPU scheduling, connect ropes with minimum cost, and furthest building via ladders all follow this template.

title: Quiz: Meeting rooms heap
body: In meeting rooms II, what does the min-heap store?
payload: {"options": ["Meeting start times", "Meeting end times of occupied rooms", "Meeting durations", "Room numbers"], "correct": "Meeting end times of occupied rooms", "explain": "The root is the room that frees earliest. If the next meeting starts after that end time, reuse the room (pop); otherwise a new room is needed. Peak heap size is the answer."}

title: Lazy deletion
body: Heaps cannot delete arbitrary elements efficiently — so do not. Mark entries invalid in a side map (or rely on stale values) and discard them when they surface at the root: while the top is outdated, pop. Cost amortizes to nothing extra.

title: Where lazy deletion appears
body: Sliding window median evicts elements that left the window lazily; Dijkstra pushes duplicate entries when a node's distance improves and skips stale ones on pop. Both avoid the O(n) search a true heap delete would need.

title: The skip-on-pop guard
body: The implementation is one guard clause: on pop, if the entry is stale — marked deleted, or worse than the recorded best — continue. State the invariant that the root is always valid after the purge loop, and the interviewer relaxes.

title: Quiz: Stale heap entries
body: In Dijkstra with a binary heap, a node is popped with a distance larger than its recorded best. What do you do?
payload: {"options": ["Update the graph", "Skip it and continue", "Re-push it", "Terminate the algorithm"], "correct": "Skip it and continue", "explain": "It is a stale duplicate from before a better path was found. Lazy deletion means duplicates are allowed in; the skip guard keeps them from doing harm."}

title: Adjacency list vs matrix
body: An adjacency list maps each node to its neighbors: O(V + E) space, perfect for sparse graphs, and iterating neighbors is proportional to degree. An adjacency matrix costs O(V squared) space but answers is there an edge in O(1). Interviews default to lists.

title: Building the graph is step one
body: Most graph problems hand you an edge list — pairs like (u, v) — and your first coding act is converting it into an adjacency list with a map of node to neighbor list. For undirected graphs, add the edge both ways. Two minutes, easy to fumble under pressure.

title: Choosing a representation
body: Dense graph or constant edge-existence checks: matrix. Sparse graph and neighbor iteration — which is nearly every interview problem: list. Algorithms that only relax all edges, like Bellman-Ford, can work straight off the edge list.

title: Quiz: Sparse graph storage
body: Which representation stores a sparse graph of V nodes and E edges most efficiently?
payload: {"options": ["Adjacency matrix", "Adjacency list", "A 2D boolean grid", "A hash of all paths"], "correct": "Adjacency list", "explain": "Lists take O(V + E) space and make neighbor iteration proportional to actual degree. A matrix burns O(V squared) regardless of how few edges exist."}

title: Directed vs undirected
body: An undirected edge is friendship — both ways; a directed edge is following — one way. It changes everything downstream: cycle detection logic, whether components mean connected or strongly connected, and whether you add edges once or twice while building.

title: Weighted vs unweighted
body: Unweighted shortest paths are BFS. Add weights and BFS breaks — you need Dijkstra. Negative weights break Dijkstra — you need Bellman-Ford. This escalation ladder is one of the most reliable interview follow-up sequences; know your next rung.

title: DAGs are special
body: A directed acyclic graph admits a topological order, which unlocks linear-time DP over the graph and makes shortest paths solvable without Dijkstra. When you prove a graph has no cycles, say the word DAG — it signals the whole toolkit.

title: Quiz: The escalation ladder
body: Shortest path in a graph with positive edge weights — which algorithm?
payload: {"options": ["BFS", "Dijkstra", "DFS", "Union-Find"], "correct": "Dijkstra", "explain": "BFS counts hops, so it only works unweighted. Dijkstra greedily settles the closest unsettled node and is correct for non-negative weights; negatives escalate to Bellman-Ford."}

title: BFS mechanics
body: BFS: seed a queue, and loop — pop a node, process it, enqueue unvisited neighbors, marking them visited AS you enqueue. Marking on enqueue rather than on pop prevents the same node entering the queue twice.

title: BFS finds shortest unweighted paths
body: BFS explores in rings of increasing distance, so the first time it reaches a node is via a shortest path — in edge count. Track distance by level or store it beside each node. Any shortest-path question on an unweighted graph or grid is BFS, full stop.

title: Multi-source BFS
