body: Seed the queue with ALL sources at distance zero and run normal BFS: every node gets its distance to the nearest source in one pass. Rotting oranges, walls and gates, and nearest-exit problems are multi-source BFS wearing costumes.

title: Quiz: Mark on enqueue
body: In BFS, why mark a node visited when it is enqueued rather than when it is popped?
payload: {"options": ["It is faster to write", "It prevents the node being enqueued multiple times", "It changes the traversal order", "It saves memory on the visited set"], "correct": "It prevents the node being enqueued multiple times", "explain": "Two neighbors can both discover an unmarked node before either copy is popped, bloating the queue and double-processing. Marking at enqueue closes that window."}

title: DFS two ways
body: Recursive DFS: visit, mark, recurse on unvisited neighbors — the call stack does the bookkeeping. Iterative DFS swaps BFS's queue for an explicit stack. Recursion is cleaner; mention stack depth if the graph could be huge or degenerate.

title: Pre and post visitation
body: Work done when first entering a node is pre-order; work done after all its descendants finish is post-order. Post-order is where the power is: finish times drive topological sort, and children-first computation drives DP on graphs and trees.

title: BFS or DFS
body: Both traverse everything in O(V + E). BFS when distance or nearest matters. DFS for exhaustive exploration, cycle detection, topological sort, and anything backtracking-shaped. If either works, say so and pick the one you write fastest.

title: Quiz: Traversal cost
body: What is the time complexity of BFS or DFS on an adjacency-list graph with V nodes and E edges?
payload: {"options": ["O(V + E)", "O(V times E)", "O(V squared)", "O(E log V)"], "correct": "O(V + E)", "explain": "Each node is processed once and each edge examined a constant number of times. With a matrix representation it would be O(V squared) — worth mentioning."}

title: Grids are graphs
body: Any grid is an implicit graph: cells are nodes, and the 4-directional neighbors are edges you compute on the fly instead of storing. Every BFS and DFS technique applies verbatim — visited sets, distances, components.

title: State-space graphs
body: Word ladder, lock combinations, and sliding puzzles are graphs where nodes are STATES and edges are legal moves. Shortest transformation means BFS over states. The skill is recognizing an implicit graph in a problem that never says the word graph.

title: Neighbors as a function
body: In implicit graphs, write a neighbors(state) generator: for grids, apply the four direction offsets and bounds-check; for word ladder, mutate each position through the alphabet and keep dictionary hits. Traversal code stays identical.

title: Quiz: Word ladder
body: Finding the shortest transformation sequence between two words, changing one letter at a time through a dictionary, is really what?
payload: {"options": ["Dynamic programming", "BFS on an implicit state graph", "A trie search", "Topological sort"], "correct": "BFS on an implicit state graph", "explain": "Words are nodes, single-letter changes are edges, and shortest sequence means fewest edges — the defining use case for BFS. The graph is never built explicitly."}

title: Number of islands
body: Number of islands: scan the grid; on each unvisited land cell, increment the count and flood-fill its whole island with DFS or BFS so it is never counted again. O(rows times cols) — each cell enters a traversal once.

title: Flood fill and marking in place
body: Flood fill spreads from a seed to connected same-valued cells. Trick of the trade: mark visited by mutating the grid — sink islands by setting land to water — saving a separate visited set. Ask the interviewer if mutation is acceptable first.

title: The grid problem family
body: Rotting oranges is multi-source BFS with time as depth. Walls and gates is multi-source BFS from gates. Surrounded regions flips the trick — flood from the BORDER to find what survives. Pacific-Atlantic floods inward from two oceans. Same engine, different seeds.

title: Quiz: Counting islands
body: In number of islands, what guarantees each island is counted exactly once?
payload: {"options": ["Sorting the grid first", "Flood-filling the entire island when first touched", "Checking all 8 neighbors", "Counting land cells and dividing"], "correct": "Flood-filling the entire island when first touched", "explain": "The fill marks every cell of the island visited, so later scan positions inside it are skipped. Count increments only on first contact with each component."}

title: Connected components
body: Count components in an undirected graph: loop every node, and each time you find an unvisited one, increment the count and traverse its entire component. Same skeleton as islands — the grid version and graph version are one pattern.

title: Labeling components
body: Assign each node a component id during traversal and same-component queries become O(1) lookups afterward. This precompute-then-query structure answers can u reach v many times without re-traversing.

title: Union-Find does it too
body: Union-Find counts components without traversal: start with n components, union each edge, decrement when two roots merge. It is the better fit when edges arrive over time — dynamic connectivity — while DFS suits a static snapshot.

title: Quiz: Component counting
body: Edges arrive one at a time and after each you must report the number of connected components. Best tool?
payload: {"options": ["Rerun DFS each time", "Union-Find", "Topological sort", "Dijkstra"], "correct": "Union-Find", "explain": "Union-Find handles incremental edge additions in near-O(1) amortized per union, decrementing a component counter on merges. Re-running DFS per edge would be O(E times (V+E))."}

title: Cycles in undirected graphs
body: DFS an undirected graph tracking each node's parent: meeting a visited neighbor that is NOT your parent means a cycle. The parent exception matters — the edge you just walked in on is not a cycle. Union-Find alternative: an edge whose endpoints already share a root closes a cycle.

title: Cycles in directed graphs
body: Directed cycles need three colors: white unvisited, gray in the current recursion stack, black finished. Reaching a GRAY node means a back edge — a cycle. Reaching a black node is fine: that is a completed branch, not a loop.

title: Why the difference
body: In directed graphs the parent trick fails because a two-node cycle u to v to u is legal and real. The gray state encodes am I currently on the path from the root — exactly what a directed back edge violates. Explaining WHY earns more than reciting the method.

title: Quiz: Gray means cycle
body: During directed-graph DFS with white, gray, and black states, hitting which state signals a cycle?
payload: {"options": ["White", "Gray", "Black", "Any visited state"], "correct": "Gray", "explain": "Gray nodes are on the current recursion path, so an edge into one loops back onto your own path — a back edge. Black nodes are finished and safe to re-reach."}

title: Kahn's algorithm
body: Topological sort by BFS: compute every node's in-degree, enqueue all zero-in-degree nodes, and repeatedly pop one into the order while decrementing its neighbors' in-degrees — enqueueing any that hit zero. Fewer nodes output than exist means a cycle.

title: DFS topological sort
body: The DFS version: run DFS and push each node onto a stack as it FINISHES; popping the stack yields a topological order. It works because a node finishes only after everything it points to — its finish time proves its prerequisites-completed status.

title: Course schedule family
body: Course schedule I asks can you finish — cycle detection. Course schedule II asks for an order — the topological sort itself. Alien dictionary derives edge constraints from word comparisons first, then toposorts. Prerequisite language in a problem means build a DAG.

title: Quiz: Kahn's cycle check
body: Running Kahn's algorithm, the output order contains fewer nodes than the graph. What does that prove?
payload: {"options": ["The graph is disconnected", "The graph contains a cycle", "The graph is undirected", "Nothing — rerun it"], "correct": "The graph contains a cycle", "explain": "Nodes in a cycle never reach in-degree zero — each waits on another — so they never enqueue. A complete output is exactly the certificate that the graph is a DAG."}

title: Union-Find in four lines
body: Union-Find tracks disjoint sets with a parent array. Find follows parents to the root; union links one root under the other. It answers are these two connected in effectively constant time — the dynamic connectivity workhorse.

title: The two optimizations
body: Path compression points every node on a find path directly at the root; union by rank or size hangs the shorter tree under the taller. Together they make operations amortized inverse-Ackermann — say effectively O(1) and note the function grows slower than log.

title: Union-Find applications
body: Redundant connection (the edge that closes a cycle), accounts merge, number of provinces, and Kruskal's MST all reduce to union and find. Grid variants map cell (r, c) to id r times cols plus c. If a problem merges groups incrementally, this is the tool.

title: Quiz: Path compression
body: What does path compression do during a find operation?
payload: {"options": ["Deletes redundant edges", "Points every traversed node directly at the root", "Sorts the parent array", "Merges two sets"], "correct": "Points every traversed node directly at the root", "explain": "Flattening the chain makes future finds on those nodes one hop. Combined with union by rank, operations become amortized near-constant."}

title: Dijkstra's loop
body: Dijkstra with a min-heap: pop the unsettled node with smallest tentative distance, settle it, and relax its edges — push (newDist, neighbor) whenever a shorter path is found. With lazy deletion of stale entries it runs in O(E log V).

title: Why no negative edges
body: Dijkstra's correctness rests on a settled node never improving — true only when all edges are non-negative, so any detour costs extra. A negative edge could undercut a settled distance later. Bellman-Ford tolerates negatives at O(V times E) by relaxing all edges V minus 1 rounds.

title: 0-1 BFS
body: When every edge weighs zero or one, a deque replaces the heap: push zero-weight discoveries to the FRONT and one-weight to the back. Distances stay sorted in the deque and the run is O(V + E). Awareness level — name it if weights are binary.

title: Quiz: Dijkstra's prerequisite
body: Which condition must edge weights satisfy for Dijkstra's algorithm to be correct?
payload: {"options": ["All weights equal", "Non-negative weights", "Integer weights", "Weights below V"], "correct": "Non-negative weights", "explain": "The greedy settle step assumes no future path can undercut a settled node, which negative edges violate. With negatives, use Bellman-Ford."}

title: Minimum spanning trees
body: An MST connects all nodes with minimum total edge weight and no cycles. Prim's grows one tree greedily with a heap of frontier edges; Kruskal's sorts all edges and adds any that join two different Union-Find components. Awareness level: know both names and mechanics.

title: Kruskal plus Union-Find
body: Kruskal is the cleaner interview implementation: sort edges ascending, iterate, union endpoints, keep edges whose endpoints were in different sets. O(E log E). Min cost to connect all points is this exact algorithm on implicit distance edges.

title: MST vs shortest path
body: An MST minimizes TOTAL connection weight; shortest-path trees minimize distance from a source. They differ — an MST path between two nodes can be longer than their shortest path. Confusing them is a known trap question.

title: Quiz: Kruskal's guard
body: In Kruskal's algorithm, when is a candidate edge rejected?
payload: {"options": ["When it is the heaviest edge", "When its endpoints already share a component", "When it touches a leaf", "When the heap is empty"], "correct": "When its endpoints already share a component", "explain": "Connecting two nodes already in the same Union-Find set would close a cycle. Accepting only cross-component edges keeps the growing forest a tree."}

title: Bipartite checking
body: A graph is bipartite when nodes split into two groups with every edge crossing between them. Test by 2-coloring: BFS or DFS, alternating colors along edges; any edge joining two same-colored nodes kills bipartiteness. Run from every component.

title: Odd cycles are the obstruction
body: A graph is bipartite exactly when it has no odd-length cycle — alternating two colors around an odd loop must collide. That theorem is the one-line justification for why 2-coloring works.

title: Bipartite in disguise
body: Is graph bipartite is the literal version; possible bipartition of people who dislike each other is the same 2-coloring with edges meaning must differ. Any can these be split into two conflict-free groups question should trigger the pattern.

title: Quiz: Bipartite failure
body: During 2-coloring BFS, what observation proves a graph is NOT bipartite?
payload: {"options": ["A node with odd degree", "An edge between two same-colored nodes", "A disconnected component", "A node with no edges"], "correct": "An edge between two same-colored nodes", "explain": "Bipartite means every edge crosses the partition. A monochromatic edge means the alternating assignment failed — equivalently, an odd cycle exists."}

title: Bridges and articulation points
body: A bridge is an edge whose removal disconnects the graph; an articulation point is such a node. Tarjan's DFS finds them by comparing discovery times with low-link values — the earliest node reachable through the subtree. Awareness level for Google.

title: The low-link intuition
body: A child subtree that cannot reach back above its parent — its low-link is not smaller than the parent's discovery time — depends entirely on that connection: cutting it isolates the subtree. That one sentence is the entire intuition to retain.

title: Where it shows up
body: Critical connections in a network — find the cables whose failure splits the network — is the bridges problem verbatim, and a real Google-style question. Recognize it, name Tarjan, state O(V + E), and sketch the low-link idea.

title: Quiz: Bridge definition
body: What makes an edge a bridge in a connected undirected graph?
payload: {"options": ["It carries maximum weight", "Removing it disconnects the graph", "It belongs to a cycle", "It touches the root"], "correct": "Removing it disconnects the graph", "explain": "A bridge is the only path between its two sides — no cycle offers an alternate route. Edges inside cycles are never bridges."}

title: Cloning a graph
body: Clone graph: traverse from the given node with a hash map from original to copy. On visiting a node, create its clone if absent, then wire clone neighbor lists recursively. The map is simultaneously the visited set and the wiring table.

title: Why the map prevents infinite loops
body: Graphs have cycles, so cloning without checking the map would recurse forever. Check the map FIRST — if a clone exists, return it immediately. This is copy list with random pointer generalized from a line to an arbitrary shape.

title: Serializing graphs
body: Graphs serialize as edge lists or adjacency maps with explicit node ids — unlike trees, structure alone does not identify nodes, and cycles forbid naive recursive output. If asked, propose the id-plus-edge-list format and note that assumption.

title: Quiz: Clone graph's map
body: In clone graph, the original-to-copy hash map serves which two roles at once?
payload: {"options": ["Sorting and searching", "Visited tracking and clone lookup", "Weighting and pruning", "Coloring and counting"], "correct": "Visited tracking and clone lookup", "explain": "A key's presence means the node was already cloned (visited), and the value hands you the copy to wire into neighbor lists — one structure, both jobs."}

title: A* in one sentence
body: A* is Dijkstra plus a heuristic: the priority becomes distance so far PLUS an estimate to the goal, steering the search toward the target instead of expanding in all directions. Awareness level — no one codes it in 45 minutes.

title: Admissible heuristics
body: A* stays optimal when its heuristic never OVERESTIMATES the true remaining cost — called admissible. Grid pathfinding uses Manhattan distance for 4-directional movement. Overestimate and A* can return a wrong, non-shortest path.

title: When to mention A*
body: If an interviewer extends a grid shortest-path solution with the goal position is known — can you search smarter, the expected answer is A* with Manhattan distance. One sentence of what and why suffices; then relate it back to your Dijkstra code.

title: Quiz: A* priority
body: What quantity does A* use to order its priority queue?
payload: {"options": ["Distance from the start only", "Estimated distance to the goal only", "Distance so far plus a heuristic estimate to the goal", "Node degree"], "correct": "Distance so far plus a heuristic estimate to the goal", "explain": "The g plus h score biases expansion toward the goal while still accounting for the path already paid — Dijkstra is the special case where h is zero."}

title: LRU as a design question
body: Design an LRU cache is a Google staple: O(1) get and put via a hash map into a doubly linked list ordered by recency — evict from the tail at capacity. Practice writing it end to end in under 15 minutes, with dummy head and tail sentinels.

title: The API contract
body: Nail the semantics before coding: get returns the value and refreshes recency; put inserts or updates, also refreshing recency, then evicts the least recent if over capacity. Half of failed LRU attempts forget that get itself counts as a use.

title: Language shortcuts and honesty
body: Java's LinkedHashMap and Python's OrderedDict implement LRU almost for free — and interviewers know. The safe move: mention the shortcut exists, then offer to build it from primitives. The question is testing the internals, so expect to write them.

title: Quiz: LRU eviction
body: An LRU cache at capacity receives a put for a new key. Which entry is evicted?
payload: {"options": ["The newest entry", "The least recently used entry", "A random entry", "The largest value"], "correct": "The least recently used entry", "explain": "LRU bets that recently touched data will be touched again. The doubly linked list's tail node is by construction the least recently used — remove it and its map key."}

title: LFU in one breath
body: LFU evicts the LEAST FREQUENTLY used key, breaking ties by recency. The O(1) design keeps buckets of keys per frequency count — each bucket an ordered list — plus a pointer to the minimum frequency. Awareness level: know the idea, not every edge case.

title: LFU vs LRU behavior
body: LRU forgets popularity instantly — one burst of new keys evicts an old favorite. LFU protects frequently used keys but is slow to evict formerly-hot items. Naming that behavioral trade-off matters more in interviews than LFU implementation detail.

title: The min-frequency pointer
body: The subtle part of O(1) LFU: track the current minimum frequency. It only ever resets to one on a brand-new insert, or increments when the last key at that level moves up — it never needs a search. That observation is the entire trick.

title: Quiz: LFU tiebreak
body: Two keys tie at the lowest use frequency in an LFU cache. Which is evicted?
payload: {"options": ["The least recently used of the two", "The most recently used", "The one with the larger value", "Both"], "correct": "The least recently used of the two", "explain": "Standard LFU breaks frequency ties by recency — each frequency bucket keeps insertion order so its oldest entry evicts first. LFU degrades to LRU within a bucket."}

title: Bloom filters
body: A Bloom filter answers set membership in tiny space: k hash functions set k bits per inserted item; a query checks those bits. It can say definitely not present or probably present — false positives possible, false negatives impossible.

title: Why the asymmetry
body: A zero bit proves the item was never inserted — insertion would have set it. All-ones can be coincidence from other items' bits, hence false positives. Deletions are unsupported for the same reason: clearing a bit could erase evidence of other items.

title: Where Bloom filters live
body: Real systems use them as cheap gatekeepers: has this URL been crawled, is this key possibly in this database file (LSM trees), is this password in a breach list. In system design answers, a Bloom filter before an expensive lookup is a strong flourish.

title: Quiz: Bloom filter guarantees
body: Which error can a Bloom filter make?
payload: {"options": ["False negatives only", "False positives only", "Both", "Neither"], "correct": "False positives only", "explain": "Other items' hashes can coincidentally set all of a query's bits — a false positive. But an inserted item's bits are never cleared, so a negative answer is always certain."}

title: Skip lists
body: A skip list is a sorted linked list with probabilistic express lanes: each node is promoted to higher levels by coin flips, letting searches skip long stretches. Expected O(log n) search, insert, and delete — a randomized alternative to balanced trees.

title: Why they exist
body: Skip lists match red-black tree performance with far simpler code — no rotations — and lock more gracefully under concurrency. Redis sorted sets famously use one. The concept-level takeaway: probabilistic layering can substitute for careful balancing.

title: Search path intuition
body: Searching starts at the top-left, moving right until the next node overshoots, then dropping a level — like an express train downgrading to local as it nears the stop. Halving expectations per level yields the O(log n) bound.

title: Quiz: Skip list levels
body: What determines how many levels a skip list node occupies?
payload: {"options": ["Its key value", "Random coin flips at insertion", "Its insertion order", "The list's current height"], "correct": "Random coin flips at insertion", "explain": "Each node promotes to the next level with probability one half, repeatedly. The randomness maintains balanced express lanes in expectation with zero rebalancing logic."}

title: Interval trees and friends
body: An interval tree stores intervals for O(log n plus k) queries like which intervals overlap this point. Awareness level: know it exists so my calendar could use an interval tree lands correctly in design discussions — coding it is out of scope.

title: Sweep line as the substitute
body: Most interview interval problems skip fancy trees: convert intervals to plus-one start and minus-one end EVENTS, sort them, and sweep while tracking a running count. Max concurrent meetings is the running count's peak.

title: Calendar booking ladder
body: My Calendar I books if no overlap — a sorted map of start times works. Calendar II allows double but not triple booking. Calendar III asks the max overlap — the sweep count in a sorted map of deltas. The ladder is a favorite escalation sequence.

title: Quiz: Max concurrent intervals
body: Using the sweep line technique, how do you compute the maximum number of overlapping intervals?
payload: {"options": ["Sort by end time and count", "Track a running sum over sorted plus-one and minus-one events, taking its peak", "Merge all intervals first", "Binary search each interval"], "correct": "Track a running sum over sorted plus-one and minus-one events, taking its peak", "explain": "Each start adds one active interval and each end removes one; sorting the events and scanning makes the running sum the live overlap count, and its maximum is the answer."}

title: O(1) insert, delete, getRandom
body: RandomizedSet pairs a dynamic array with a hash map of value to index. Insert appends and records the index; getRandom picks a random array slot; delete swaps the target with the LAST element, updates the swapped element's map entry, and pops.

title: The swap-with-last trick
body: Arrays delete from the middle in O(n) but from the END in O(1). Swapping the victim to the back converts any deletion into a tail pop — order is sacrificed, which this structure never promised. That reframe is the entire question.

title: Why both structures
body: The map alone cannot do getRandom — no O(1) way to pick a uniformly random key. The array alone cannot do O(1) delete by value — no way to find it. Each covers the other's weakness; explaining that pairing logic IS the interview answer.

title: Quiz: O(1) delete from array
body: How does RandomizedSet delete an arbitrary value from its array in O(1)?
payload: {"options": ["Shifts all later elements left", "Swaps it with the last element and pops", "Marks it as a tombstone", "Rebuilds the array"], "correct": "Swaps it with the last element and pops", "explain": "The hash map locates the value's index instantly; swapping with the tail makes removal a pop. Just remember to update the moved element's index in the map."}

title: Design-a-structure questions
body: Design a hit counter, a time-based key-value store, a snapshot array: these test composing primitives — maps, lists, heaps, binary search — under stated complexity targets. Start from the required operations and their target costs, then pick parts.

title: Time-based key-value store
body: TimeMap stores per key a list of (timestamp, value) pairs appended in increasing time order — so get(key, t) is a binary search for the rightmost timestamp at most t. Sorted-by-construction plus binary search is the recurring recipe.

title: Hit counter trade-offs
body: A 5-minute hit counter: a queue of timestamps popped when stale is simple but memory scales with hits; a 300-slot circular buffer of per-second counts caps memory and trades exactness granularity. Presenting both with the trade-off is the complete answer.

title: Quiz: TimeMap retrieval
body: In a time-based key-value store, get(key, timestamp) returns the value at the largest time at most the query. Which technique finds it?
payload: {"options": ["Hash lookup by exact timestamp", "Binary search over that key's time-ordered list", "A max-heap of timestamps", "Linear scan from the front"], "correct": "Binary search over that key's time-ordered list", "explain": "Writes arrive in increasing time order, so each key's list is sorted for free — a rightmost-at-most binary search answers get in O(log n)."}

title: The simple three
body: Bubble sort swaps adjacent inversions repeatedly; selection sort pulls the minimum into place each round; insertion sort slides each element back into the sorted prefix. All O(n squared) — you know them to discuss, almost never to use.

title: Insertion sort's niche
body: Insertion sort earns its keep on nearly-sorted or tiny inputs: it runs in O(n + inversions), degrading gracefully. That is why production sorts like Timsort hand small runs to insertion sort — a great trivia beat when discussing built-ins.

title: Know the properties table
body: For each simple sort, know three attributes: complexity, stability, and in-place. Bubble and insertion are stable and in-place; selection is in-place but NOT stable in its common form. These attribute questions are cheap interview points.

title: Quiz: Nearly sorted input
body: Which O(n squared) sort runs in nearly linear time when the input is almost sorted?
payload: {"options": ["Selection sort", "Insertion sort", "Bubble sort with no optimization", "None of them"], "correct": "Insertion sort", "explain": "Insertion sort's work is proportional to the number of inversions, so an almost-sorted array needs only a few short slides — O(n + inversions)."}

title: Merge sort mechanics
body: Merge sort splits the array in half, sorts each recursively, and merges the two sorted halves with a two-pointer walk. Guaranteed O(n log n) in every case, at the price of an O(n) merge buffer.

title: Merge sort is stable
body: Merging takes from the LEFT half on ties, so equal elements keep their original order — merge sort is stable. It is the go-to answer when a problem needs guaranteed O(n log n) plus stability, or when sorting linked lists.

title: External sorting
body: Data too big for memory sorts in chunks: sort what fits, write sorted runs to disk, then k-way merge the runs with a heap. Merge sort's sequential access pattern is exactly why it scales beyond RAM — a nice systems-flavored aside.

title: Quiz: Merge sort worst case
body: What is merge sort's worst-case time complexity?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(n log n)", "O(n squared)", "It depends on the pivot"], "correct": "O(n log n)", "explain": "The split is always exactly in half regardless of data, so the recursion tree has log n levels of O(n) merging — no bad inputs exist. Pivots are quicksort's problem."}

title: Quicksort mechanics
body: Quicksort picks a pivot, partitions the array so smaller elements precede it and larger follow, then recurses on both sides. Average O(n log n), in-place, cache-friendly — the reason it is the practical default despite a worse worst case.

title: The O(n squared) trap
body: Quicksort degrades to O(n squared) when partitions are lopsided — classically, a first-element pivot on already-sorted input. Random or median-of-three pivot selection makes the bad case astronomically unlikely. Always volunteer this caveat.

title: Lomuto vs Hoare
body: Lomuto partitioning walks one pointer, swapping small elements forward — simpler to write. Hoare converges two pointers from both ends — fewer swaps, slightly trickier boundaries. For interviews, master one cold and name the other.

title: Quiz: Quicksort's bad day
body: What input triggers naive quicksort's O(n squared) behavior with a first-element pivot?
payload: {"options": ["Random data", "Already-sorted data", "All-negative numbers", "Data with many duplicates only"], "correct": "Already-sorted data", "explain": "The first element is always the minimum, so every partition is 0 versus n-1 — a degenerate recursion of depth n. Random pivots fix it."}

title: Quickselect recap
body: Quickselect is quicksort that recurses into only the side containing the target index: average O(n) for the kth smallest or largest. After it runs, the kth element is in its final sorted position — a fact some problems exploit directly.

title: Partial ordering for free
body: Quickselect leaves everything before position k less than or equal to it and everything after greater or equal. K closest points to origin uses exactly this: quickselect on distance, then return the first k — no full sort needed.

title: Stating the trade-off
body: The kth-element decision in one sentence: heap gives O(n log k) guaranteed and streams; quickselect gives O(n) average, in place, but mutates the array and has a theoretical O(n squared) tail. Interviewers want the sentence, then your pick.

title: Quiz: After quickselect
body: After running quickselect for index k, what is guaranteed about the array?
payload: {"options": ["It is fully sorted", "The element at k is in its sorted position, smaller-or-equal values before it", "Nothing changed", "Only the first k elements are sorted"], "correct": "The element at k is in its sorted position, smaller-or-equal values before it", "explain": "Partitioning places the kth element exactly where full sorting would, partitioning the rest around it without ordering them internally."}

title: Heapsort
body: Heapsort builds a max-heap in O(n), then repeatedly swaps the root to the array's end and sifts down over the shrunken heap: O(n log n) guaranteed, in place, O(1) auxiliary. Its weakness is cache-hostile jumps and no stability.

title: The sort-selection triangle
body: The three O(n log n) sorts each win one axis: merge sort is stable with guaranteed bounds but O(n) space; quicksort is fastest in practice but has a worst case; heapsort guarantees bounds in O(1) space but is slow in practice and unstable.

title: Heapsort in interviews
body: Heapsort is rarely coded in interviews but often discussed: it proves O(n log n) sorting with O(1) extra space exists, and it anchors the which sort would you use conversation. Know the two-phase mechanics and its property profile.

title: Quiz: In-place guarantee
body: Which sort achieves guaranteed O(n log n) time with O(1) auxiliary space?
payload: {"options": ["Merge sort", "Quicksort", "Heapsort", "Counting sort"], "correct": "Heapsort", "explain": "Merge sort needs an O(n) buffer, quicksort's guarantee is only average-case. Heapsort heapifies in place and extracts in place — the trade-off is practical speed and stability."}

title: Counting sort
body: Counting sort tallies occurrences of each value in a range k, then rebuilds the array from the counts: O(n + k) time — no comparisons, hence not bound by the O(n log n) comparison floor. Practical when k is modest, like grades, ages, or letters.

title: Radix sort
body: Radix sort applies a stable counting sort per digit, least significant first: O(d times (n + k)) for d digits. Stability between passes is what preserves earlier digits' work — remove it and the algorithm silently breaks.

title: Bucket sort
body: Bucket sort scatters values into ranges, sorts each small bucket, and concatenates — O(n) expected on uniformly distributed data. Sort colors and top-k-frequent both have bucket-flavored O(n) solutions worth mentioning after the standard one.

title: Quiz: Beating n log n
body: How do counting, radix, and bucket sort beat the O(n log n) lower bound?
payload: {"options": ["Better pivots", "They do not compare elements", "Parallel hardware", "They only sort integers under 100"], "correct": "They do not compare elements", "explain": "The n log n floor applies only to comparison-based sorting. These algorithms exploit value structure — counting occurrences or digits — sidestepping the bound entirely."}

title: Stability defined
body: A stable sort preserves the original relative order of equal elements. Sorting people by age stably keeps alphabetical order within each age — if the input was alphabetical. Merge and insertion sort are stable; plain quicksort, heapsort, and selection are not.

title: When stability matters
body: Stability enables multi-key sorting by chaining passes: sort by the secondary key first, then STABLY by the primary — ties settle by the earlier pass. It also matters whenever input order is meaningful, like preserving arrival order among equal priorities.

title: The interview probe
body: Is your sort stable, and does it matter here is a standard follow-up. Know your language: Java sorts objects stably (Timsort) but primitives unstably (dual-pivot quicksort); Python's sort is always stable. Concrete language facts land well.

title: Quiz: Multi-key sorting
body: To sort records by department, and by name within each department, using a stable sort you should...
payload: {"options": ["Sort by department, then by name", "Sort by name, then by department", "Sort once by department only", "Use an unstable sort twice"], "correct": "Sort by name, then by department", "explain": "The stable second pass preserves the name order established by the first pass among records tying on department — secondary key first is the rule."}

title: Timsort awareness
body: Python's sort and Java's object sort use Timsort: a merge-insertion hybrid that detects existing sorted runs, achieving O(n) on sorted data and O(n log n) worst case, stable. Saying the built-in sort is Timsort, which is stable is effortless credibility.

title: Custom comparators
body: Real problems sort by custom logic: intervals by start, strings by length, pairs by second element descending. Know your syntax cold — a Python key lambda or Java Comparator.comparing — because fumbling comparator syntax burns visible minutes.

title: Largest number's comparator
body: Arrange numbers to form the largest value: compare string concatenations — a before b when a+b beats b+a. It is the canonical the comparator IS the problem question, and it needs a transitivity sanity-check sentence when you present it.

title: Quiz: Language sort facts
body: What algorithm powers Python's built-in list.sort?
payload: {"options": ["Quicksort", "Timsort", "Heapsort", "Radix sort"], "correct": "Timsort", "explain": "Timsort — a stable merge-insertion hybrid that exploits pre-sorted runs, giving O(n) best case on ordered data. Java uses it for objects too."}

title: Sort first, then think
body: Sorting as preprocessing unlocks whole strategies: two pointers need order, intervals merge only after sorting by start, greedy pairing wants extremes adjacent. When stuck, ask what becomes easy if this were sorted — often the O(n log n) cost is the whole solution.

title: The interval opener
body: Nearly every interval problem opens the same way: sort by start time, then sweep once comparing each interval to the merged frontier. Merge intervals, insert interval, and non-overlapping intervals differ only in what the sweep does.

title: Greedy setups
body: Greedy algorithms usually need sorted input to make locally optimal choices meaningful: assign cookies sorts both sides, boats to save people pairs lightest with heaviest, activity selection sorts by end time. Sorting is the greedy enabler, not an afterthought.

title: Quiz: Why sort intervals
body: Why do interval problems almost always begin by sorting intervals by start time?
payload: {"options": ["It reduces memory", "Overlaps become adjacent, enabling a single linear sweep", "It removes duplicates", "End times stop mattering"], "correct": "Overlaps become adjacent, enabling a single linear sweep", "explain": "After sorting by start, any interval overlapping yours cannot appear later than the first non-overlapping one — so one pass with a running merged interval suffices."}

title: Cycle sort ideas
body: When an array holds values 1 to n, each value has a home index — value minus one. Cycle sort swaps each element toward home until everything settles, in O(n) total swaps. This index-as-address view drives a family of clever in-place problems.

title: Index as hash
body: Find the missing or duplicate number in O(n) time and O(1) space by using indices as hash slots: mark presence by negating the value at index abs(v) minus one. A second pass reads the signs. The array becomes its own hash set.

title: First missing positive
body: First missing positive is the boss fight: cycle-sort every value v in range onto index v minus one, then scan for the first index holding the wrong value. O(n) time, O(1) space — exactly what the problem demands and why it is rated hard.

title: Quiz: O(1) space duplicate
body: An array contains n+1 numbers from 1 to n with one duplicate. Which technique finds it in O(n) time and O(1) space without modifying the array?
payload: {"options": ["Sorting", "A hash set", "Floyd's cycle detection on index jumps", "Binary search on values only"], "correct": "Floyd's cycle detection on index jumps", "explain": "Treat each value as a pointer to an index: the duplicate creates a cycle, and the tortoise-hare entry-finding routine locates it — linked list cycle II in disguise."}

title: Binary search skeleton
body: Binary search maintains an invariant over lo and hi: while lo is at most hi, probe mid, and discard the half that cannot contain the answer. O(log n), and compute mid as lo plus (hi minus lo) over 2 to dodge overflow in fixed-width languages.

title: Off-by-one discipline
body: Binary search bugs live in three choices: loop condition (lo <= hi versus lo < hi), boundary updates (mid plus 1 versus mid), and what lo means at exit. Fix a convention, state the invariant out loud, and reuse it every time — do not improvise under pressure.

title: Test the two-element case
body: The fastest binary search sanity check: trace an array of exactly two elements. Infinite loops and boundary bugs almost always reveal themselves there, because mid equals lo and a lazy update fails to shrink the range.

title: Quiz: Safe midpoint
body: Which midpoint computation avoids integer overflow in fixed-width languages?
payload: {"options": ["(lo + hi) / 2", "lo + (hi - lo) / 2", "hi - lo / 2", "(lo * hi) / 2"], "correct": "lo + (hi - lo) / 2", "explain": "lo + hi can exceed the integer maximum when both are large; the subtraction form stays in range. A famous real bug in Java's own library binary search."}

title: Leftmost and rightmost
body: Finding the FIRST or LAST occurrence of a duplicate value means not stopping at a match: record it and keep searching the corresponding half. These are lower_bound and upper_bound — the primitives behind count occurrences and search range.

title: The boundary template
body: Cleanest formulation: binary search for the boundary between condition-false and condition-true territory. Lower bound is the first index where value at index is at least the target. Framing every variant as find the first true unifies the whole family.

title: Built-in bounds
body: Know your library: Python's bisect_left and bisect_right, C++ lower_bound and upper_bound, Java's binarySearch with its insertion-point encoding. Using bisect confidently — and knowing left versus right semantics — reads as real experience.

title: Quiz: Counting duplicates
body: How do you count occurrences of a value in a sorted array in O(log n)?
payload: {"options": ["Linear scan", "upper_bound minus lower_bound", "One standard binary search", "A hash map"], "correct": "upper_bound minus lower_bound", "explain": "Lower bound finds the first occurrence index, upper bound the first index past the last occurrence — their difference is the count, in two O(log n) searches."}

title: Rotated array search
body: A rotated sorted array has one sorted half at every midpoint. Determine which half is sorted by comparing endpoints, check whether the target lies inside that half's range, and recurse accordingly. Still O(log n) — the invariant just needs one more case.

title: Finding the rotation point
body: The minimum of a rotated sorted array: compare mid against HI — if the mid value is greater, the pivot is right of mid; otherwise it is at mid or left. Comparing against lo instead breaks on already-sorted input; that detail is the question's teeth.

title: Duplicates break the guarantee
body: With duplicates, values at lo, mid, and hi can all be equal, making both halves indistinguishable — shrink both ends by one and continue. Worst case degrades to O(n), and stating that degradation unprompted is the point of the variant.

title: Quiz: Rotated with duplicates
body: Searching a ROTATED sorted array that may contain duplicates, what is the worst-case complexity?
payload: {"options": ["O(log n)", "O(n)", "O(n log n)", "O(1)"], "correct": "O(n)", "explain": "When lo, mid, and hi hold equal values, neither half can be ruled out — you can only shrink boundaries one step, degrading to linear in the worst case like an array of all-equal values."}

title: Fully sorted matrix
body: A matrix whose rows read as one continuous sorted list is a disguised sorted array: binary search indices 0 to rows times cols minus 1, mapping each mid to (mid / cols, mid % cols). One search, O(log(m n)).

title: Row and column sorted
body: When rows and columns are each sorted but not globally, use the staircase: start at the top-right; a larger value moves left, a smaller moves down. Each step eliminates a row or column: O(m + n). Distinguish which matrix variant you are given FIRST.

title: Reading the matrix problem
body: Search a 2D matrix I is the flattened O(log mn) version; search a 2D matrix II is the staircase O(m + n) version. Applying the wrong technique to the wrong variant is the intended trap — clarify the sorting guarantee before choosing.

title: Quiz: The staircase start
body: In a matrix with sorted rows and sorted columns, where does the O(m+n) elimination search start?
payload: {"options": ["Top-left corner", "Top-right corner", "The center", "Any random cell"], "correct": "Top-right corner", "explain": "From the top-right, everything left is smaller and everything down is larger — so each comparison eliminates a full row or column. Top-left offers no such split (bottom-left works symmetrically)."}

title: Binary search the answer
body: When the answer itself lies in a numeric range and feasibility is MONOTONIC — if capacity c works, every larger capacity works — binary search the answer space: guess a value, test feasibility in O(n), and halve. Recognizing this reframe is a Google favorite.

title: The feasibility check
body: The pattern splits into two functions: canDo(x), a greedy O(n) simulation, and the binary search over x calling it. Koko eating bananas checks can she finish at speed s; split array largest sum checks can we split with no part exceeding s.

title: Setting the bounds
body: Bounds come from the problem's physics: minimum ship capacity is the largest single package (it must fit) and maximum is the total sum (one trip). Justifying lo and hi from first principles, not guessing 0 and infinity, shows command of the technique.

title: Quiz: When answer-space search applies
body: What property must the feasibility function have for binary search on the answer to work?
payload: {"options": ["Linearity", "Monotonicity — feasible past some threshold, infeasible before", "Symmetry", "Convexity"], "correct": "Monotonicity — feasible past some threshold, infeasible before", "explain": "Binary search discards half the space per probe, which is only valid if feasibility flips exactly once along the axis. Verify and STATE the monotonicity before searching."}

title: Peak finding
body: Find a peak element in O(log n): probe mid and climb the rising side — if the next value is larger, a peak lies right; otherwise a peak lies at mid or left. It works because following an uphill slope must eventually summit inside the array boundary.

title: Why any peak suffices
body: The problem asks for ANY peak, and the boundary convention treats outside-the-array as negative infinity, so an ascending direction guarantees a peak that way. Binary search here does not need sortedness — only a guarantee of where an answer must exist.

title: The lesson underneath
body: Peak finding teaches the deeper truth: binary search needs a predicate that reliably tells you which half CONTAINS an answer — sorted order is just the most common source of that predicate. Say that sentence and follow-ups get easier.

title: Quiz: Peak logic
body: In peak finding, the value at mid+1 is greater than at mid. Where must a peak exist?
payload: {"options": ["Left of mid", "Right of mid", "At mid", "Cannot be determined"], "correct": "Right of mid", "explain": "Climbing rightward, values must eventually stop rising — at the array edge if nowhere sooner, since beyond the boundary counts as negative infinity. That descent point is a peak."}

title: Unknown-size arrays
body: Searching a sorted array of unknown length: galloping — probe indices 1, 2, 4, 8 until the value overshoots the target or the reader errors, then binary search the last bracket. Total O(log n) even without knowing n.

title: Exponential bracketing
body: The doubling phase costs O(log n) probes and establishes bounds where the answer must live — the same idea that grows dynamic arrays. Interfaces returning a sentinel for out-of-bounds reads (like LeetCode's ArrayReader) are the setup cue.

title: Infinite streams framing
body: This pattern appears in system-flavored wrappers: find a record in a sorted log of unknown length, or first bad version where n is astronomically large. Whenever the domain is unbounded but sorted, bracket first, then binary search.

title: Quiz: Bracketing cost
body: Searching a sorted array of unknown size by doubling the probe index, what is the total complexity?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(log n)", "O(n log n)", "O(sqrt n)"], "correct": "O(log n)", "explain": "Doubling reaches past the target position in O(log n) probes, and the binary search inside the final bracket adds another O(log n) — logarithmic overall."}

title: Ternary search
body: Ternary search finds the extremum of a UNIMODAL function — strictly rising then falling — by probing two interior points and discarding the third that cannot contain the peak. O(log n). Awareness level: recognize unimodal optimization when it appears.

title: Binary search does it too
body: On integer domains, binary search on the slope replaces ternary search: compare f(mid) with f(mid+1) and walk uphill — exactly the peak-finding code. Ternary search matters mostly for continuous domains; on arrays the peak template suffices.

title: Spotting unimodality
body: Phrases like costs decrease then increase or maximize a rise-then-fall quantity signal a unimodal objective. State the unimodality assumption explicitly — searching a non-unimodal function with these tools silently returns garbage.

title: Quiz: Ternary's requirement
body: Ternary search correctly finds a maximum only when the function is what?
payload: {"options": ["Sorted", "Unimodal", "Periodic", "Linear"], "correct": "Unimodal", "explain": "One rise, one fall, one peak — that shape lets two probes discard a third of the domain safely. Multiple local maxima break the discard logic entirely."}

title: Opposite-end pointers
body: Two pointers starting at both ends and converging solve sorted-pair problems in O(n): two sum on a sorted array moves left on a small sum and right on a large one, each step provably discarding candidates that cannot work.

title: Container with most water
body: Container with most water converges from the ends, always advancing the SHORTER wall: the width shrinks every step, so only a taller limiting wall could improve area. That exchange argument — why moving the shorter side is safe — is the whole interview.

title: Trapping rain water
body: Trapping rain water's two-pointer form tracks leftMax and rightMax, processing whichever side has the smaller max: that side's water level is already determined by its own max. It reduces an apparently-DP problem to O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Quiz: Why move the shorter wall
body: In container with most water, why is it always safe to move the shorter wall's pointer inward?
payload: {"options": ["It is faster to compute", "Keeping the shorter wall caps area while width only shrinks", "The taller wall might fall", "It avoids duplicates"], "correct": "Keeping the shorter wall caps area while width only shrinks", "explain": "Area is width times the SHORTER height. Fixing the short wall and shrinking width can never improve, so all those configurations are safely discarded at once."}

title: Reader and writer pointers
body: Same-direction two pointers split roles: a reader scans every element while a writer marks where the next kept element lands. Remove duplicates from sorted array and move zeroes are both write-pointer problems — O(n), in place, order preserved.

title: The invariant sentence
body: State the writer's invariant and the code writes itself: everything before the write pointer is the finished, filtered output. Every read step either copies forward and advances both pointers, or skips and advances only the reader.

title: Partitioning in place
body: Dutch national flag — sort colors — runs three pointers: low, mid, high. Swap zeroes down, twos up (without advancing mid, since the swapped-in value is unexamined), and ones stay. One pass, O(1) space, and a very common Google warm-up.

title: Quiz: Dutch flag subtlety
body: In sort colors, after swapping the mid element with the HIGH pointer, why does mid not advance?
payload: {"options": ["It always advances", "The value swapped in from high is unexamined", "High might be negative", "To keep the loop O(n)"], "correct": "The value swapped in from high is unexamined", "explain": "The element arriving from the high side could be a 0, 1, or 2 — it must be classified before moving on. Swaps with LOW are safe to advance past because that region is already classified."}

title: Fast and slow beyond lists
body: Floyd's cycle detection works on any function iterated repeatedly, not just linked lists: happy number cycles through digit-square sums, and find the duplicate number treats array values as next-pointers. Any repeated state means a cycle the tortoise can catch.

title: Happy number
body: Happy number: repeatedly replace n with the sum of squared digits; happy numbers reach 1, unhappy ones loop. Detect the loop with fast and slow pointers in O(1) space — or a seen-set in O(k) space. Offering both shows range.

title: Duplicate as a cycle
body: Find the duplicate number forbids modifying the array and extra space — so view index-to-value as a linked structure: the duplicate value is pointed to twice, creating a cycle whose ENTRY is the answer. Run cycle-entry finding on index jumps.

title: Quiz: Functional cycles
body: Why does Floyd's tortoise-and-hare apply to problems like happy number?
payload: {"options": ["They use linked lists internally", "Iterating any function over a finite space must eventually cycle", "Digits are pointers", "It does not apply"], "correct": "Iterating any function over a finite space must eventually cycle", "explain": "A deterministic next-state function on finitely many states must revisit one, creating a cycle — and Floyd's detection only needs a next function, not actual list nodes."}

title: Fixed windows
body: A fixed-size window of k elements slides by adding the entering element and removing the leaving one — O(1) per step instead of recomputing O(k). Maximum average subarray is the canonical warm-up; the update-not-recompute habit is the lesson.

title: Windows with structure
body: When the window must answer more than a sum — say, its maximum — pair it with the right structure: a monotonic deque for max, a frequency map for composition checks like find all anagrams. Window mechanics stay identical; the payload changes.

title: Off-by-one hygiene
body: Fixed-window bugs concentrate at the seams: start shrinking or emitting answers only once the window reaches size k, and remember the element leaving is at index i minus k. Trace one example with k equals 2 before declaring victory.

title: Quiz: Sliding efficiency
body: Sliding a fixed window of size k one position costs how much for a running sum?
payload: {"options": ["O(1)", "O(k)", "O(log k)", "O(n)"], "correct": "O(1)", "explain": "Add the entering element, subtract the leaving one. Recomputing the whole window would make the scan O(nk) — the entire point of sliding is avoiding that."}

title: Variable windows
body: Variable-size windows grow the right edge every step and shrink the left only while a constraint is violated: while invalid, advance left. Both pointers move at most n times, so despite the nested look it is O(n).

title: Longest substring family
body: Longest substring without repeating characters grows right, and on a repeat, jumps left past the previous occurrence (tracked in a last-seen map). The family — at most k distinct characters, replace k letters — differs only in the validity condition.

title: Minimum window substring
body: Minimum window substring inverts the objective: grow right until the window COVERS all required characters, then shrink left as far as coverage survives, recording the best. A needed-count integer alongside the frequency map keeps coverage checks O(1).

title: Quiz: Why still O(n)
body: A variable sliding window has a while loop inside its for loop. Why is the total still O(n)?
payload: {"options": ["The while loop rarely runs", "Each pointer only ever moves forward, at most n steps total", "The compiler optimizes it", "It is actually O(n squared)"], "correct": "Each pointer only ever moves forward, at most n steps total", "explain": "Left never retreats, so all its advances across the whole run sum to at most n — amortized analysis over the scan, same argument as the monotonic stack."}

title: Window plus frequency map
body: The heavy-duty combo: a frequency map records what the window holds, and a counter of satisfied requirements tells validity in O(1). Permutation in string keeps a fixed window and compares counts; character replacement tracks the max single-letter frequency.

title: The matches counter
body: Comparing two 26-entry maps per step costs O(26); maintaining a count of how many characters currently satisfy their requirement makes each step O(1). Increment or decrement the matches count exactly when a character crosses its required threshold.

title: Character replacement insight
body: Longest repeating character replacement keeps the window valid when window length minus the count of its most frequent character is at most k. The subtle accepted trick: the max frequency never needs to decrease when shrinking — a stale max only makes the check conservative.

title: Quiz: Anagram windows
body: Find all anagrams of p in s is best solved with which combination?
payload: {"options": ["Sorting every substring", "A fixed-size window with a frequency map", "Dynamic programming", "A trie of all substrings"], "correct": "A fixed-size window with a frequency map", "explain": "Slide a window of length p over s, updating character counts incrementally and comparing to p's counts — O(n) total versus O(n times p log p) for sorting each substring."}

title: Parallel pointers on two arrays
body: Merging two sorted arrays walks one pointer per array, emitting the smaller head — the merge step of merge sort, and the skeleton for intersection of two sorted arrays and merge sorted array from the back.

title: Merging from the back
body: Merge sorted array in place (nums1 has trailing space) fills from the END: compare the largest remaining elements and write backward. Writing forward would overwrite unread values — the backward insight is the entire problem.

title: Intersection choices
body: Intersection of two arrays: hash sets in O(n + m), or if both are sorted, two pointers in O(n + m) with O(1) extra space, or if one is tiny, binary search each of its elements. Choosing by the input's properties is the actual test.

title: Quiz: In-place merge direction
body: Merging sorted nums2 into nums1's trailing empty space, why fill from the back?
payload: {"options": ["It is faster asymptotically", "Forward writing would overwrite nums1 values not yet compared", "Backward loops are simpler", "nums2 is reversed"], "correct": "Forward writing would overwrite nums1 values not yet compared", "explain": "The free space sits at the end, so the largest elements can be placed there immediately. Writing forward destroys unmerged nums1 data; writing backward never collides."}

title: 3Sum
body: 3Sum: sort, then for each anchor element run opposite-end two pointers on the remainder seeking the negated anchor. O(n squared) with O(1) extra space — better than hashing every pair — and sorting also enables clean duplicate skipping.

title: Deduplication discipline
body: 3Sum's real difficulty is emitting unique triples: skip repeated anchor values, and after recording a hit, advance both pointers past their duplicates. Most failed attempts die on duplicate handling, not the core algorithm.

title: kSum generalizes
body: kSum recurses: fix one element and solve (k-1)Sum on the rest, bottoming out at two-pointer 2Sum. Complexity O(n to the k minus 1). 3Sum closest swaps exact matching for tracking the nearest sum — same skeleton, softer target.

title: Quiz: 3Sum complexity
body: What is the standard time complexity of 3Sum via sorting plus two pointers?
payload: {"options": ["O(n log n)", "O(n squared)", "O(n cubed)", "O(n squared log n)"], "correct": "O(n squared)", "explain": "Sorting is O(n log n), then each of n anchors runs an O(n) two-pointer sweep — the sweep dominates at O(n squared), well below the brute-force O(n cubed)."}

title: Recursion's two parts
body: Every recursion needs a base case that answers trivially and a recursive case that shrinks toward it. Missing or unreachable base cases mean stack overflow — check the base case FIRST in the function body, before any recursive work.

title: Trust the recursion
body: The mental model that unlocks recursion: assume the recursive call already works for smaller input, and only design one layer — how to combine its answer. Tracing every nested call by hand is how people get lost; the leap of faith is the technique.

title: Call stack behavior
body: Each call pushes a frame holding its own parameters and locals; frames pop as calls return. Depth equals maximum nesting — O(n) for a linear recursion, O(log n) for halving. Python's default limit near 1000 frames is worth mentioning for deep inputs.

title: Quiz: The first line
body: Where should the base case check appear in a recursive function?
payload: {"options": ["After the recursive calls", "At the top, before recursive work", "In a separate function", "It is optional"], "correct": "At the top, before recursive work", "explain": "Guarding first ensures every path toward smaller input terminates before attempting further descent — putting it later risks recursing past the boundary and overflowing."}

title: Recursion vs iteration
body: Anything recursive can be written iteratively with an explicit stack, and vice versa. Recursion buys clarity for tree-shaped problems; iteration buys control over memory and immunity from stack limits. Complexity class is identical either way.

title: When to convert
body: Convert to iteration when depth could hit language limits — a linked list of a million nodes recursed in Python dies. Simple linear recursions convert to loops directly; tree recursions convert by simulating the frames with a stack of pending states.

title: Saying it in the room
body: A strong interview line: I will write this recursively for clarity; if depth is a concern it converts to an explicit stack. It shows you know the trade-off and made a deliberate choice — and it preempts the what about stack overflow follow-up.

title: Quiz: Deep list recursion
body: Why might recursively processing a million-node linked list fail even though the algorithm is correct?
payload: {"options": ["Integer overflow", "Call stack depth limits", "Garbage collection", "Cache misses"], "correct": "Call stack depth limits", "explain": "A million stacked frames exceeds default runtime limits — Python caps near 1000. The fix is converting to iteration, since the logic itself is fine."}

title: Tail recursion
body: A tail call is a recursive call in return position with nothing left to do afterward. Languages with tail-call optimization reuse the frame, making such recursion O(1) stack — effectively a loop written recursively.

title: Who optimizes tail calls
body: The catch: Python and Java do NOT optimize tail calls; Scheme and some functional languages guarantee it, and C compilers often do it as an optimization. Claiming tail recursion saves stack in Python is a small but real credibility ding.

title: Accumulator style
body: Tail form usually means threading an accumulator parameter: factorial(n, acc) returns factorial(n-1, n times acc) with the answer carried forward rather than combined on the way back. The rewrite is mechanical and mirrors the loop version exactly.

title: Quiz: Tail call reality
body: Does writing tail-recursive Python reduce stack usage?
payload: {"options": ["Yes, Python optimizes tail calls", "No, Python performs no tail-call optimization", "Only with decorators", "Only in Python 3"], "correct": "No, Python performs no tail-call optimization", "explain": "CPython deliberately keeps full frames for debuggability, so tail form still consumes one frame per call. Convert to a loop for real O(1) stack."}

title: Subsets by include-exclude
body: Power set generation: at each element, branch on include it or exclude it — a binary decision tree with 2 to the n leaves, each a subset. The recursion carries the current path and the next index; emit the path at index n.

title: Subsets iteratively
body: The iterative build: start with the empty set, and for each element, append a copy of every existing subset with the element added. Also 2 to the n — and the bitmask version maps each number 0 to 2 to the n minus 1 to a subset via its bits.

title: Subsets with duplicates
body: Subsets II sorts first, then skips a duplicate element at the same decision level: if it equals its predecessor and the predecessor was not taken at this level, skip. Level-wise duplicate skipping is the reusable trick across the whole backtracking family.

title: Quiz: Power set size
body: How many subsets does a set of n distinct elements have?
payload: {"options": ["n squared", "n factorial", "2 to the n", "n(n+1)/2"], "correct": "2 to the n", "explain": "Each element is independently in or out — two choices apiece, multiplied n times. This count is also why subset enumeration only suits small n."}

title: Permutations by swapping or marking
body: Permutations: either swap each remaining element into the current position and recurse, or keep a used-boolean array and build the path element by element. n factorial outputs, each costing O(n) to copy — state the O(n times n factorial) honestly.

title: Duplicate permutations
body: Permutations II sorts, then at each position skips an element equal to its predecessor when that predecessor is currently UNUSED — meaning both would start identical branches at the same level. That one condition removes all duplicate outputs without a set.

title: Permutations vs subsets shape
body: Subsets recurse with a start index and never look back — order does not matter. Permutations restart the scan every level with a used-set — order matters. Diagnosing which shape a problem wants is half of solving any backtracking question.

title: Quiz: Output counts
body: How many permutations does an array of n distinct elements have?
payload: {"options": ["2 to the n", "n factorial", "n squared", "n to the n"], "correct": "n factorial", "explain": "n choices for the first slot, n-1 for the next, and so on — the factorial count that limits permutation enumeration to roughly n of 10 to 12."}

title: Combinations
body: Choose k of n: recurse with a start index so each element only combines with LATER ones, preventing reordered duplicates. Emit when the path reaches size k. The start-index discipline is what separates combinations from permutations.

title: Combination sum variants
body: Combination sum I allows reuse: recurse with the SAME index after taking an element. Combination sum II forbids reuse and has duplicates: advance the index and add level-wise duplicate skipping. Reuse-versus-advance is a one-character difference doing all the work.

title: Early termination on sorted input
body: Sort candidates first, and the moment a candidate exceeds the remaining target, BREAK the loop — everything after is larger. That single prune converts many time-limit failures into passes and is the first optimization to mention.

title: Quiz: Reuse in combination sum
body: In combination sum where each number may be reused, what index does the recursive call pass after choosing candidate i?
payload: {"options": ["i, the same index", "i + 1", "0", "A random index"], "correct": "i, the same index", "explain": "Passing i allows choosing candidate i again for unlimited reuse, while still forbidding earlier candidates — which prevents duplicate combinations in different orders."}

title: N-Queens
body: N-Queens places one queen per row, recursing row by row and checking column and both diagonal conflicts. Track occupied columns and diagonals in sets — same-diagonal cells share row minus col, anti-diagonals share row plus col — for O(1) validity checks.

title: Sudoku solver
body: Sudoku backtracking: find the next empty cell, try digits 1 through 9 that pass row, column, and 3x3 box checks, recurse, and un-assign on failure. Precomputing three sets of used digits per row, column, and box keeps each check O(1).

title: The constraint problem template
body: Both are the same skeleton: choose, check constraints, recurse, un-choose. The un-choose — restoring every mutated structure on the way out — is where correctness lives. Forgetting to remove from ONE of the tracking sets is the classic N-Queens bug.

title: Quiz: Diagonal identity
body: In N-Queens, two squares lie on the same top-left-to-bottom-right diagonal exactly when they share what value?
payload: {"options": ["row + col", "row - col", "row * col", "max(row, col)"], "correct": "row - col", "explain": "Moving down-right increments both coordinates, keeping their difference constant. Anti-diagonals conserve row + col — track both sets for O(1) conflict checks."}

title: Word search
body: Word search DFS: from each cell matching the word's first letter, explore the four neighbors for the next letter, marking the current cell visited — mutate it to a sentinel — and RESTORING it on return. The restore is the backtracking; omit it and correct paths get blocked.

title: Backtracking vs plain DFS
body: Plain graph DFS marks visited permanently — each node processed once. Backtracking search un-marks on return because different PATHS may reuse a cell. Knowing which regime a problem needs, permanent or path-local marking, prevents both infinite loops and false negatives.

title: Grid backtracking complexity
body: Word search costs O(cells times 4 to the L) for word length L — each cell starts a search branching up to four ways per letter (three excluding where you came from). Quote it as exponential in word length, and mention the trie upgrade for multi-word search.

title: Quiz: The restore step
body: In word search, why must a cell be un-marked after the recursive exploration returns?
payload: {"options": ["To save memory", "Other paths may legally pass through that cell", "The grid is read-only", "To avoid integer overflow"], "correct": "Other paths may legally pass through that cell", "explain": "Visited status in backtracking is per-path, not global. A cell blocked by one failed path might be essential to a different successful path starting elsewhere."}

title: Palindrome partitioning
body: Palindrome partitioning: at each position, try every prefix that is a palindrome, recurse on the remainder, and backtrack. Precomputing a palindrome table — or memoizing checks — trims the repeated substring testing.

title: Restore IP addresses
body: Restore IP addresses places three dots: at each step try segments of length 1 to 3, rejecting values over 255 and leading zeros (a lone 0 is fine, 01 is not). Exactly four segments consuming the whole string — tight constraints make heavy pruning natural.

title: The partitioning shape
body: Partitioning problems choose where to CUT rather than what to take: the choice at each level is the length of the next piece. Recognizing cut-position choice versus element choice tells you what the recursion's loop iterates over.

title: Quiz: IP segment rules
body: In restore IP addresses, which candidate segment is invalid?
payload: {"options": ["0", "25", "255", "01"], "correct": "01", "explain": "Leading zeros are forbidden except for the single digit 0 itself. Values must also stay at or below 255 — both checks prune the search hard."}

title: Prune early, prune often
body: Pruning cuts branches that provably cannot succeed: remaining target below zero, not enough characters left, partial cost already exceeding the best found. Each check runs before recursing — the earlier a doomed branch dies, the more of its subtree you never pay for.

title: Branch ordering
body: Trying likelier or more-constrained options first shrinks search in practice: Sudoku solvers pick the cell with fewest candidates, and sorted candidates enable break-on-overshoot. Ordering does not change worst-case bounds but transforms real running time.

title: Feasibility pre-checks
body: Some pruning happens before recursion even starts: partition to k equal subsets first checks the total divides by k and the largest element fits. A one-line arithmetic check can eliminate the entire search — always look for one.

title: Quiz: Prune placement
body: Where should a can-this-branch-possibly-succeed check execute?
payload: {"options": ["After the recursion returns", "Before making the recursive call", "Only at the base case", "In a separate validation pass"], "correct": "Before making the recursive call", "explain": "Checking before descent means the doomed subtree is never entered at all. Checking after returns still pays the subtree's full exponential cost."}

title: Generate parentheses
body: Generate parentheses tracks open and close counts: add an open bracket while opens remain under n, add a close while closes trail opens. The two conditions ARE the validity proof — no post-hoc checking, every generated string is valid by construction.

title: Constructive validity
body: That build-only-valid-strings idea generalizes: constrain choices so invalid states are unreachable, rather than generating everything and filtering. It converts generate-and-test exponential waste into exactly the outputs — the mark of a strong backtracking design.

title: Catalan numbers cameo
body: Valid parentheses strings of n pairs number the nth Catalan number — roughly 4 to the n over n to the 1.5 — which also counts BSTs on n nodes and valid stack sequences. Naming Catalan when asked how many outputs is a nice flourish.

title: Quiz: Close-bracket condition
body: In generate parentheses, when may a closing bracket be appended?
payload: {"options": ["Whenever fewer than n exist", "When the count of closes is less than the count of opens", "Only at the end", "After every open bracket"], "correct": "When the count of closes is less than the count of opens", "explain": "A close must match an earlier unmatched open. Enforcing closes strictly behind opens guarantees every completed string is balanced — validity by construction."}

title: When DP applies
body: Dynamic programming needs two ingredients: overlapping subproblems — the same smaller question recurs — and optimal substructure — the best answer composes from best sub-answers. Fibonacci has both; finding the longest simple path in a graph lacks the second.

title: DP smell tests
body: Phrases that smell like DP: count the ways, minimum cost to reach, longest or shortest subsequence, can it be partitioned. If brute force is exponential and its recursion tree repeats states, memoization is usually the intended step up.

title: DP vs divide and conquer
body: Merge sort splits into DISJOINT subproblems — no overlap, no caching needed. DP subproblems overlap, so caching converts exponential trees into polynomial tables. The presence of overlap is the entire distinction; say it if asked how DP differs.

title: Quiz: The two ingredients
body: Which pair of properties makes a problem suitable for dynamic programming?
payload: {"options": ["Sorted input and unique elements", "Overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure", "Small input and integer values", "Greedy choice and feasibility"], "correct": "Overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure", "explain": "Overlap makes caching profitable; optimal substructure makes composing cached optima correct. Missing either, DP gives no benefit or wrong answers."}

title: Memoization
body: Top-down memoization is the brute-force recursion plus a cache checked on entry and written before return. Each distinct state computes once, so complexity becomes states times transition cost. It is the fastest path from a working brute force to a passing solution.

title: Tabulation
body: Bottom-up tabulation fills a table from base cases toward the answer with explicit loops. No recursion overhead or stack risk, and it exposes the dependency order — which is what enables the rolling-array space optimizations later.

title: The reliable interview route
body: The dependable sequence: write brute-force recursion, name the repeated states, add a memo, then — if asked — convert to tabulation. Narrating those steps shows the derivation rather than a memorized table, which is exactly what Google interviewers probe for.

title: Quiz: Memoized complexity
body: After memoizing a recursion, what determines its time complexity?
payload: {"options": ["The original tree size", "Number of distinct states times work per state", "Always O(n)", "The cache's memory size"], "correct": "Number of distinct states times work per state", "explain": "Each state computes once and is served from cache afterward, so total work is states multiplied by per-state transition cost — the universal DP counting formula."}

title: Defining the state
body: A DP solution is four decisions: what the state means (in words — dp of i is the best answer using the first i items), the transition equation, the base cases, and the iteration order. Writing the state definition as a comment before coding prevents most DP bugs.

title: Transitions come from choices
body: The transition enumerates the LAST choice: climb stairs — the final step was 1 or 2; coin change — the last coin was one of the denominations. Ask what was the final decision, and the recurrence writes itself as a max, min, or sum over those options.

title: Iteration order follows dependencies
body: Tabulation must compute dependencies before dependents: 1D usually left to right, grids row by row, intervals by increasing LENGTH. Wrong order reads uninitialized entries and fails quietly — when a DP gives nonsense, check order first.

title: Quiz: First thing to write
body: What should you articulate FIRST when constructing a DP solution?
payload: {"options": ["The space optimization", "A precise sentence defining what dp[i] means", "The final loop", "Test cases"], "correct": "A precise sentence defining what dp[i] means", "explain": "Transitions, base cases, and ordering all derive from the state's meaning. An imprecise state definition is the root cause of most broken recurrences."}

title: Climbing stairs and friends
body: Climbing stairs is Fibonacci: ways to reach step i equals ways to i-1 plus ways to i-2. Min cost climbing stairs swaps sum for min-plus-cost. These trivial-looking 1D problems are the template every harder linear DP elaborates.

title: House robber
body: House robber: dp of i is the max loot through house i — either skip it (dp of i-1) or rob it (dp of i-2 plus its value). The circular version runs the linear solve twice, excluding the first house or the last, and takes the better.

title: Decode ways and word break
body: Decode ways counts step sizes of one or two digits with validity checks — zeroes are the trap. Word break asks whether some prefix cut leaves a dictionary word ending at i: dp of i is true if any dp of j holds with the j-to-i slice in the dictionary.

title: Quiz: House robber recurrence
body: In house robber, what is the transition for dp[i], the best loot considering the first i houses?
payload: {"options": ["dp[i-1] + value[i]", "max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + value[i])", "dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]", "min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2])"], "correct": "max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + value[i])", "explain": "The final choice at house i is binary: skip it and keep dp[i-1], or rob it — which forbids house i-1 — earning dp[i-2] plus its value. Take the max."}

title: Kadane's algorithm
body: Maximum subarray in O(n): carry a running best-sum-ending-here; at each element, either extend the previous run or start fresh — whichever is larger — and track the global max. Kadane is DP with the table compressed to one variable.

title: The extend-or-restart insight
