body: Kadane's decision is local: a negative running sum can only drag down whatever follows, so discard it and restart. The state dp of i, best subarray ENDING at i, is the classic example of adding an anchor to make substructure work.

title: Maximum product subarray
body: The product variant must track BOTH the max and min product ending here, because a negative element flips them — the smallest product times a negative becomes the largest. Two running values, same single pass, and the twist interviews are checking for.

title: Quiz: Why track the minimum
body: In maximum product subarray, why maintain the minimum product ending at each position?
payload: {"options": ["To handle zeroes", "A negative number turns the minimum into the new maximum", "To save memory", "It is unnecessary"], "correct": "A negative number turns the minimum into the new maximum", "explain": "Multiplying a large negative product by another negative yields a large positive — so the running min is a future max candidate and must travel alongside."}

title: Unique paths
body: Unique paths on a grid moving only right or down: paths to a cell equal paths from above plus paths from the left, with the first row and column as base cases of one. Obstacles just zero out blocked cells before the sum.

title: Min path sum
body: Minimum path sum swaps addition of counts for min of costs: cell cost plus the smaller of the top and left answers. Nearly all easy grid DP is this one recurrence with the operator swapped — sum for counting, min or max for optimizing.

title: Grid DP space
body: Each grid row depends only on the previous row, so a single 1D array updated in place suffices: O(cols) space instead of O(rows times cols). This two-line change is the most commonly requested space optimization in the follow-up.

title: Quiz: Grid recurrence
body: In unique paths (moves: right and down only), dp[r][c] equals what?
payload: {"options": ["dp[r-1][c] + dp[r][c-1]", "dp[r-1][c-1] + 1", "max of top and left", "dp[r][c-1] * 2"], "correct": "dp[r-1][c] + dp[r][c-1]", "explain": "Every path arrives from directly above or directly left, and those path sets are disjoint — so counts add. Switching + for min turns it into min path sum."}

title: 0/1 knapsack
body: 0/1 knapsack: for each item, take it or leave it — dp of capacity c equals max of skipping, or the item's value plus dp of c minus its weight using earlier items only. States are item-count times capacity: O(n W), pseudo-polynomial.

title: Coin change, both flavors
body: Coin change I (fewest coins) is UNBOUNDED knapsack — coins reuse freely. Coin change II (count the ways) must iterate COINS in the outer loop so each combination counts once regardless of order. Outer-loop order deciding combinations versus permutations is the famous subtlety.

title: Subset sum and partition
body: Partition equal subset sum reduces to subset-sum for half the total: a boolean dp over achievable sums, iterating capacity DOWNWARD in the 1D form so each item is used at most once. Downward-versus-upward capacity loops encode 0/1 versus unbounded.

title: Quiz: The loop direction
body: In 1D 0/1 knapsack, why must the capacity loop run from high to low?
payload: {"options": ["It is faster", "Upward iteration would let the same item be used twice", "To handle negative weights", "Direction does not matter"], "correct": "Upward iteration would let the same item be used twice", "explain": "Going upward, dp[c - w] may already include the current item this round — reusing it. Downward reads only last round's values, enforcing take-at-most-once. Upward is exactly how unbounded knapsack is written on purpose."}

title: LIS two ways
body: Longest increasing subsequence: O(n squared) DP — dp of i is the best LIS ending at i, scanning all earlier smaller elements — or O(n log n) patience sorting: keep an array of the smallest possible tail per length and binary-search each element's slot.

title: LCS
body: Longest common subsequence is the canonical 2D sequence DP: matching characters extend the diagonal answer by one; otherwise take the max of dropping a character from either string. Edit distance and shortest common supersequence are its immediate relatives.

title: Edit distance
body: Edit distance: equal characters cost nothing and move diagonally; otherwise one plus the min of insert (left), delete (up), and replace (diagonal). Being able to say which neighbor corresponds to which edit operation is the depth check interviewers apply.

title: Quiz: Fast LIS
body: What technique achieves O(n log n) for longest increasing subsequence?
payload: {"options": ["Memoized recursion", "Binary search over an array of minimal tails per length", "A max-heap", "Sorting the array first"], "correct": "Binary search over an array of minimal tails per length", "explain": "Maintain tails[k], the smallest possible tail of an increasing subsequence of length k+1; each element binary-searches its position. The array's LENGTH is the LIS — its contents are not the subsequence itself."}

title: Palindromic substrings
body: Count or find palindromic substrings by expanding around all 2n minus 1 centers in O(n squared) — usually simpler and faster in practice than the equivalent interval DP table. Longest palindromic SUBSEQUENCE, however, is genuine 2D DP over the string and its reverse-relation.

title: Regex and wildcard matching
body: Regex matching DP: dp of i, j means the first i characters match the first j pattern tokens. Stars branch — match zero of the preceding element or one more. It is a stretch question; deriving the star cases carefully matters more than speed.

title: String DP orientation
body: Most string DP states are prefix-based: answers over the first i characters of one or two strings. When a problem compares two strings in any way — matching, distance, interleaving — the default state is the 2D prefix pair.

title: Quiz: Substring vs subsequence palindromes
body: Which pairing of technique to problem is correct?
payload: {"options": ["Expand around center for palindromic substrings; 2D DP for palindromic subsequences", "2D DP for substrings; expand-around-center for subsequences", "Both need tries", "Both are O(n)"], "correct": "Expand around center for palindromic substrings; 2D DP for palindromic subsequences", "explain": "Substrings are contiguous, so growing outward from each center enumerates them all. Subsequences can skip characters — centers do not exist, so an interval DP over (i, j) is required."}

title: Interval DP
body: Interval DP states span ranges: dp of i, j is the best over the subarray from i to j, computed for increasing lengths, with a transition choosing a split or a LAST element. Burst balloons picks the last balloon popped — the reversal that makes subproblems independent.

title: Why last, not first
body: Choosing the FIRST balloon popped leaves neighbors that merge across the gap — subproblems entangle. Choosing the LAST leaves two fully independent sides with known boundaries. When interval subproblems entangle, invert the decision order — the transferable trick.

title: Interval DP calibration
body: Interval DP is awareness-tier for intern and new-grad loops: recognize the shape (matrix chain multiplication, burst balloons, merging stones), state the O(n cubed) cost, and sketch the length-ordered iteration. Full derivations under pressure are not the bar.

title: Quiz: Burst balloons pivot
body: In burst balloons, the interval transition fixes which decision to decouple the subproblems?
payload: {"options": ["The first balloon popped", "The last balloon popped in the interval", "The largest balloon", "The middle index"], "correct": "The last balloon popped in the interval", "explain": "If balloon k pops last, both sides burst entirely with k still standing as their boundary — making left and right independent. Fixing the first pop entangles the sides."}

title: DP on trees
body: Tree DP returns per-node summaries combined postorder. House robber III: each node returns (best if robbed, best if not) — rob the node and children must be skipped; skip it and take each child's better option. Two numbers per node, one traversal.

title: States as tuples
body: The general recipe: identify what a parent needs from each child and return exactly that tuple. Diameter needs height; robber needs the robbed/not pair; camera placement needs covered-state. Designing the returned tuple IS designing the DP.

title: No memo needed on trees
body: Tree DP usually skips the cache: each node is visited once, so there are no overlapping subproblems in a plain traversal — it is optimal substructure alone. Saying this is DP-style reasoning even without memoization shows conceptual precision.

title: Quiz: Robber III return value
body: In house robber III, what does each node's recursion return?
payload: {"options": ["A single best value", "A pair: best if this node is robbed, best if it is not", "The subtree sum", "The node's depth"], "correct": "A pair: best if this node is robbed, best if it is not", "explain": "The parent's legality depends on whether the child was robbed, so both scenarios must travel upward. Rob the parent: sum children's not-robbed values. Skip it: sum each child's max."}

title: Bitmask DP
body: Bitmask DP encodes a SET as an integer's bits — state dp of mask covers subsets of up to about 20 elements, 2 to the 20 being a million. Traveling salesman and assignment problems use dp of (mask, last). Awareness tier: recognize n at most 20 as the signal.

title: Bitmask mechanics
body: The idioms: check membership with mask AND (1 shifted left i); add with OR; iterate submasks with sub equals (sub minus 1) AND mask. Complexity is typically O(2 to the n times n) — quote it and note why n must stay tiny.

title: Where it appears
body: Partition to k equal subsets, shortest superstring, and minimum incompatibility are bitmask DP territory. In an intern loop you would likelier solve the first with pruned backtracking — mentioning the bitmask alternative is the differentiator, not the requirement.

title: Quiz: The bitmask signal
body: Which constraint most strongly hints a bitmask DP is intended?
payload: {"options": ["n up to 10 to the 5", "n at most 20", "Values are negative", "The array is sorted"], "correct": "n at most 20", "explain": "2 to the 20 is about a million states — feasible; 2 to the 30 is not. Tiny n plus subset-flavored decisions is the classic bitmask fingerprint."}

title: State machine DP
body: Buy and sell stock problems are state machines: on each day you occupy a state — holding, not holding, cooling down — and transitions carry prices. dp of (day, state) with a handful of states collapses the entire family into one pattern.

title: The stock family ladder
body: One transaction: track min price and best profit. Unlimited: sum every positive day-to-day rise. With cooldown or fee: add a state or subtract the fee on sell. At most k transactions: dp of (day, transactions, holding). Same machine, more registers.

title: Drawing the machine
body: Under pressure, DRAW the states and arrows before coding: hold to sold, rest to hold, sold to cooldown. The picture makes transitions and base cases mechanical and gives the interviewer a shared artifact to discuss — a huge communication win.

title: Quiz: Cooldown states
body: Best time to buy and sell stock with cooldown needs which state set per day?
payload: {"options": ["Holding only", "Holding, not holding and free to buy, cooling down", "Bought and sold", "One state per price"], "correct": "Holding, not holding and free to buy, cooling down", "explain": "Selling forces a rest day, so freshly-sold must be distinguishable from free-to-buy. Three states with price-weighted transitions capture the rule exactly."}

title: Rolling arrays
body: When dp row i reads only row i minus 1, keep two rows — or one, updated carefully — cutting space from O(n m) to O(m). The correctness question is always the same: does the update read values from the correct generation.

title: One-row updates and direction
body: Collapsing to a single row couples with iteration direction: left-to-right makes leftward reads CURRENT-generation and upward reads previous — exactly right for LCS-style recurrences needs care, and for knapsack the downward capacity trick applies. Always re-verify direction after collapsing.

title: When to optimize
body: Space optimization is a FOLLOW-UP, not an opener: get the 2D version correct first, then compress when asked. Optimizing prematurely and corrupting generations is a self-inflicted wound interviewers see weekly. Correct, then compact.

title: Quiz: Two-row reduction
body: A DP where row i depends only on row i-1 can reduce space to what?
payload: {"options": ["O(1) always", "O(columns)", "O(rows)", "No reduction possible"], "correct": "O(columns)", "explain": "Keep the previous row and the row being built — or one row updated in the safe direction. The full grid of rows never needs to coexist."}

title: Reconstructing solutions
body: Recovering the actual answer — the path, the subsequence, the item set — either stores a parent pointer per state during the fill, or walks the finished table BACKWARD re-deriving which choice produced each value. Backward walking costs no extra memory.

title: Backtracking the table
body: Edit distance operations reconstruct by standing at (m, n) and asking which neighbor plus which operation yields this cell's value, stepping there, and repeating to the origin. The recorded operations reverse into the edit script.

title: Expect the follow-up
body: Now print the actual subsequence is a standard escalation after any optimize-the-value DP. Mentioning during the initial solve that the table supports reconstruction — before being asked — signals you have seen the whole arc of the question.

title: Quiz: Recovering the path
body: How do you reconstruct the optimal solution from a completed DP table without extra bookkeeping?
payload: {"options": ["Rerun the whole DP", "Trace backward from the answer, re-deriving each winning choice", "Sort the table", "It cannot be done without parent pointers"], "correct": "Trace backward from the answer, re-deriving each winning choice", "explain": "At each state, test which predecessor-plus-transition reproduces its value and step there. Parent pointers trade memory for a simpler walk — both are acceptable answers."}

title: The greedy-choice property
body: Greedy works when a locally optimal choice is always part of SOME globally optimal solution — the greedy-choice property. When it holds, no backtracking or table is needed: commit and move on. When it silently fails, greedy returns confident wrong answers.

title: Exchange arguments
body: The standard correctness sketch: take any optimal solution that disagrees with the greedy choice, swap in the greedy choice, and show the result is no worse. Interviewers rarely demand full proofs — but one exchange-argument sentence separates justified greed from hopeful greed.

title: When greedy fails
body: Coin change with denominations 1, 3, 4 for target 6: greedy takes 4+1+1, optimal is 3+3. Greedy fails when early choices constrain later ones invisibly — which is exactly what DP's exhaustive table handles. Keep a failure example in your pocket.

title: Quiz: Greedy coin failure
body: With coins 1, 3, and 4, why does greedy fail to make 6 optimally?
payload: {"options": ["It cannot make 6 at all", "Taking 4 first forces 4+1+1 while 3+3 uses fewer coins", "Greedy needs sorted coins", "6 requires DP by definition"], "correct": "Taking 4 first forces 4+1+1 while 3+3 uses fewer coins", "explain": "The largest-coin-first choice is locally appealing but excludes the two-coin solution. Arbitrary denominations break the greedy-choice property; canonical systems like US coins happen to satisfy it."}

title: Merge intervals
body: Merge intervals: sort by start, then sweep with a current merged interval — overlap extends its end with a max; a gap emits it and starts anew. O(n log n) for the sort, and the template underneath half of all interval questions.

title: Non-overlapping intervals
body: Minimum removals to make intervals non-overlapping: sort by END time and greedily keep every interval that starts at or after the last kept end. Earliest-finish-first is the provably optimal activity-selection rule — end-time sorting is the tell.

title: Meeting rooms I and II
body: Meeting rooms I — can one person attend all — is a sort-by-start plus adjacent-overlap check. Meeting rooms II — minimum rooms — needs the min-heap of end times or the sweep-line count. The pair is a classic escalation; prep them together.

title: Quiz: Activity selection
body: To keep the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals, sort by what and keep greedily?
payload: {"options": ["Start time", "End time", "Duration", "Midpoint"], "correct": "End time", "explain": "Finishing earliest leaves maximal room for the future — the exchange argument: any optimal solution's first interval can be swapped for the earliest-ending one without loss."}

title: Sweep line
body: Sweep line converts intervals into events: plus one at each start, minus one at each end, sorted by coordinate. Scanning while summing gives the live overlap count at every moment — its peak answers minimum rooms and max concurrent events.

title: The tie-breaking detail
body: When a start and an end share a coordinate, sort order encodes the semantics: process ends first if touching intervals do not conflict (a meeting ending at 10 frees the room for one starting at 10). Stating the tie rule is the difference between right and off-by-one.

title: Sweeping in two dimensions
body: Harder sweeps move a line across the plane: the skyline problem sweeps building edges with a height multiset, and car pooling sweeps passenger counts along the route. Any capacity over time question is a sweep in disguise.

title: Quiz: Sweep events
body: In the sweep line technique, an interval (start, end) contributes which two events?
payload: {"options": ["(start, +1) and (end, -1)", "(start, -1) and (end, +1)", "Two +1 events", "One event at the midpoint"], "correct": "(start, +1) and (end, -1)", "explain": "Entering raises the active count and leaving lowers it; sorting all events and scanning makes the running sum the exact concurrency at every point."}

title: Jump game
body: Jump game — can you reach the end — greedily tracks the farthest reachable index while scanning: standing past it means stuck, return false. One pass, O(1) space, and no DP table required despite the problem's DP smell.

title: Jump game II
body: Minimum jumps uses implicit BFS levels: track the current jump's boundary and the farthest reach within it; hitting the boundary increments jumps and extends to that farthest reach. Each level is the set of indices reachable in k jumps.

title: Reach problems generalize
body: Video stitching and minimum taps to water a garden are jump game re-skinned: intervals become jumps from their start to their end, and the greedy farthest-reach level scan applies verbatim. Spot the reduction and say it — reductions impress.

title: Quiz: Jump game check
body: Scanning left to right in jump game, what condition proves the end is unreachable?
payload: {"options": ["A zero appears anywhere", "The current index exceeds the farthest reachable index", "The array is unsorted", "The last value is zero"], "correct": "The current index exceeds the farthest reachable index", "explain": "Reach is the running max of i plus nums[i]. Standing beyond it means no prior position could jump here — zeroes are only fatal when they cap reach below your position."}

title: Gas station
body: Gas station: if total gas covers total cost, a solution EXISTS and is unique. Scan tracking a running tank; whenever it dips negative, no start in the exhausted stretch works — restart the candidate at the next station with an empty tank.

title: Why skipping the stretch is safe
body: If starting at A fails by station B, any start between A and B also fails: it enters B with no more fuel than A's attempt did, since it skipped A's surplus. That one argument justifies the O(n) single pass over the obvious O(n squared).

title: Circular feasibility pattern
body: The shape recurs: a circular arrangement, a running balance, and a claim that one reset-scan finds the unique valid start. Related trick: any circular array problem may also yield to doubling the array or modular indexing — mention the toolbox.

title: Quiz: Gas station restart
body: During the gas station scan, the running tank goes negative at station B. Where does the candidate start move?
payload: {"options": ["Back to station 0", "To B + 1", "To the station with most gas", "The problem is infeasible"], "correct": "To B + 1", "explain": "Every start in the failed stretch inherits the same or worse fuel arriving at B, so all are eliminated at once. If total gas is at least total cost, the surviving candidate is the answer."}

title: Task scheduler
body: Task scheduler with cooldown n: the most frequent task dictates the frame — (maxCount minus 1) times (n plus 1) slots plus the tasks tying that max. The answer is the larger of that formula and the plain task count when idles are unnecessary.

title: Formula or simulation
body: Two accepted solves: the frame formula in O(n), or simulation with a max-heap of counts plus a cooldown queue re-admitting tasks. The simulation generalizes to variants the formula cannot handle — offering both, with when each applies, is the strong answer.

title: Why the most frequent leads
body: Spacing constraint problems are dominated by the most frequent element: it needs the most separations, so everything else packs into its gaps. That dominance intuition transfers directly to reorganize string and distant barcodes.

title: Quiz: Scheduler bottleneck
body: In task scheduler with cooldowns, which quantity determines the minimum schedule length?
payload: {"options": ["The rarest task's count", "The most frequent task's count", "The alphabet size", "The order tasks were given"], "correct": "The most frequent task's count", "explain": "The max-frequency task forces the widest spacing skeleton; other tasks fill its idle gaps. When tasks outnumber the frame, no idles are needed and the count itself is the answer."}

title: Sort, then pair greedily
body: Assign cookies sorts children and cookies, matching the smallest sufficient cookie to each child. Boats to save people sorts weights and pairs lightest with heaviest via two pointers. Sorting exposes the extremes that greedy pairing rules operate on.

title: Partition labels
body: Partition labels: record each character's LAST index, then sweep extending the current partition's end to the max last-index seen; reaching that end closes a partition. Greedy on precomputed reach — no sorting, one elegant pass.

title: The two-pointer pairing proof
body: Why lightest-with-heaviest is optimal for boats: if the heaviest cannot share with the lightest, it can share with no one — send it alone. Otherwise pairing them wastes nothing. Each case is one sentence; together they are the whole proof.

title: Quiz: Boat pairing
body: In boats to save people (two per boat, weight limit), after sorting, who pairs with the heaviest person?
payload: {"options": ["The second heaviest", "The lightest, if they fit together", "Nobody, ever", "A random partner"], "correct": "The lightest, if they fit together", "explain": "If even the lightest cannot join the heaviest, the heaviest goes alone. If they fit, no better use of the lightest exists — the exchange argument in miniature."}

title: Reorganize string
body: Reorganize string so no neighbors match: repeatedly pop the two most frequent characters from a max-heap, append both, decrement, and re-push — taking two at once prevents immediate repeats. Infeasible when one character exceeds half the length, rounded up.

title: Furthest building
body: Furthest building with ladders and bricks: greedily spend LADDERS on every climb, but keep the climbs in a min-heap — when ladders run out, retroactively convert the SMALLEST climb to bricks. Greedy with regret via a heap: commit cheaply, revise optimally.

title: Heap-powered greed
body: The pattern: greedy needs the current best or worst candidate at every step under changing data, and the heap serves it in O(log n). IPO (pick the most profitable affordable project) and minimum cost to connect ropes follow the same engine.

title: Quiz: Greedy with regret
body: In furthest building, what does the min-heap of past climbs enable?
payload: {"options": ["Sorting the buildings", "Retroactively swapping the smallest ladder-climb to bricks when ladders run out", "Skipping buildings", "Counting total height"], "correct": "Retroactively swapping the smallest ladder-climb to bricks when ladders run out", "explain": "Ladders should ultimately cover the LARGEST climbs. Committing them greedily but keeping climbs in a heap lets the algorithm undo its cheapest commitment — greedy plus regret equals optimal here."}

title: Greedy or DP
body: The decision test: can you argue an exchange — swapping any optimal solution's choice for the greedy one without loss? Yes: greedy, O(n log n) or better. No, or unsure with counterexamples looming: DP explores what greedy would forfeit.

title: Try greedy first, carefully
body: In interviews, propose greedy first when it is plausible — it is simpler and faster — but immediately probe it with an adversarial example. Greedy that survives your own attack, stated aloud, reads as rigor; greedy asserted without testing reads as luck.

title: The telltale structures
body: Greedy problems tend to involve sortable one-dimensional structure — intervals, deadlines, magnitudes — where earliest or largest has provable meaning. Problems with interacting choices across the whole input (knapsack values, arbitrary coins) usually demand DP.

title: Quiz: Choosing the paradigm
body: Which question best distinguishes whether greedy suffices or DP is required?
payload: {"options": ["Is the input large", "Can an exchange argument show the local choice is never regretted", "Are the numbers integers", "Is recursion allowed"], "correct": "Can an exchange argument show the local choice is never regretted", "explain": "Greedy is DP minus the exploration — legitimate only when the greedy choice provably belongs to some optimal solution. If a swap can lose value, the table must consider both branches."}

title: Binary and two's complement
body: Integers store as binary place values; negatives use two's complement — flip every bit and add one. The scheme makes addition hardware identical for both signs and gives one zero, with the top bit signaling negativity.

title: Reading two's complement
body: In 8 bits, negative one is all ones (11111111) and the minimum is 10000000, negative 128 — one more negative value than positive because zero occupies the positive side. That asymmetry is why negating the minimum integer overflows.

title: Language integer models
body: Java ints are fixed 32-bit two's complement; Python integers grow arbitrarily large, so bit tricks assuming a 32-bit wraparound need explicit masking with 0xFFFFFFFF. Knowing your language's model prevents silent correctness bugs in bit problems.

title: Quiz: All ones
body: In 8-bit two's complement, the pattern 11111111 represents which value?
payload: {"options": ["255", "-1", "-127", "128"], "correct": "-1", "explain": "Adding one to all-ones carries out to zero, and the number one below zero is negative one. Interpreted UNSIGNED the same bits mean 255 — interpretation depends on the type."}

title: The six operators
body: AND keeps bits set in both; OR keeps bits set in either; XOR keeps bits that differ; NOT flips everything. Left shift by k multiplies by 2 to the k; right shift divides. These six operators are the entire vocabulary of bit manipulation.

title: Arithmetic vs logical shift
body: Right shifts split by sign handling: arithmetic shift copies the sign bit in (Java's single arrow), logical shift fills with zeroes (Java's triple arrow). Python's right shift is arithmetic on its unbounded ints. Mention which you mean when shifting negatives.

title: Masks
body: A mask is a bit pattern selecting positions of interest: AND with a mask reads them, OR sets them, AND with the mask's complement clears them. Building the mask — usually one shifted left by i — is step one of every bit recipe.

title: Quiz: XOR truth
body: What does XOR return for two equal bits?
payload: {"options": ["1", "0", "The bit itself", "Undefined"], "correct": "0", "explain": "XOR is one only where inputs DIFFER. Equal inputs — both zero or both one — give zero, which is exactly why x XOR x is always zero."}

title: The four bit recipes
body: Check bit i: n AND (1 shifted left i). Set it: OR the mask. Clear it: AND the mask's complement. Toggle it: XOR the mask. These four one-liners should be reflex — they are the assembly language of every harder bit problem.

title: n AND n minus 1
body: Subtracting one flips the lowest set bit to zero and everything below it to ones — so n AND (n minus 1) REMOVES the lowest set bit. It drives Kernighan's bit counting and the classic power-of-two test.

title: n AND negative n
body: Two's complement makes negative n equal NOT n plus one, so n AND negative n ISOLATES the lowest set bit — everything else cancels. This isolation trick powers Fenwick tree index arithmetic and lowest-bit parity tests.

title: Quiz: Removing the lowest bit
body: What does the expression n AND (n minus 1) compute?
payload: {"options": ["n divided by 2", "n with its lowest set bit removed", "The lowest set bit alone", "The highest set bit"], "correct": "n with its lowest set bit removed", "explain": "Subtracting one turns the trailing 100...0 into 011...1; ANDing wipes that whole run. Isolating the lowest bit alone is the sibling trick n AND negative n."}

title: XOR's algebra
body: XOR is commutative and associative, anything XOR itself is zero, and anything XOR zero is itself. Consequence: XOR of a whole list cancels every value appearing twice, leaving only the odd one out — the single number solution in one pass, O(1) space.

title: Missing number by XOR
body: Missing number: XOR all indices 0 to n together with all array values — every present number cancels, leaving the absent one. The sum formula works too but risks overflow in fixed-width languages; XOR does not.

title: Single number II and III
body: Two singles among pairs: XOR everything to get their combined XOR, isolate any set bit of it (they differ there), and partition-XOR the array by that bit. Every appears-three-times variant instead counts bits per position modulo three. Know both extensions by shape.

title: Quiz: The lone element
body: Every element appears twice except one. Why does XOR-ing the entire array find it in O(1) space?
payload: {"options": ["XOR sorts the values", "Pairs cancel to zero and the survivor remains", "XOR counts occurrences", "It only works for positive numbers"], "correct": "Pairs cancel to zero and the survivor remains", "explain": "x XOR x is zero and XOR is order-independent, so all duplicates annihilate regardless of position, and zero XOR the unique value returns it untouched."}

title: Kernighan's count
body: Count set bits by looping n equals n AND (n minus 1) until zero — one iteration per SET bit rather than per bit position. On sparse values it beats the shift-and-test loop; both are acceptable, but naming Kernighan is the fluency marker.

title: Counting bits for a range
body: Counting bits for every number 0 to n is a DP: bits of i equal bits of i shifted right one, plus i's lowest bit — or equivalently bits of i AND (i minus 1), plus one. O(n) total, reusing previously computed answers.

title: Built-in popcounts
body: Real code uses intrinsics: Java's Integer.bitCount, Python's bin(n).count or int.bit_count in 3.10 plus, C++ builtin popcount. In interviews, mention the builtin exists, then implement Kernighan if asked to do it manually.

title: Quiz: Kernighan iterations
body: How many loop iterations does Brian Kernighan's bit-count perform for the value 10110000?
payload: {"options": ["8", "3", "4", "1"], "correct": "3", "explain": "Each n AND (n-1) step deletes exactly one set bit, and 10110000 has three set bits — three iterations, versus eight for a shift-through-every-position loop."}

title: Power of two test
body: A positive n is a power of two exactly when n AND (n minus 1) equals zero: powers of two have a single set bit, and removing it leaves nothing. Guard the n greater than zero condition — zero passes the AND test but is not a power of two.

title: Powers of four and beyond
body: Power of four adds a position check: single set bit AND that bit in an even position — test with the mask 0x55555555. Power-of-three has no bit trick; use divisibility of the largest 32-bit power of three. Matching trick to base is the follow-up game.

title: Rounding to powers of two
body: Hash tables sized as powers of two replace modulo with AND (n minus 1) for bucket indexing — a real performance idiom worth mentioning in design contexts. Bit smearing (spreading the highest set bit rightward) rounds up to the next power of two.

title: Quiz: One-liner power check
body: Which expression tests whether positive n is a power of two?
payload: {"options": ["n % 2 == 0", "(n & (n - 1)) == 0", "(n | (n - 1)) == 0", "n >> 1 == 0"], "correct": "(n & (n - 1)) == 0", "explain": "A power of two is one set bit; deleting the lowest set bit leaves zero only in that case. Evenness alone fails — 12 is even but not a power of two."}

title: Sets as integers
body: A bitmask encodes a subset of up to 64 items in one integer: bit i means item i is in. Union is OR, intersection is AND, membership is one AND-shift. Iterating all subsets is counting from 0 to 2 to the n minus 1.

title: Masks as visited state
body: When search states include WHICH items are used — permutation-like exploration, TSP, matchsticks to square — a bitmask replaces the used-boolean array and makes states hashable for memoization: dp of (mask) or dp of (mask, position).

title: Submask enumeration
body: Enumerate every submask of m with: sub equals m, then repeatedly sub equals (sub minus 1) AND m until wrapping past zero. Across all masks this totals 3 to the n — a bound worth quoting when analyzing partition-style bitmask DPs.

title: Quiz: Bitmask membership
body: Using an integer mask to represent a set, how do you test whether item i is included?
payload: {"options": ["mask % i == 0", "(mask & (1 << i)) != 0", "mask ^ i", "mask >> i == 1"], "correct": "(mask & (1 << i)) != 0", "explain": "Shift a lone one to position i and AND it against the mask — nonzero means the bit is set. OR adds membership, AND-with-complement removes it."}

title: Overflow by language
body: Java and C++ ints overflow silently at 2 to the 31 minus 1, wrapping negative; Python promotes to arbitrary precision and never overflows. Reverse integer and atoi are overflow-check questions in Java and bookkeeping questions in Python — say which game you are playing.

title: Checking before it happens
body: Detect overflow BEFORE the multiply-add: rev greater than (INT_MAX minus digit) over 10 means rev times 10 plus digit will exceed. Post-hoc checks are too late in fixed-width languages — the wrap has already corrupted the value.

title: Famous overflow bugs
body: Overflow stories land well: the classic Java binary search bug where lo plus hi overflowed on giant arrays, fixed by lo plus (hi minus lo) over 2. Citing it shows you connect interview trivia to production reality.

title: Quiz: Python overflow
body: What happens when a Python int exceeds 2 to the 31 minus 1?
payload: {"options": ["It wraps negative", "It raises OverflowError", "Nothing — Python ints grow arbitrarily", "It becomes a float"], "correct": "Nothing — Python ints grow arbitrarily", "explain": "Python promotes integers to arbitrary precision automatically. Problems demanding 32-bit overflow behavior require you to SIMULATE the bound with explicit comparisons or masking."}

title: Euclid's algorithm
body: GCD by Euclid: gcd(a, b) equals gcd(b, a mod b), bottoming out when b is zero. Runs in O(log min(a, b)) — the remainder at least halves every two steps. LCM follows as a over gcd times b, ordered to dodge overflow.

title: Why the recurrence holds
body: Any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b (which is a minus some multiple of b), and vice versa — so the divisor sets are identical and the max is preserved. One sentence of justification turns a memorized identity into understanding.

title: GCD in disguise
body: GCD appears inside other problems: rotating an array by k touches cycles of length n over gcd(n, k); water jug feasibility is whether the target divides by gcd of the jugs; simplifying fractions and synchronized orbits both reduce to it.

title: Quiz: Euclid's step
body: What is the recursive step of the Euclidean GCD algorithm?
payload: {"options": ["gcd(a, b) = gcd(a - 1, b)", "gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b)", "gcd(a, b) = gcd(a/2, b/2)", "gcd(a, b) = a * b"], "correct": "gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b)", "explain": "Common divisors of a and b are exactly the common divisors of b and a mod b, so the pair shrinks logarithmically until the remainder hits zero and the survivor is the GCD."}

title: Sieve of Eratosthenes
body: All primes below n: start with everything marked prime, and for each prime p from 2, cross out multiples starting at p squared. O(n log log n) — effectively linear — and the standard preprocessing for any many-primality-queries problem.

title: Single primality checks
body: One number's primality: trial-divide by candidates up to its square root — any factor above the root pairs with one below. Skipping even numbers after 2 halves the work. O(sqrt n) per check; the sieve wins when checks are numerous.

title: Sieve details that get probed
body: Why start crossing at p squared? Smaller multiples of p were crossed by smaller primes already. Count primes is the sieve verbatim; ugly numbers and smooth-number problems reuse the generate-by-multiples idea with a heap or DP.

title: Quiz: Trial division bound
body: Checking whether n is prime by trial division, you only need candidates up to what?
payload: {"options": ["n / 2", "The square root of n", "log n", "All numbers below n"], "correct": "The square root of n", "explain": "Factors pair across the square root: if n = a times b with a at most b, then a is at most sqrt(n). No factor below the root means none above it either."}

title: Modular arithmetic
body: Mod distributes over addition and multiplication: (a plus b) mod m equals (a mod m plus b mod m) mod m, and similarly for products. Big-count problems that say return the answer modulo 10 to the 9 plus 7 expect you to reduce at EVERY step, not once at the end.

title: Negative mods
body: Languages disagree on negative remainders: Python's mod is always non-negative; Java and C++ can return negatives. The portable fix: ((x mod m) plus m) mod m. Circular index arithmetic is where this bites hardest.

title: Division needs inverses
body: Mod does NOT distribute over division — dividing requires the modular multiplicative inverse, via Fermat's little theorem when the modulus is prime. Awareness level: know division-under-mod is special, and name the inverse if pressed.

title: Quiz: Portable negative mod
body: Which expression guarantees a non-negative result for x mod m across Java, C++, and Python?
payload: {"options": ["x % m", "((x % m) + m) % m", "abs(x) % m", "x % (m + 1)"], "correct": "((x % m) + m) % m", "explain": "Java and C++ can return a negative remainder for negative x; adding m and reducing again normalizes it. abs changes the ANSWER for negative x — a subtle wrong fix."}

title: Fast exponentiation
body: pow(x, n) in O(log n): square x while halving n, multiplying the result in whenever n's current bit is odd. The naive n-multiplication loop is the wrong answer this question exists to disqualify.

title: The edge cases
body: Negative exponents invert: pow(x, negative n) equals one over pow(x, n) — and in fixed-width languages negating INT_MIN overflows, so handle n as a long. Zero to the zero is conventionally one here. Edge handling is half this problem's grade.

title: Squaring powers everything
body: Exponentiation by squaring generalizes beyond numbers: the same halving applies to matrix powers (Fibonacci in O(log n)) and to repeated function composition. Recognize compute the n-th anything with an associative combine as this pattern.

title: Quiz: pow complexity
body: What is the time complexity of exponentiation by squaring for pow(x, n)?
payload: {"options": ["O(n)", "O(log n)", "O(sqrt n)", "O(1)"], "correct": "O(log n)", "explain": "Each squaring halves the exponent, so the loop runs once per bit of n. A billion-power needs about 30 multiplications instead of a billion."}

title: Atoi's gauntlet
body: String to integer runs a fixed gauntlet: skip leading whitespace, consume one optional sign, read digits until a non-digit, clamp on overflow to the integer limits. Enumerating those steps aloud BEFORE coding is the intended demonstration of rigor.

title: Reverse integer
body: Reverse integer peels digits with mod ten and rebuilds with times ten plus digit — checking the overflow BEFORE each rebuild step in fixed-width languages. Python needs a manual range check at the end instead, plus care with negative signs.

title: Palindrome number without strings
body: Palindrome number, no string conversion: reverse only HALF the number — stop when the reversed part reaches the remaining part — and compare, with an odd-length middle digit dropped by dividing by ten. Negative numbers fail immediately by the sign.

title: Quiz: Atoi order of operations
body: In atoi, which step order is correct?
payload: {"options": ["Digits, sign, whitespace", "Whitespace, sign, digits, clamp on overflow", "Sign, whitespace, digits", "Clamp, digits, sign"], "correct": "Whitespace, sign, digits, clamp on overflow", "explain": "Leading spaces are skipped first, exactly one sign may follow, digits accumulate until any non-digit stops parsing, and out-of-range values clamp to the integer limits."}

title: Fisher-Yates shuffle
body: Uniform shuffle: iterate i from the end down, swapping position i with a uniformly random position from 0 through i. Every permutation becomes equally likely — the naive swap-with-anywhere-every-step version is provably biased.

title: Reservoir sampling
body: Pick k uniform samples from a stream of unknown length: keep the first k, then replace a random reservoir slot for item i with probability k over i. Linked list random node and random pick index are the k equals one form. The proof is a neat induction — sketch it if asked.

title: Rejection sampling
body: Generate a small uniform range from a bigger one by rejecting overflow: rand10 from rand7 composes two calls into 49 outcomes, keeps 40, retries otherwise. Expected calls stay constant. The keep-a-multiple-of-the-target detail is where correctness lives.

title: Quiz: Reservoir replacement
body: In reservoir sampling with k=1, with what probability does stream item i (1-indexed) replace the current pick?
payload: {"options": ["1/2", "1/i", "1/k", "It never replaces"], "correct": "1/i", "explain": "Accepting item i with probability 1/i leaves every item seen so far with exactly 1/i survival probability — the induction that makes single-pass uniform sampling of unknown-length streams possible."}

title: Distance and comparisons
body: Euclidean distance needs a square root — but COMPARING distances does not: compare squared distances and skip the sqrt, avoiding floating point entirely. K closest points runs entirely on squared magnitudes.

title: Rectangle overlap
body: Two axis-aligned rectangles overlap unless one is fully left, right, above, or below the other — check the four separations and negate. Overlap AREA multiplies the two 1D interval overlaps: max(0, min of rights minus max of lefts) per axis.

title: Collinearity without division
body: Max points on a line groups by slope — but raw float slopes collide on precision. Use reduced fraction pairs (dy over gcd, dx over gcd) with a sign convention as hash keys, or check collinearity by cross product: (y2-y1)(x3-x1) equals (y3-y1)(x2-x1). No division, no epsilon.

title: Quiz: Avoiding float slopes
body: How do you group points by slope without floating point precision bugs?
payload: {"options": ["Round slopes to 6 decimals", "Use reduced (dy, dx) fraction pairs as keys", "Multiply slopes by 1000", "Use doubles instead of floats"], "correct": "Use reduced (dy, dx) fraction pairs as keys", "explain": "Dividing dy and dx by their GCD with a normalized sign yields an exact canonical key per direction. Rounding merely relocates the precision failures."}

title: Permutations vs combinations counting
body: Order matters: permutations P(n, k) equals n factorial over (n minus k) factorial. Order ignored: combinations C(n, k) divides that by k factorial. The interview reflex is mapping does order matter in this problem to the right formula before counting anything.

title: Pascal's triangle
body: Pascal's triangle rows ARE binomial coefficients: each entry is the sum of the two above, mirroring the identity C(n, k) equals C(n-1, k-1) plus C(n-1, k). Generating row n iteratively in O(n) space is the standard easy question hiding this identity.

title: Computing C(n, k) safely
body: Compute C(n, k) multiplicatively — result times (n minus i) over (i plus 1) for i from 0 — never via full factorials, which overflow fast. Symmetry C(n, k) equals C(n, n-k) halves the loop. Counting paths in a grid is C(m plus n minus 2, m minus 1) — connect the dots.

title: Quiz: Order or not
body: Choosing a 3-person committee from 10 people uses which count?
payload: {"options": ["P(10, 3) = 720", "C(10, 3) = 120", "10 cubed", "3 to the 10th"], "correct": "C(10, 3) = 120", "explain": "A committee has no ordering among members, so divide the 720 ordered selections by 3 factorial arrangements each — combinations, not permutations."}

title: Matrix exponentiation
body: Linear recurrences accelerate to O(log n): express the state transition as a matrix and raise it by fast exponentiation — Fibonacci via powers of [[1,1],[1,0]]. Awareness tier: name the technique when n is astronomically large, like 10 to the 18.

title: When it is the intended tool
body: The signal: a DP with O(1)-size state but an input size no linear loop can touch. Climbing stairs for n equals 10 to the 15 or counting length-n strings avoiding a pattern — the transition matrix is small, and log n powers of it finish instantly.

title: Framing it in the room
body: One sentence suffices: this recurrence is linear, so I would encode it as a 2x2 matrix and use exponentiation by squaring for O(log n). Then solve the expected linear version unless the interviewer redirects — calibration over showmanship.

title: Quiz: Fibonacci at scale
body: Computing the 10-to-the-18th Fibonacci number modulo a prime calls for which technique?
payload: {"options": ["Memoized recursion", "Matrix exponentiation", "A bigger array", "Binet's formula with floats"], "correct": "Matrix exponentiation", "explain": "O(n) loops cannot touch 10 to the 18 steps; raising the 2x2 Fibonacci transition matrix to the n-th power by squaring takes about 60 matrix multiplications. Floats lose precision long before."}

title: Integer square root
body: sqrt(x) truncated to an integer: binary search the answer space for the largest m with m squared at most x — guarding overflow by comparing m against x over m instead of squaring in fixed-width languages. O(log x), the expected interview solution.

title: Newton's method
body: Newton's iteration for square roots: repeatedly set guess to the average of guess and x over guess. It converges quadratically — correct digits roughly double per step — and is how real math libraries do it. Offering it after binary search shows depth.

title: Perfect squares and friends
body: Valid perfect square is the same binary search demanding exact equality. sqrt-adjacent problems recur across arithmetic puzzles — bucket counting in O(sqrt n) blocks and factor-pair enumeration to the root reuse the same square-root boundary logic.

title: Quiz: Newton's update
body: Newton's method computes sqrt(x) by iterating which update on the current guess g?
payload: {"options": ["g = g / 2", "g = (g + x / g) / 2", "g = x - g * g", "g = g * g / x"], "correct": "g = (g + x / g) / 2", "explain": "Averaging g with x over g is the Newton step for the function g squared minus x, converging quadratically — far faster per iteration than binary search's single bit."}

title: Merge intervals, the anchor problem
body: Merge intervals anchors the whole cluster: sort by start, keep a growing merged interval, extend its end with a max on overlap, emit and restart on a gap. Overlap test after sorting is simply next start at most current end.

title: Touching or not
body: Decide with the interviewer whether touching intervals — one ends at 5, the next starts at 5 — merge. The comparison flips between at most and strictly less, and both conventions appear in real problems. Asking is a clarity point, not pedantry.

title: The cluster map
body: Interval questions decompose into three moves: sort (by start or end — the choice IS the algorithm), sweep with running state, and sometimes a heap for concurrent resources. Naming which move a new problem needs is pattern recognition paying rent.

title: Quiz: Post-sort overlap test
body: After sorting intervals by start, the next interval overlaps the current merged one exactly when...
payload: {"options": ["next.end > current.start", "next.start <= current.end", "next.start == current.start", "They never overlap after sorting"], "correct": "next.start <= current.end", "explain": "Sorting guarantees next.start is at least current.start, so overlap reduces to the single boundary comparison — the simplification that makes the one-pass sweep valid."}

title: Insert interval
body: Insert interval exploits the input's existing sorted, disjoint structure: emit all intervals ending before the new one, absorb every overlapping interval into it with min-start and max-end, then emit it and the untouched rest. O(n), no re-sorting.

title: Three-phase framing
body: Framing it aloud as three phases — before, merge, after — before typing converts a fiddly index problem into three tiny loops. Most failed attempts come from interleaving the phases into one loop with tangled conditions.

title: Why not just re-sort
body: Appending and re-running merge intervals works at O(n log n) — say it as the baseline, then note the sorted input invites the O(n) three-phase scan. Improving on a correct baseline you named yourself is the ideal interview arc.

title: Quiz: Absorbing overlaps
body: During insert interval's merge phase, the new interval absorbs an overlapping interval by taking which bounds?
payload: {"options": ["Min of starts, max of ends", "Max of starts, min of ends", "The new interval's bounds always", "The older interval's bounds"], "correct": "Min of starts, max of ends", "explain": "The union of two overlapping intervals spans from the earlier start to the later end. Max-start with min-end computes their INTERSECTION — the other problem."}

title: Interval list intersections
body: Intersecting two sorted interval lists: two pointers; the intersection of the current pair is max of starts to min of ends (keep it when nonempty), then advance whichever interval ENDS first — it can intersect nothing further. O(n plus m).

title: Advance the earlier end
body: Advancing the earlier-ending interval is the correctness crux: the later-ending one might still overlap the other list's next interval. It is the interval analogue of merge's take-the-smaller-head — the same two-pointer soul.

title: Intersection vs union vocabulary
body: Keep the two formulas paired mentally: union is min-start to max-end (merging), intersection is max-start to min-end (overlapping region, valid only when start is at most end). Half the interval bugs in interviews are these two swapped.

title: Quiz: Which pointer moves
body: In interval list intersections with two pointers, after emitting an intersection, which side advances?
payload: {"options": ["The one that started earlier", "The one that ends earlier", "Both always", "The shorter interval's list"], "correct": "The one that ends earlier", "explain": "An interval that has ended can never intersect anything else, while the longer-lasting one may still overlap the other list's next entry. Ending time, not starting time, drives the advance."}

title: Minimum rooms, two engines
body: Minimum meeting rooms solves two ways: a min-heap of end times (pop when a room frees, peak size is the answer) or a sweep over sorted plus-one and minus-one events. Same O(n log n); the sweep also answers WHEN the peak occurs.

title: The split-array variant
body: The sweep's slick implementation sorts starts and ends as two separate arrays and walks them with two pointers — a start before the next end opens a room, otherwise one closes. No event objects, no tuples, same counting.

title: Presenting both
body: Offering both engines and their trade-off — the heap generalizes to WHICH room hosts which meeting, the sweep to full concurrency timelines — turns a standard medium into a design conversation. That pivot is where senior signal lives.

title: Quiz: Heap size meaning
body: In the min-heap solution to meeting rooms II, what does the heap's maximum size over the run represent?
payload: {"options": ["Total meetings", "The minimum number of rooms needed", "The longest meeting", "Overlap of the first two meetings"], "correct": "The minimum number of rooms needed", "explain": "Each heap entry is an occupied room's end time; entries coexist exactly while meetings overlap. The high-water mark is the peak simultaneous demand — the room requirement."}

title: Employee free time
body: Employee free time flattens everyone's schedules into one list, sorts, merges busy intervals — and reads the answer from the GAPS between merged blocks. Finding free time is merging busy time and inverting; the inversion is the entire insight.

title: Gaps as first-class objects
body: The gap-reading pattern recurs: available slots between bookings, idle CPU windows, uncovered ranges after merging coverage. Whenever a problem asks where is there NOTHING, merge the somethings and walk the seams.

title: The k-list refinement
body: With k employees' sorted schedules, a heap-based k-way merge feeds the sweep in O(n log k) instead of flatten-and-sort's O(n log n) — the meet-the-family moment where interval merging and merge k lists shake hands. Mention it as the scalable refinement.

title: Quiz: Free time inversion
body: Employee free time is computed by which two-step move?
payload: {"options": ["Intersect all busy intervals, output them", "Merge all busy intervals, output the gaps between merged blocks", "Sort by end and count", "Binary search each hour"], "correct": "Merge all busy intervals, output the gaps between merged blocks", "explain": "Free time is the complement of anyone-is-busy time. Merging produces disjoint busy blocks, and the intervals between consecutive blocks are exactly the common free windows."}

title: My Calendar I
body: Design a calendar rejecting double bookings: keep bookings in a sorted map keyed by start; a new event checks only its floor (must end by the new start) and ceiling (must start at or after the new end) neighbors. O(log n) per booking.

title: Calendar II and III
body: Calendar II tolerates double but not triple booking — track single-booked and overlap intervals separately, checking candidates against the overlap list. Calendar III asks the maximum overlap ever: a sorted map of plus-one and minus-one deltas, prefix-summed per query.

title: Design plus intervals hybrid
body: The calendar ladder is the interval cluster wearing a design-question costume: the interviewer watches structure CHOICE (sorted map versus list versus delta map) as requirements escalate. Practicing all three rungs in sequence builds exactly that muscle.

title: Quiz: Calendar III engine
body: MyCalendarThree must report the maximum booking overlap after each new event. Which structure powers the standard solution?
payload: {"options": ["A max-heap of events", "A sorted map of +1 start and -1 end deltas, prefix-summed", "A trie of times", "A union-find over hours"], "correct": "A sorted map of +1 start and -1 end deltas, prefix-summed", "explain": "Each booking adds a delta pair; sweeping the sorted map's running sum reproduces concurrency over time, and its maximum is the answer — the sweep line living inside a data structure."}

title: One language, mastered
body: Interviewers care less about WHICH language than how fluently you drive it. Pick one and drill until sorting with a custom key, hash map iteration, string building, and heap usage flow without pauses — syntax fumbling reads as inexperience even when thinking is sound.

title: Standard library fluency
body: Know your language's interview toolkit cold: in Python — collections.deque, Counter, defaultdict, heapq, bisect, sorted with key. In Java — ArrayDeque, PriorityQueue with comparators, TreeMap, StringBuilder, Arrays.sort. Reaching for the right tool instantly buys minutes of thinking time.

title: Idioms signal experience
body: Idiomatic code is a hiring signal: Python enumerate instead of range-len, tuple unpacking for swaps, comprehensions where they clarify. Fighting the language with foreign idioms suggests you have not lived in it — practice in the dialect natives write.

title: Quiz: Language choice
body: What matters most about your interview language choice?
payload: {"options": ["Choosing the fastest-executing language", "Deep fluency in whichever language you pick", "Choosing the interviewer's favorite", "Using the newest language"], "correct": "Deep fluency in whichever language you pick", "explain": "Interviewers evaluate problem solving through the medium of code — hesitant syntax obscures good thinking. Any mainstream language is acceptable; mastery of it is not optional."}

title: What Java and Python really pass
body: Java is strictly pass-by-value — but the value of an object variable IS a reference, so methods can mutate the object yet cannot rebind the caller's variable. Python's model is the same idea: call-by-object-reference. Reassignment inside a function never affects the caller.

title: Mutation vs rebinding
body: The distinction that resolves every confusion: list.append inside a function is MUTATION — visible to the caller; list = [] inside is REBINDING — invisible. Interview bugs from aliasing (two variables, one underlying list) trace to exactly this line.

title: Mutable default arguments
body: Python's famous trap: def f(x, acc=[]) evaluates the default ONCE, so every call shares one list that accumulates garbage across calls. The idiom is acc=None, then acc = [] inside. Naming this unprompted when writing recursive helpers is a strong fluency tell.

title: Quiz: Rebinding visibility
body: A Python function receives a list and executes lst = [1, 2, 3]. What does the caller observe?
payload: {"options": ["The caller's list becomes [1, 2, 3]", "Nothing — rebinding a parameter never affects the caller", "A runtime error", "The list is deep-copied"], "correct": "Nothing — rebinding a parameter never affects the caller", "explain": "Assignment points the LOCAL name at a new object; the caller's reference still targets the original. Mutation like lst.append WOULD be visible — the mutation-versus-rebinding line."}

title: The four pillars
body: Encapsulation hides internal state behind an interface; abstraction exposes essential behavior while hiding mechanism; inheritance lets types extend types; polymorphism lets one call site dispatch to many implementations. Have a one-sentence definition AND a concrete example for each.

title: Polymorphism in practice
body: Polymorphism is why code can call shape.area() without knowing the shape: each subclass supplies its own implementation and dynamic dispatch picks at runtime. It is the pillar interviews probe deepest — be ready to contrast overriding (runtime) with overloading (compile-time).

title: Encapsulation's real payoff
body: Encapsulation is not secrecy for its own sake — it is invariant protection: a BankAccount whose balance changes only through deposit and withdraw can enforce non-negativity in one place. Framing pillars by the bugs they prevent beats reciting textbook definitions.

title: Quiz: Which pillar
body: Code calls animal.speak() and a Dog barks while a Cat meows through the same call site. Which OOP pillar is this?
payload: {"options": ["Encapsulation", "Polymorphism", "Abstraction", "Composition"], "correct": "Polymorphism", "explain": "One interface, many behaviors, selected by the object's runtime type — the definition of polymorphism via method overriding and dynamic dispatch."}

title: Interface vs abstract class
body: An interface declares a capability contract with no state — a class can implement many. An abstract class shares partial implementation and fields — single inheritance in Java. Rule of thumb: interfaces for CAN-DO capabilities, abstract classes for IS-A families with shared code.

title: Composition over inheritance
body: Prefer building behavior by CONTAINING collaborators over inheriting: composition swaps behavior at runtime, avoids fragile base class coupling, and dodges hierarchy explosions. The classic illustration: a Stack IMPLEMENTED-WITH a list, not extending one — extending exposes insert-anywhere and breaks the stack contract.

title: When inheritance is right
body: Inheritance still earns its place for genuine is-a relationships with stable base behavior — template method patterns, framework extension points. The interview answer is not never inherit but default to composition; justify inheritance. Nuance beats slogans.

title: Quiz: Modeling a capability
body: Several unrelated classes — File, Socket, Database — all need a close() capability. Which construct fits best?
payload: {"options": ["A shared abstract base class", "An interface like Closeable", "Copy-pasted methods", "A global function"], "correct": "An interface like Closeable", "explain": "These types share no is-a family or state — only a capability contract. Interfaces attach capabilities across unrelated hierarchies; an abstract class would force an artificial common ancestor."}

title: Static vs dynamic typing
body: Static typing (Java, C++, Rust) checks types at compile time, catching mismatches before running and enabling tooling; dynamic typing (Python, JavaScript) defers checks to runtime, buying flexibility and speed of iteration. Neither is winning the argument — know both trade-off columns.

title: Compile-time vs runtime errors
body: A missing semicolon or type mismatch fails compilation; a null dereference, index out of bounds, or division by zero explodes at runtime. The static-typing pitch is shifting bug discovery leftward; the counterpoint is tests catch what types cannot, like logic errors.

title: Type hints as middle ground
body: Python's gradual typing — annotations checked by mypy but ignored at runtime — and TypeScript over JavaScript show the industry converging on optional static analysis atop dynamic languages. Mentioning gradual typing marks your knowledge as current.

title: Quiz: When is it caught
body: Passing a string where an int is expected — when does Java flag it versus Python?
payload: {"options": ["Both at compile time", "Java at compile time, Python at runtime (if at all)", "Both at runtime", "Python at compile time, Java at runtime"], "correct": "Java at compile time, Python at runtime (if at all)", "explain": "Java's static checker rejects the mismatch before execution. Python happily runs until an operation actually fails — possibly never, if the string quacks like the needed type."}

title: Stack vs heap allocation
body: The stack holds each call's frame — locals, parameters, return address — allocated and freed by pointer bumps, fast and automatic. The heap holds dynamically allocated objects with lifetimes beyond any one call, managed by an allocator plus garbage collection or manual frees.

title: What lives where
body: In Java, primitives and references live in stack frames; every object body lives on the heap. Deep recursion overflows the STACK; unbounded object growth exhausts the HEAP. Knowing which resource an algorithm strains explains its failure mode under scale.

title: Why stack allocation is fast
body: Stack allocation is a pointer increment and enjoys cache locality; heap allocation searches for space, synchronizes, and fragments. This is the memory-model backdrop to iterative-versus-recursive trade-offs and why tight loops avoid per-iteration allocations.

title: Quiz: Overflow location
body: Infinite recursion crashes a program by exhausting which memory region?
payload: {"options": ["The heap", "The call stack", "Static storage", "The instruction cache"], "correct": "The call stack", "explain": "Every unreturned call pushes another frame onto the fixed-size stack until it overflows — the literal stack overflow. Heap exhaustion instead throws out-of-memory on allocation."}

title: Three memory philosophies
body: Java garbage-collects: allocate freely, a runtime collector reclaims unreachable objects — safe but with pause and overhead costs. C++ is manual: new and delete (tamed by RAII and smart pointers) — maximal control, footgun included. Rust's ownership checks lifetimes at COMPILE time: safety without a collector.

title: How GC decides
body: Collectors trace reachability from roots — stack references, globals — and reclaim what nothing references. Note that reachable-but-never-used objects still leak: a static map that only grows defeats GC. Generational collectors exploit the fact that most objects die young.

title: Rust's ownership pitch
body: Rust gives each value one owner; moves transfer it, borrows lend it under compiler-enforced rules — no use-after-free, no data races, no GC pauses. Comparing the three models fluently is a genuine differentiator in language-design conversations; one paragraph per model suffices.

title: Quiz: Memory safety without GC
body: Which language achieves memory safety with neither garbage collection nor manual frees?
payload: {"options": ["Java", "C", "Rust", "Python"], "correct": "Rust", "explain": "Rust's ownership and borrowing rules are verified at compile time, so lifetimes are proven safe before the program runs — no runtime collector, no delete calls, no dangling pointers."}

title: Floating point comparison
body: Never compare floats with equality: 0.1 plus 0.2 does not equal 0.3 in binary floating point. Compare within an epsilon, or better, restructure to integers — cents instead of dollars, cross products instead of slopes. Money in floats is the canonical review-comment offense.

title: Shallow vs deep copy
body: A shallow copy duplicates the container but SHARES nested objects — mutate a nested list through one copy and the other sees it. Deep copy recursively duplicates everything. Backtracking bugs where saved paths all mutate together are shallow-copy bugs: append a COPY of the path.

title: Language-specific traps list
body: Keep a per-language trap sheet: Java integer overflow wraps silently and Integer equality above 127 breaks with ==. Python slices copy shallowly and is compares identity. C++ iterator invalidation on container growth. Naming your language's traps preempts the gotcha round.

title: Quiz: The shared-guts bug
body: After a shallow copy of a list of lists, modifying an inner list through the copy does what?
payload: {"options": ["Nothing to the original", "Also changes the original's inner list", "Raises an error", "Deep-copies on write"], "correct": "Also changes the original's inner list", "explain": "Shallow copies duplicate only the outer container; inner references still point at the SAME nested objects. Deep copy is the fix when independent state is required."}

title: Identity vs equality
body: Java's == compares references for objects — use equals for content; Python's is compares identity — use == for content. String comparison with == in Java works just often enough (interning) to create heisenbugs. State which comparison you mean when keys matter.

title: The equals-hashCode contract, applied
body: Custom objects as hash keys demand the contract: equal objects must share a hash code. Override one without the other and map lookups silently miss. In Python, define eq and hash together; better, use tuples, records, or dataclasses that generate them correctly.

title: Immutability's dividends
body: Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe, safe as map keys (their hash never shifts), and alias-proof. The cost is copying on change — usually worth it. Java Strings and Python tuples model it; explaining WHY keys should be immutable connects three interview topics at once.

title: Quiz: Mutable map keys
body: Why is using a mutable object as a hash map key dangerous?
payload: {"options": ["It is slower", "Mutating it after insert changes its hash, stranding the entry", "Maps reject mutable keys", "It causes deadlocks"], "correct": "Mutating it after insert changes its hash, stranding the entry", "explain": "The entry sits in the bucket chosen by the OLD hash; lookups with the new state probe a different bucket and miss. The entry becomes unreachable garbage — immutable keys prevent it entirely."}

title: Exceptions vs error codes
body: Exception philosophy: separate the happy path from failure handling, propagate automatically, fail loudly. The alternative — returned error values — makes failure explicit at every call site. Java adds checked exceptions (declared, compiler-enforced) versus unchecked; know your language's stance.

title: Result types
body: Rust's Result and Option, Go's error returns, and functional Either types encode failure IN the type system: the compiler forces callers to acknowledge it, and null vanishes as a concept. The trade-off is ceremony on every call versus invisible propagation.

title: Interview error hygiene
body: In interviews, do not litter solutions with try-catch — instead SAY the strategy: I would validate inputs here, and this parse could throw, which I would surface to the caller. Demonstrated awareness at boundaries beats defensive clutter throughout.

title: Quiz: Result's enforcement
body: What does returning a Result type give you that throwing an exception does not?
payload: {"options": ["Faster execution always", "The compiler forces callers to handle the failure case", "Automatic retries", "Smaller binaries"], "correct": "The compiler forces callers to handle the failure case", "explain": "Failure lives in the return TYPE, so ignoring it is a compile error. Exceptions propagate invisibly and are easy to forget — the core trade-off between the two philosophies."}

title: Generics in one line
body: Generics parameterize types over other types: List of String is checked at compile time, no casts, no runtime type errors on retrieval. Pre-generics Java collections held Object and cast on the way out — a whole bug class generics deleted.

title: Bounded type parameters
body: Bounds constrain parameters usefully: T extends Comparable of T lets a generic max function actually compare. C++ templates instantiate per-type at compile time; Java erases to Object at runtime — the erasure detail explains why Java cannot do new T().

title: Where interviews touch generics
body: Generics appear in interviews as reading fluency — understanding PriorityQueue of int-array with a comparator — and occasionally in design: make this cache generic over key and value. Writing one clean generic class signature is worthwhile prep.

title: Quiz: What generics buy
body: What is the primary benefit of List<String> over a raw List holding Objects?
payload: {"options": ["It uses less memory", "Compile-time type safety without casting", "It is sorted automatically", "Faster iteration"], "correct": "Compile-time type safety without casting", "explain": "The compiler rejects wrong-type inserts and retrieval needs no cast — errors move from runtime ClassCastExceptions to compile-time messages."}

title: Matching language to problem
body: Different problems favor different tools: Python for algorithmic brevity and rich built-ins; Java or C++ where types clarify design questions or performance semantics matter; scripting for text munging. If you know several, choosing per-problem — and saying why — is a differentiator.

title: Announce the choice
body: Open with one line: I will use Python for concise data structure work — happy to discuss how this maps to Java. It preempts language-availability questions and displays deliberateness. Then never switch mid-problem; consistency beats showmanship.

title: Knowing your language's costs
body: Per-language performance folklore worth knowing: Python function calls and loops carry real overhead (hence library vectorization); Java JIT warms up hot paths; C++ zero-cost abstractions are actually zero-cost. One such fact, placed well, signals depth beyond syntax.

title: Quiz: Multi-language positioning
body: You know Python, Java, and Rust. What is the strongest way to handle language choice in an interview?
payload: {"options": ["Alternate languages between problems to show range", "Pick the best fit, state the reason in one sentence, and stay consistent", "Always choose the fastest language", "Let the interviewer choose"], "correct": "Pick the best fit, state the reason in one sentence, and stay consistent", "explain": "A deliberate, justified choice signals engineering judgment; switching mid-loop signals indecision. Breadth is best shown through one comparative remark, not code tourism."}

title: Process vs thread
body: A process is a running program with its OWN address space; threads live inside a process sharing its memory while owning their own stacks and registers. Sharing memory makes thread communication cheap — and race conditions possible. Process isolation is safety; thread sharing is speed.

title: Context switching
body: A context switch saves one execution context and restores another: registers, program counter, and — for processes — the memory mappings. Process switches cost more than thread switches (address space and cache/TLB effects). Too many threads means the CPU spends its time switching, not working.

title: Chrome as the example
body: Concrete anchor: browsers run each tab as a separate PROCESS so one crash cannot corrupt others — isolation over efficiency. A web server handling requests with a THREAD pool chooses the opposite: shared caches and cheap spawning. Pick examples like these over definitions alone.

title: Quiz: What threads share
body: Threads within one process share which of the following?
payload: {"options": ["Stacks and registers", "Heap memory and open file descriptors", "Nothing", "Program counters"], "correct": "Heap memory and open file descriptors", "explain": "The address space — heap, globals, code — and process resources like file descriptors are shared; each thread keeps only its own stack, registers, and program counter."}

title: Concurrency vs parallelism
body: Concurrency is STRUCTURING work as independently progressing tasks — achievable on one core by interleaving. Parallelism is EXECUTING work simultaneously on multiple cores. A single-core server juggling a thousand connections is concurrent; a matrix multiply across 8 cores is parallel.

title: IO-bound vs CPU-bound
body: IO-bound work (network calls, disk reads) benefits from concurrency — other tasks run while one waits. CPU-bound work needs true parallelism — more cores. Diagnosing which kind of bound a workload has decides whether more threads help at all.

title: The GIL footnote
body: Python's Global Interpreter Lock permits one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time: threads still help IO-bound loads (the lock releases during waits) but not CPU-bound ones — use multiprocessing there. A precise GIL explanation is a reliable interview point-scorer.

title: Quiz: One core, many tasks
body: A single-core machine smoothly runs a chat server handling hundreds of clients. This demonstrates what?
payload: {"options": ["Parallelism", "Concurrency without parallelism", "Hyperthreading", "Distributed computing"], "correct": "Concurrency without parallelism", "explain": "Tasks interleave — each progresses during others' IO waits — but at any instant only one executes. Parallelism requires simultaneous execution on multiple cores."}

title: Race conditions
body: A race condition is correctness depending on unlucky timing: two threads read-modify-write a shared counter, interleave between the read and the write, and one increment vanishes. The failure is intermittent and unreproducible — the worst debugging class there is.

title: Critical sections
body: The code touching shared mutable state is the critical section; correctness demands mutual exclusion — one thread inside at a time. Keep critical sections MINIMAL: lock, touch the shared state, unlock. Long critical sections serialize your parallelism away.

title: Check-then-act
body: The subtlest race shape is check-then-act: if key not in map, then insert — another thread can insert between your check and your act. Compound operations need atomic equivalents (putIfAbsent) or a lock around BOTH steps; each step being individually safe is not enough.

title: Quiz: The lost update
body: Two threads each run counter++ once on a shared counter starting at 0. Which final values are possible without synchronization?
payload: {"options": ["Only 2", "1 or 2", "Only 1", "0, 1, or 2"], "correct": "1 or 2", "explain": "counter++ is read, add, write. If both read 0 before either writes, both write 1 — a lost update. Correct interleavings still give 2, which is what makes races intermittent."}

title: Mutex vs semaphore
body: A mutex is a lock with ownership: one holder, who must release it. A semaphore is a counter admitting up to N holders — a permit pool, usable for signaling between threads too. The interview line: mutex for exclusive access, semaphore for limited resources or coordination.

title: Atomic operations
body: Atomics are hardware-level indivisible operations — compare-and-swap, atomic increment — that fix simple races without locks: an AtomicInteger counter needs no mutex. They power lock-free structures, but composing MULTIPLE atomics is not atomic; compound invariants still need locks.

title: Condition variables
body: Condition variables let a thread sleep until a predicate might hold: wait releases the lock and blocks; notify wakes waiters to re-check. The canonical detail — wait in a WHILE loop, not an if, because of spurious wakeups — is a favorite depth probe.

title: Quiz: Limiting concurrency
body: At most 5 threads may query a fragile downstream service simultaneously. Which primitive fits directly?
payload: {"options": ["A mutex", "A semaphore initialized to 5", "An atomic boolean", "A condition variable alone"], "correct": "A semaphore initialized to 5", "explain": "A counting semaphore hands out up to 5 permits; the sixth thread blocks until one releases. A mutex is the special case of exactly one permit."}

title: Deadlock's four conditions
