body: Deadlock requires all four Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. Break ANY one and deadlock is impossible — which turns the memorized list into an actual prevention toolkit.

title: Lock ordering
body: The workhorse prevention: impose a GLOBAL order on locks and always acquire in that order — circular wait becomes impossible. The classic bug is two threads locking A-then-B and B-then-A; the classic fix is everyone locks A before B, by ID or address.

title: Other escapes
body: Alternatives each break one condition: acquire all locks atomically upfront (no hold-and-wait), use tryLock with timeout and back off (preemption, effectively), or reduce to one lock (no circularity). Naming which CONDITION each tactic breaks is the answer structure interviewers want.

title: Quiz: Why ordering works
body: Enforcing a global lock acquisition order prevents deadlock by eliminating which Coffman condition?
payload: {"options": ["Mutual exclusion", "Circular wait", "No preemption", "Hold and wait"], "correct": "Circular wait", "explain": "A cycle of waiters requires someone holding a later lock to wait on an earlier one — impossible if everyone acquires in ascending order. One broken condition, deadlock gone."}

title: Thread safety defined
body: Thread-safe code behaves correctly under any interleaving of concurrent callers. Routes there: immutability (nothing to race on), confinement (each thread owns its data), synchronization (locks guarding shared state), or thread-safe library structures like ConcurrentHashMap.

title: Producer-consumer
body: The producer-consumer pattern decouples work generation from processing through a BOUNDED blocking queue: producers block when full (backpressure), consumers block when empty. It is the standard design-a-concurrent-thing interview question — practice sketching it with two condition variables.

title: Why bounded matters
body: An unbounded queue lets fast producers outrun consumers until memory dies. The bound converts overload into backpressure — producers slow to match consumers. Volunteering the memory argument for boundedness is precisely the systems maturity being screened for.

title: Quiz: Full queue behavior
body: In producer-consumer with a bounded blocking queue, what happens when a producer finds the queue full?
payload: {"options": ["It drops the item", "It blocks until a consumer removes an item", "The queue grows", "It crashes"], "correct": "It blocks until a consumer removes an item", "explain": "Blocking on full is the backpressure mechanism — production throttles to consumption speed automatically, keeping memory bounded. Dropping or growing are different, explicit policy choices."}

title: Virtual memory
body: Virtual memory gives every process a private address space, mapped to physical RAM in pages by the OS and hardware. Benefits stack up: process isolation, memory over-commitment via swapping, and the illusion of contiguous space over fragmented physical frames.

title: Paging and page faults
body: Address translation walks page tables, cached by the TLB. Touching an unmapped page triggers a page fault — the OS loads it from disk or backing store, brutally slower than RAM. Thrashing is the death spiral of constant faulting when working sets exceed memory.

title: Leaks in managed languages
body: Memory leaks survive garbage collection: a static cache that only grows, listeners never unregistered, thread-locals never cleared — all REACHABLE, so the collector keeps them. Leak means unintentionally retained, not merely unfreed. That distinction is a common probe.

title: Quiz: Leaking with a GC
body: How can a Java program leak memory despite garbage collection?
payload: {"options": ["It cannot", "By keeping unwanted objects reachable, e.g. an ever-growing static map", "By forgetting to call free", "By using too many threads"], "correct": "By keeping unwanted objects reachable, e.g. an ever-growing static map", "explain": "GC reclaims only UNREACHABLE objects. References held in long-lived structures pin objects forever — the collector is doing its job; the program's retention is the bug."}

title: Scheduling basics
body: The scheduler multiplexes runnable threads onto cores. Classic strategies: round-robin time slices, priority-based preemption, and multilevel feedback queues that demote CPU hogs and favor interactive tasks. Awareness tier — the vocabulary matters more than the algorithms.

title: Preemptive vs cooperative
body: Preemptive scheduling interrupts threads at timer boundaries — no one can hog a core; cooperative scheduling waits for tasks to yield — simpler, but one bad actor stalls everything. Async event loops (JavaScript, Python asyncio) are cooperative: a blocking call in the loop freezes the world.

title: Starvation and priority inversion
body: Two named pathologies worth recognizing: starvation — a low-priority task never scheduled under load — and priority inversion — a high-priority task stuck behind a low-priority lock holder. The famous Mars Pathfinder reboot bug was priority inversion; priority inheritance fixed it.

title: Quiz: The frozen event loop
body: In a cooperative single-threaded event loop, one callback enters an infinite computation. What happens to other tasks?
payload: {"options": ["They run on other threads", "They are starved until the callback yields", "The OS preempts the callback", "They queue-jump by priority"], "correct": "They are starved until the callback yields", "explain": "Cooperative scheduling relies on tasks yielding voluntarily; nothing preempts a running callback. This is why blocking work must be offloaded from event loops."}

title: User vs kernel mode
body: CPUs run in user mode (restricted) or kernel mode (full hardware access). Applications must ask the kernel for privileged work — touching hardware, other processes' memory — via system calls. The boundary is the OS's security and stability perimeter.

title: System calls
body: A system call — read, write, open, fork — traps into the kernel, switches mode, executes, and returns. Mode transitions cost real time, which is why batching IO (buffered readers, writev) outperforms many tiny calls. Library functions like printf WRAP syscalls; they are not syscalls themselves.

title: Why this appears in interviews
body: This surfaces in performance intuition questions: why is reading a file byte-by-byte slow (a syscall per byte) or what happens during a network read (block in kernel until data arrives). One accurate sentence about crossing the boundary usually suffices.

title: Quiz: Buffered IO's win
body: Why does buffered file reading dramatically outperform reading one byte at a time?
payload: {"options": ["Disks read bytes backwards", "It amortizes expensive syscalls and mode switches over large chunks", "Buffers compress data", "The kernel caches nothing otherwise"], "correct": "It amortizes expensive syscalls and mode switches over large chunks", "explain": "Each read syscall pays a user-to-kernel round trip. One 64KB read replaces 65536 one-byte trips — the classic batching argument, applicable to network and database calls alike."}

title: File descriptors
body: A file descriptor is a small integer handle into a process's table of open IO resources — files, sockets, pipes all uniformly. Descriptors 0, 1, and 2 are stdin, stdout, stderr; shell redirection is descriptor rewiring. Everything is a file is the Unix design slogan behind it.

title: Descriptor exhaustion
body: Descriptors are a finite per-process resource; leaking them — opening sockets or files without closing — eventually fails all new IO with too many open files. It is a classic production incident, and the reason languages push with-open patterns and try-with-resources.

title: Blocking vs non-blocking IO
body: A blocking read parks the thread until data arrives — simple, thread-per-connection. Non-blocking IO plus readiness notification (epoll, kqueue) lets ONE thread supervise thousands of sockets — the engine inside nginx and Node. The C10K problem is the historical frame for why this matters.

title: Quiz: Standard descriptors
body: Which file descriptor numbers are conventionally stdin, stdout, and stderr?
payload: {"options": ["1, 2, 3", "0, 1, 2", "-1, 0, 1", "3, 4, 5"], "correct": "0, 1, 2", "explain": "Unix convention: 0 reads input, 1 writes output, 2 writes errors. Shell redirection like 2>&1 is literally re-pointing descriptor 2 at descriptor 1's target."}

title: URL to pixels, the pipeline
body: What happens when you type a URL: DNS resolves the name to an IP, TCP connects (plus TLS for HTTPS), the browser sends an HTTP request, the server responds with HTML, and the browser parses, fetches subresources, and renders. Narrate those five stages FIRST, then go deep where asked.

title: Depth on demand
body: This question is a zoom test: the interviewer picks a stage and descends — how does DNS caching work, what is in the TCP handshake, how does the browser build the render tree. Prepare one extra level of detail under each stage rather than a memorized monologue.

title: Do not forget the ends
body: Two commonly dropped bookends: BEFORE DNS, the browser checks its own and the OS cache, and may short-circuit entirely; AFTER the response, rendering, JavaScript execution, and further requests continue for seconds. Mentioning caches and the render phase rounds out the answer.

title: Quiz: Pipeline order
body: Which ordering is correct for loading an HTTPS page?
payload: {"options": ["TCP, DNS, TLS, HTTP", "DNS, TCP, TLS, HTTP", "TLS, DNS, TCP, HTTP", "HTTP, DNS, TCP, TLS"], "correct": "DNS, TCP, TLS, HTTP", "explain": "You need the IP before connecting, a TCP connection before securing it, and a secure channel before the encrypted HTTP request flows. Each stage feeds the next."}

title: DNS resolution
body: DNS resolution walks a hierarchy: your resolver asks a root server, which points to the top-level-domain servers, which point to the domain's authoritative server, which returns the record. Recursive resolvers do this walk FOR you and cache the results.

title: DNS caching and TTL
body: Every DNS answer carries a TTL; browsers, OSes, and resolvers cache until it expires. Caching is why most lookups never leave your machine — and why DNS changes propagate slowly. Low TTLs enable fast failover at the cost of more lookups.

title: Record types worth naming
body: Know four record types: A maps name to IPv4 (AAAA for IPv6), CNAME aliases one name to another, MX routes mail, NS delegates to name servers. DNS load balancing — returning different IPs per query — is the one-liner connecting DNS to system design.

title: Quiz: The resolver walk
body: In a full uncached DNS resolution, which server order does the recursive resolver query?
payload: {"options": ["Authoritative, TLD, root", "Root, TLD, authoritative", "TLD only", "Any random order"], "correct": "Root, TLD, authoritative", "explain": "The hierarchy narrows: root servers know where dot-com lives, TLD servers know the domain's name servers, and the authoritative server holds the actual record."}

title: TCP vs UDP
body: TCP is a connection with guarantees: ordered, reliable, retransmitted, flow-controlled bytes. UDP is fire-and-forget datagrams: no ordering, no delivery promise, minimal latency. Web pages and APIs ride TCP; gaming, voice, video, and DNS queries favor UDP where stale retransmits are useless.

title: The three-way handshake
body: TCP opens with SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK: each side proves it can send and receive and exchanges initial sequence numbers. That full round trip before any data is why connection reuse (keep-alive) and connection pooling matter for latency.

title: Reliability's machinery
body: TCP's guarantees come from sequence numbers, ACKs, retransmission timers, and congestion control that throttles senders when the network drops packets. QUIC — HTTP/3's transport — rebuilds this reliability over UDP to cut handshake latency; one sentence of QUIC awareness lands well.

title: Quiz: Choosing the transport
body: A real-time multiplayer game sends 60 position updates per second. Why is UDP typically preferred?
payload: {"options": ["UDP is more secure", "A retransmitted stale position is worthless; low latency beats reliability here", "TCP cannot send small packets", "UDP guarantees ordering"], "correct": "A retransmitted stale position is worthless; low latency beats reliability here", "explain": "TCP would delay newer updates to retransmit older ones — head-of-line blocking. The next position update supersedes the lost one anyway, so UDP's fire-and-forget fits."}

title: HTTP methods
body: HTTP's core verbs: GET reads (safe, cacheable), POST creates or triggers, PUT replaces (idempotent), PATCH partially updates, DELETE removes (idempotent). Idempotency — repeat-safe requests — decides retry behavior; that is the practical reason the distinctions matter.

title: Status code families
body: Codes group by hundreds: 2xx success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content), 3xx redirects (301 permanent, 304 not modified), 4xx client errors (400, 401 unauthenticated, 403 forbidden, 404, 429 rate-limited), 5xx server errors (500, 503). Knowing 401 versus 403 is a recurring micro-probe.

title: Statelessness and headers
body: HTTP is stateless: each request carries everything needed — hence headers (Content-Type, Authorization, Cache-Control) and cookies to simulate continuity. Statelessness is what lets any server behind a load balancer answer any request; the design consequence worth saying aloud.

title: Quiz: 401 vs 403
body: What distinguishes HTTP 401 from 403?
payload: {"options": ["They are interchangeable", "401 means not authenticated; 403 means authenticated but not allowed", "401 is a server error", "403 means not found"], "correct": "401 means not authenticated; 403 means authenticated but not allowed", "explain": "401 challenges you to prove identity (missing or bad credentials); 403 recognizes you and still refuses — authorization, not authentication, failed."}

title: TLS in one paragraph
body: HTTPS is HTTP inside a TLS tunnel. The handshake uses ASYMMETRIC cryptography to authenticate the server via its certificate and agree on keys, then switches to fast SYMMETRIC encryption for the actual data. Certificates chain to trusted certificate authorities baked into your OS or browser.

title: What TLS buys
body: Three guarantees: confidentiality (eavesdroppers see noise), integrity (tampering is detected), and authentication (the server is who the certificate says). Note what it does NOT hide: the destination host and traffic timing remain visible to observers.

title: Why the hybrid design
body: The asymmetric-then-symmetric structure exists because public-key operations are orders of magnitude slower than symmetric ciphers: use the expensive math once to exchange a session key, then encrypt gigabytes cheaply. That one sentence answers most TLS follow-ups.

title: Quiz: TLS key strategy
body: Why does TLS use asymmetric cryptography only during the handshake?
payload: {"options": ["Asymmetric crypto is insecure for data", "Asymmetric operations are slow, so they bootstrap a fast symmetric session key", "Certificates expire mid-connection", "Symmetric keys are longer"], "correct": "Asymmetric operations are slow, so they bootstrap a fast symmetric session key", "explain": "Public-key math authenticates and exchanges keys safely over an untrusted channel; bulk data then flows under cheap symmetric encryption — the best of both systems."}

title: HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2
body: HTTP/1.1 handles one request at a time per connection, so browsers opened six parallel connections and still suffered head-of-line blocking. HTTP/2 multiplexes many concurrent streams over ONE connection, adds header compression and server push. Awareness tier: multiplexing is the headline.

title: HTTP/3 and QUIC
body: HTTP/2 still blocks at the TCP layer — one lost packet stalls all streams. HTTP/3 moves to QUIC over UDP: independent streams, faster handshakes, connection migration across networks. The arc — fewer connections, less blocking, lower latency — is the story to tell.

title: Old habits made obsolete
body: HTTP/1.1-era hacks — domain sharding, sprite sheets, file concatenation — existed to work around connection limits; HTTP/2 multiplexing made them unnecessary or counterproductive. Citing one obsolete optimization shows you understand WHY the protocol changed.

title: Quiz: HTTP/2's headline
body: What is HTTP/2's principal improvement over HTTP/1.1?
payload: {"options": ["Encryption by default", "Multiplexing many streams over a single connection", "New status codes", "Replacing TCP with UDP"], "correct": "Multiplexing many streams over a single connection", "explain": "Concurrent interleaved streams eliminate request queuing per connection. Replacing TCP with UDP is HTTP/3's move via QUIC — a common conflation to avoid."}

title: REST conventions
body: REST models an API as resources named by nouns and manipulated by HTTP verbs: GET /users/42 reads, POST /users creates, DELETE /users/42 removes. Verbs in URLs — /getUser, /createUser — are the anti-pattern reviewers flag first.

title: Designing responses
body: Good REST uses status codes semantically (201 with a Location header on create, 404 for missing resources), plural nouns, nesting for ownership (/users/42/orders), and pagination on collections. Idempotency guides verb choice: PUT retries safely, POST may not.

title: REST in interviews
body: Design an API for X is a common intern-level design warm-up: enumerate resources, map operations to verbs and routes, sketch request and response bodies, and mention auth and pagination. Five minutes of clean conventions beats exotic architecture talk.

title: Quiz: RESTful create
body: Which endpoint design is most RESTful for creating a new order?
payload: {"options": ["GET /createOrder", "POST /orders", "POST /doCreateOrder", "PUT /orders/new"], "correct": "POST /orders", "explain": "Resources are nouns; POST to the collection creates a member, conventionally returning 201 with the new resource's location. Verbs belong in the HTTP method, not the path."}

title: Cookies and sessions
body: Classic session auth: the server stores session state and hands the browser a session ID cookie, sent automatically on every request to that domain. Server-side sessions revoke instantly but require shared session storage once you scale past one server.

title: JWTs
body: A JWT packs claims (user ID, expiry, roles) into a SIGNED token the server verifies without any session store — stateless auth that scales horizontally for free. The trade-off: revocation before expiry is hard, so pair short-lived access tokens with refresh tokens.

title: Sessions vs tokens, the decision
body: The interview comparison: sessions centralize state (easy revocation, storage dependency), JWTs distribute it (no lookup per request, awkward revocation). Also name the cookie security flags — HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite — as the one-line defense against theft and CSRF.

title: Quiz: JWT's core property
body: What allows a server to trust a JWT without a session-store lookup?
payload: {"options": ["The token is encrypted by the browser", "The server verifies the token's signature over its claims", "JWTs are stored in the database", "The token is very long"], "correct": "The server verifies the token's signature over its claims", "explain": "The signature proves the claims were issued by the trusted signer and not altered — statelessness by cryptography. The catch: a stolen, unexpired token stays valid, hence short lifetimes."}

title: Polling vs WebSockets
body: Short polling asks are we there yet on a timer — simple, wasteful, latent. Long polling holds the request open until data exists. WebSockets upgrade the HTTP connection into a persistent two-way channel — true push for chat, live dashboards, and games.

title: Server-sent events
body: SSE is the middle option: a one-directional server-to-client stream over plain HTTP with automatic reconnection — perfect for feeds and notifications where the client rarely talks back. Naming SSE alongside WebSockets shows the full menu, not just the famous item.

title: Choosing the push strategy
body: The decision rubric: rare updates — polling is fine and simplest; server-to-client streams — SSE; genuine bidirectional low-latency — WebSockets, accepting the connection-state and scaling costs. Design questions about chat or live scores expect this ladder.

title: Quiz: Bidirectional push
body: A live chat app needs sub-second, two-way message delivery. Which mechanism fits best?
payload: {"options": ["Short polling every 5 seconds", "WebSockets", "Server-sent events", "DNS updates"], "correct": "WebSockets", "explain": "Chat needs pushes BOTH directions over a persistent channel. SSE only streams server-to-client; polling adds latency and waste. WebSockets are built for exactly this."}

title: CDNs
body: A CDN replicates content to edge servers near users, cutting latency (shorter round trips) and origin load. Static assets — images, scripts, video — are the classic cargo; modern CDNs also cache API responses and run edge compute.

title: Cache invalidation at the edge
body: The CDN's hard problem is freshness: cache-control headers set lifetimes, purges force updates, and versioned asset names (app.3f2a1.js) sidestep invalidation entirely — deploy new names, never stale content. Content-hashed filenames are the interview-worthy trick.

title: CDNs in design answers
body: In any design question serving global users, placing a CDN in front of static content is a near-free correctness point: name the latency win, the origin offload, and the invalidation strategy. It also absorbs traffic spikes and some DDoS pressure — say so.

title: Quiz: Dodging invalidation
body: How do content-hashed filenames like app.9f31c.js eliminate CDN cache invalidation problems?
payload: {"options": ["They compress better", "New content gets a new URL, so old caches are simply never hit again", "CDNs refuse unhashed files", "Hashes encrypt the file"], "correct": "New content gets a new URL, so old caches are simply never hit again", "explain": "Immutable URL-to-content mapping means edge caches can hold assets forever; deploys reference fresh names in the HTML, and stale copies die of neglect rather than purges."}

title: IP and ports
body: An IP address locates a MACHINE; a port selects a PROCESS's socket on it. Well-known ports anchor the ecosystem: 80 HTTP, 443 HTTPS, 22 SSH, 53 DNS. A connection is the four-tuple of source and destination IPs and ports — which is how one server juggles thousands of clients.

title: Sockets
body: A socket is the programming handle for a network endpoint: servers bind an address, listen, and accept; clients connect. Accept returns a NEW socket per client while the listening socket keeps listening — the detail that makes concurrent servers make sense.

title: Private addresses and NAT
body: Private ranges (10.x, 192.168.x) do not route on the public internet; NAT rewrites them to a shared public IP at the router, tracking flows by port. IPv4 exhaustion is why NAT is everywhere and IPv6's vast space exists. One sentence each is the expected depth.

title: Quiz: The four-tuple
body: What uniquely identifies a TCP connection?
payload: {"options": ["The destination IP alone", "Source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port", "The URL", "The MAC address"], "correct": "Source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port", "explain": "The four-tuple distinguishes every flow — which is how a server on one port 443 maintains thousands of simultaneous distinct connections from different client IP-port pairs."}

title: The relational model
body: Relational databases store rows in typed tables. A PRIMARY key uniquely identifies each row; a FOREIGN key references another table's primary key, encoding relationships. Constraints — not-null, unique, foreign-key — make the database enforce invariants applications would otherwise botch.

title: Choosing keys
body: Natural keys (email) carry meaning but change; surrogate keys (auto-increment or UUID ids) are stable and opaque — the common default. Composite keys span columns, typical in join tables mapping many-to-many relationships. Being able to sketch a users-orders-products schema quickly is table stakes.

title: Referential integrity
body: Foreign keys police referential integrity: no order may reference a nonexistent user, and deletion policies (cascade, restrict, set null) decide what happens to dependents. Naming the delete policy question when sketching schemas signals real modeling experience.

title: Quiz: Foreign key role
body: What does a foreign key constraint guarantee?
payload: {"options": ["Faster joins", "Referenced rows actually exist in the parent table", "Unique values in the column", "Automatic indexing of all columns"], "correct": "Referenced rows actually exist in the parent table", "explain": "It enforces referential integrity — an order cannot point at user 42 unless user 42 exists, and deletion of user 42 must follow the declared policy. Uniqueness is the primary key's job."}

title: JOIN types
body: INNER JOIN keeps only matching rows; LEFT JOIN keeps every left row with nulls for missing matches; RIGHT mirrors it; FULL OUTER keeps both sides. Find users with NO orders is the classic probe: LEFT JOIN then WHERE the right side IS NULL.

title: GROUP BY and HAVING
body: GROUP BY collapses rows per key for aggregates; WHERE filters rows BEFORE grouping, HAVING filters groups AFTER. Customers with more than five orders needs HAVING count greater than 5 — putting the aggregate condition in WHERE is the standard mistake.

title: Window functions
body: Window functions compute across related rows WITHOUT collapsing them: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, running SUM OVER a partition. Top three earners per department — PARTITION BY department, ORDER BY salary, filter rank at most 3 — is the archetypal interview query for them.

title: Quiz: Users without orders
body: Which query pattern finds users who placed no orders?
payload: {"options": ["INNER JOIN orders", "LEFT JOIN orders, then WHERE orders.id IS NULL", "GROUP BY user", "FULL OUTER JOIN with HAVING"], "correct": "LEFT JOIN orders, then WHERE orders.id IS NULL", "explain": "LEFT JOIN preserves every user, filling order columns with NULL where nothing matched — the null check isolates exactly the order-less users. INNER JOIN would discard them."}

title: B-tree indexes
body: An index is a B-tree over a column's values: lookups, ranges, and sorted scans in O(log n) pages instead of a full table scan. The tree is wide and shallow — hundreds of keys per node — so real lookups touch only three or four disk pages.

title: When indexes hurt
body: Indexes tax every write — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE must maintain each one — and consume storage. They also go UNUSED by queries that do not match their leading columns or that wrap the column in a function. Index the columns your WHERE clauses and joins actually use.

title: Composite index order
body: A composite index on (a, b) serves filters on a, or on a and b — but NOT b alone, like a phone book sorted by last name is useless for first-name searches. Leading-column order is the most common index design question in interviews.

title: Quiz: Composite index reach
body: An index exists on (last_name, first_name). Which query can it serve efficiently?
payload: {"options": ["WHERE first_name = 'Ana'", "WHERE last_name = 'Chen'", "WHERE UPPER(last_name) = 'CHEN'", "None of these"], "correct": "WHERE last_name = 'Chen'", "explain": "The index is sorted by last_name first, so leading-column filters seek directly. first_name alone lacks an entry point, and wrapping the column in a function defeats the sorted order."}

title: ACID
body: Transactions promise ACID: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (constraints hold before and after), Isolation (concurrent transactions do not corrupt each other), Durability (committed means survives crashes). The money-transfer example — debit and credit must both land — sells atomicity instantly.

title: Isolation levels
body: Isolation is a dial, not a switch: read committed permits non-repeatable reads; repeatable read stops them; serializable acts as if transactions ran one at a time, at a throughput cost. Knowing that a dial EXISTS — and the phantom-read vocabulary — is the expected depth.

title: How durability works
body: Durability comes from write-ahead logging: changes hit an append-only log BEFORE commit is acknowledged; crash recovery replays the log. WAL is the same trick underlying replication streams and many storage engines — one mechanism, several payoffs.

title: Quiz: Mid-transfer crash
body: A transfer debits account A, then the database crashes before crediting B. What does atomicity guarantee after recovery?
payload: {"options": ["The debit stands, credit is retried", "The whole transaction rolled back — A keeps its money", "Both accounts are frozen", "Half the amount moves"], "correct": "The whole transaction rolled back — A keeps its money", "explain": "Uncommitted work vanishes on recovery: all or nothing. The write-ahead log is what lets recovery decide which transactions completed and which must be undone."}

title: Normalization
body: Normalization splits data so every fact lives once: customer addresses in a customers table, not copied onto every order. It kills update anomalies — change the address in one place — at the cost of joins to reassemble. Third normal form is the practical default.

title: Denormalization
body: Denormalization deliberately duplicates data to skip joins — an order row caching the product name, a follower_count column instead of counting rows. It buys read speed and pays in write complexity and drift risk. Analytics and feed systems denormalize routinely.

title: The interview framing
body: Present it as a spectrum with a default: normalize until reads hurt, then denormalize measured hot spots, keeping a source of truth and syncing derived copies. That sentence handles the normalization question at any depth of follow-up.

title: Quiz: The anomaly
body: Storing a customer's address on every order row creates which classic problem?
payload: {"options": ["Slower reads", "Update anomaly — changing the address requires touching many rows and risks inconsistency", "Index bloat only", "Deadlocks"], "correct": "Update anomaly — changing the address requires touching many rows and risks inconsistency", "explain": "Duplicated facts drift: miss one row and two addresses disagree with no authority. Normalization's whole purpose is storing each fact exactly once."}

title: The NoSQL menu
body: Four NoSQL families by data shape: document stores (MongoDB — nested JSON per entity), key-value (Redis, DynamoDB — opaque blobs by key), wide-column (Cassandra — sparse rows, massive write throughput), graph (Neo4j — relationship traversal). Name the family, its shape, and one exemplar.

title: When each wins
body: Choose by access pattern: relational for transactions and ad-hoc queries across entities; document when an entity is naturally one nested blob; key-value for caching and sessions; wide-column for write-heavy time-series at scale; graph when queries ARE traversals (friends of friends).

title: The scaling subtext
body: The historical driver: relational databases scale UP (bigger machine) gracefully but shard awkwardly; most NoSQL systems were born distributed, trading joins and transactions for horizontal scale. Modern reality blurs it — Postgres scales far, NewSQL adds SQL over distribution — so avoid absolutism.

title: Quiz: Session storage
body: Storing millions of short-lived user sessions accessed by session ID calls for which store?
payload: {"options": ["A graph database", "A key-value store like Redis", "A normalized relational schema", "A document store with rich queries"], "correct": "A key-value store like Redis", "explain": "The access pattern is pure get and set by key with TTL expiry — no queries, no joins. In-memory key-value stores are built for exactly this shape and speed."}

title: CAP theorem
body: CAP: under a network Partition, a distributed system must choose Consistency (refuse some requests) or Availability (serve possibly-stale data). Partitions are facts of networks, so the real decision is behavior DURING them — CP versus AP.

title: CP vs AP by example
body: A bank ledger picks consistency: better to reject a withdrawal than double-spend. A social feed or shopping cart picks availability: stale likes beat an error page. Tying the letter choice to a business consequence is what elevates the answer.

title: Beyond the acronym
body: Two refinements worth one sentence each: absent partitions, the trade-off is latency versus consistency (PACELC), and eventual consistency means replicas converge after writes stop — the model behind DNS and most AP stores. Do not claim you pick two of three; partitions are not optional.

title: Quiz: During the partition
body: In CAP, what does an AP system do when a network partition splits its replicas?
payload: {"options": ["It shuts down", "It keeps serving requests, accepting that some answers may be stale", "It refuses writes until healed", "It merges the partitions"], "correct": "It keeps serving requests, accepting that some answers may be stale", "explain": "Availability under partition means answering with possibly divergent data, reconciling later — the eventual consistency posture. CP systems instead reject requests to preserve one truth."}

title: Reading a query plan
body: Query performance intuition starts with EXPLAIN: a full table scan reads every row — fine for tiny tables, fatal for hot queries on big ones; an index seek jumps straight to matching rows. When a production query is slow, the plan is where diagnosis begins.

title: Selectivity
body: Indexes shine on SELECTIVE predicates — few matching rows. Filtering status equals active where 95 percent of rows match gains little; the optimizer may rightly prefer scanning. Selectivity is also why leading a composite index with the most selective useful column tends to win.

title: Common query killers
body: Patterns that silently force scans: functions wrapping indexed columns (WHERE lower(email)), leading-wildcard LIKE, type mismatches coercing the column, and OR across differently-indexed columns. N-plus-one query loops — one query per row in application code — are the ORM-era cousin.

title: Quiz: The wrapped column
body: Why does WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024 ignore an index on created_at?
payload: {"options": ["Years cannot be indexed", "Applying a function to the column defeats the index's sorted order", "The index is corrupt", "2024 is too large"], "correct": "Applying a function to the column defeats the index's sorted order", "explain": "The index sorts raw created_at values, not YEAR of them, so every row must be evaluated. Rewrite as a range — created_at at least Jan 1 and before next Jan 1 — and the index seeks."}

title: ORMs
body: An ORM maps classes to tables and objects to rows — SQLAlchemy, Hibernate, Prisma — trading raw SQL for productivity and type integration. Awareness tier: know the mapping idea, the lazy-versus-eager loading distinction, and that generated SQL can surprise you.

title: The N-plus-one problem
body: The signature ORM failure: load 100 orders, then lazily touch order.customer in a loop — 101 queries fire. Fixes are eager loading (join fetch), batching, or a hand-written join. Naming N-plus-one with its fix is the single highest-value ORM fact.

title: When to drop to SQL
body: The balanced take interviews reward: ORMs for routine CRUD and schema evolution; hand-written SQL for reports, bulk operations, and anything performance-critical — while ALWAYS understanding the SQL being generated. Tool skepticism plus fluency, not tribalism.

title: Quiz: 101 queries
body: Loading a list of 100 orders then accessing each order's customer lazily produces 101 queries. What is this called and what fixes it?
payload: {"options": ["Deadlock; add locks", "The N-plus-one problem; eager-load the association", "Cache miss; add Redis", "Index scan; add an index"], "correct": "The N-plus-one problem; eager-load the association", "explain": "One query for the list plus one per element. Eager loading joins or batches the association into the initial fetch, collapsing 101 round trips into one or two."}

title: Connection pooling
body: Opening a database connection costs a TCP handshake, auth, and server-side setup — far too much per request. A pool keeps warm connections that requests borrow and return, capping concurrency and amortizing setup. Every serious backend runs one.

title: Sizing and starving
body: Pool size is a real dial: too small starves requests into queueing; too large overwhelms the database (each connection costs server memory and scheduler load). Leaked connections — borrowed, never returned — exhaust pools and mimic outages; the fix is scoped acquire-release patterns.

title: Pooling in design answers
body: In system design, the pool is the throttle between app fleet and database: mention it as the reason app instances times pool size must respect the database's max connections — and why serverless platforms with thousands of tiny instances need proxy poolers like pgbouncer.

title: Quiz: Why pool at all
body: What is the primary purpose of a database connection pool?
payload: {"options": ["Encrypting queries", "Reusing expensive connections instead of opening one per request", "Backing up data", "Load-balancing between databases"], "correct": "Reusing expensive connections instead of opening one per request", "explain": "Connection setup — handshake, authentication, session state — dwarfs many queries' cost. Borrow-and-return of warm connections amortizes it and simultaneously caps database concurrency."}

title: Git's model
body: Git stores snapshots, not diffs: each commit points to a full tree plus its parent commits, and branches are just movable pointers to commits. Internalizing pointers-to-snapshots makes merge, rebase, and detached HEAD stop being magic.

title: Merge vs rebase
body: Merge ties two histories with a merge commit — truthful, but braided. Rebase replays your commits atop the target — linear and clean, but it REWRITES history. The team rule that follows: rebase your own unpushed work freely; never rebase shared branches.

title: PR workflow
body: The standard loop: branch from main, commit small coherent units, push, open a pull request, address review, squash or merge, delete the branch. Speaking this vocabulary fluently — and knowing why small PRs review faster and revert cleaner — is expected of interns who have shipped.

title: Quiz: The rebase rule
body: Why is rebasing a branch that teammates have already pulled considered dangerous?
payload: {"options": ["It deletes their files", "It rewrites shared history, forcing divergent copies and painful reconciliation", "It is slower than merging", "It skips code review"], "correct": "It rewrites shared history, forcing divergent copies and painful reconciliation", "explain": "Rebase creates NEW commits replacing the old ones; anyone holding the originals now disagrees with the branch. Private history is yours to rewrite — shared history is a contract."}

title: The testing pyramid
body: Unit tests check one function fast and in isolation; integration tests check components together (real database, real API calls); end-to-end tests drive the whole system like a user. The pyramid shape — many units, fewer integrations, few E2Es — balances confidence against speed and flakiness.

title: Edge case selection
body: Interviewers watch WHICH tests you propose: empty input, single element, duplicates, negatives and zero, boundary sizes, already-sorted and reverse-sorted, unicode and overflow where relevant. Enumerating edge families beats listing random examples — it shows systematic coverage thinking.

title: Test-driven mindset
body: TDD's loop — write a failing test, make it pass, refactor — matters in interviews as a MINDSET: state expected behavior on concrete examples before coding, and walk your finished code against them. Even without writing test code, that discipline visibly reduces bugs.

title: Quiz: Pyramid proportions
body: Why does the testing pyramid recommend many unit tests but few end-to-end tests?
payload: {"options": ["E2E tests are unimportant", "Unit tests are fast and precise; E2E tests are slow, flaky, and vague about failure causes", "Unit tests catch all bugs", "E2E tests cannot run in CI"], "correct": "Unit tests are fast and precise; E2E tests are slow, flaky, and vague about failure causes", "explain": "A failed unit test names the broken function; a failed E2E test names... something, somewhere. Fast, targeted feedback belongs at the base; a few E2E tests validate the wiring."}

title: Reviewing code
body: Good review feedback is specific, kind, and reasoned: this loop is quadratic on large inputs — a set membership check makes it linear, not this is bad. Distinguish blocking issues (correctness, security) from nits (style), and label them so the author can prioritize.

title: Receiving review
body: Receiving feedback well is a hiring signal Google explicitly screens for in behavioral rounds: thank the reviewer, ask clarifying questions, concede good points without ego, and push back with reasons when you disagree. Practice a story where review changed your design.

title: Review culture facts
body: Healthy review culture keeps changes small (hundreds of lines, not thousands), turnaround fast, and standards automated — linters argue about formatting so humans discuss design. Google's own practice of readability and mandatory review is worth referencing if asked why review matters.

title: Quiz: Useful feedback
body: Which review comment is most effective?
payload: {"options": ["This is wrong.", "Rewrite this.", "This lookup is O(n) per call inside a loop — a hash set here makes the whole pass linear. Thoughts?", "Nit: I would have done it differently."], "correct": "This lookup is O(n) per call inside a loop — a hash set here makes the whole pass linear. Thoughts?", "explain": "It names the specific issue, quantifies the impact, proposes a fix, and invites dialogue — actionable and respectful. The others are vague, commanding, or noise."}

title: Debugging methodology
body: Debug like a scientist: REPRODUCE reliably first, ISOLATE by shrinking the failing case, form a hypothesis, test it with one change, and VERIFY the fix plus add a regression test. Random print-and-pray edits are the anti-pattern this framework replaces.

title: Binary-searching the cause
body: Halving applies to debugging: git bisect halves commit history to find the breaking change; commenting out half a pipeline halves the suspect surface; checking a midpoint value halves the data. Say bisect out loud — interviewers recognize the discipline instantly.

title: Debugging in the interview
body: When YOUR interview code fails a trace, model good debugging: pick the smallest failing input, walk the state line by line aloud, and fix the cause — not the symptom. Staying calm and systematic on a bug is worth more than never bugging at all.

title: Quiz: First move
body: A user reports an intermittent crash. What is the FIRST step of systematic debugging?
payload: {"options": ["Patch the likeliest suspect", "Find a reliable reproduction", "Add more logging everywhere", "Rewrite the module"], "correct": "Find a reliable reproduction", "explain": "Without reproduction you cannot verify any fix or test any hypothesis — you are guessing. Reproduce, shrink, hypothesize, test, verify: in that order."}

title: Logging levels
body: Logs carry levels for a reason: DEBUG for development detail, INFO for normal milestones, WARN for survivable anomalies, ERROR for failures needing attention. Structured logs — key-value or JSON, with request IDs — turn grep archaeology into queryable data.

title: The three pillars
body: Observability's triad: LOGS (discrete events with detail), METRICS (cheap aggregates over time — latency percentiles, error rates), TRACES (one request's journey across services). Metrics tell you THAT something broke; logs and traces tell you WHY and WHERE.

title: Correlation IDs
body: Attach a request ID at the edge and propagate it through every service and log line, and one grep reconstructs a request's whole story across machines. It is the single highest-leverage observability habit and a reliable senior-sounding detail in design answers.

title: Quiz: Pillar matching
body: Which observability signal best answers: did p99 latency degrade after the deploy?
payload: {"options": ["Logs", "Metrics", "Traces", "Stack dumps"], "correct": "Metrics", "explain": "Latency percentiles over time are aggregate time-series — exactly what metrics are. A trace then explains WHERE one slow request spent its time; logs explain what happened at that spot."}

title: Scrum's skeleton
body: Scrum runs in sprints — fixed iterations, often two weeks — with four ceremonies: planning (choose sprint work), daily standup (sync and unblock), review (demo outcomes), retrospective (improve process). The artifacts: product backlog, sprint backlog, and the increment.

title: The three roles
body: Product owner owns WHAT and priority order; the Scrum master owns the PROCESS — facilitating ceremonies, removing blockers, shielding the team; developers own HOW. Note the Scrum master leads by facilitation, not authority — servant leadership is the term interviews expect.

title: Agile stories that land
body: Process experience becomes behavioral gold when told through outcomes: a retrospective change that cut carryover, a standup pattern that surfaced a blocker early, backlog triage that saved a deadline. Concrete process-improvement stories double as leadership evidence.

title: Quiz: Ceremony purpose
body: Which Scrum ceremony exists specifically to improve how the team works, not what it builds?
payload: {"options": ["Sprint planning", "Daily standup", "Sprint review", "Retrospective"], "correct": "Retrospective", "explain": "Review inspects the PRODUCT with stakeholders; the retrospective inspects the PROCESS — what went well, what hurt, what to change next sprint. Confusing the two is the classic slip."}

title: CI
body: Continuous integration merges everyone's work to main frequently, with every push building and running tests automatically. The payoff is shrinking integration risk: conflicts and breakage surface in hours, not at a monthly merge apocalypse. A red build blocks the merge — that is the contract.

title: CD
body: Continuous delivery keeps main always deployable with push-button releases; continuous deployment goes further — every green build ships automatically. Safe versions lean on staged rollouts, canaries (a slice of traffic first), feature flags, and instant rollback.

title: Why pipelines matter
body: The interview framing: CI/CD converts releases from feared events into boring routine, which means smaller changes, faster feedback, and quicker recovery when something slips through. If you have set up even a simple GitHub Actions pipeline, that story is worth telling.

title: Quiz: CI's core practice
body: What does continuous integration require on every push?
payload: {"options": ["Manual QA signoff", "An automated build and test run gating the merge", "Deployment to production", "A full release meeting"], "correct": "An automated build and test run gating the merge", "explain": "The automation is the definition: integrate often, verify automatically, block on red. Deploying every green build is continuous DEPLOYMENT — one step further down the pipeline."}

title: Naming is design
body: Clean code starts with names that say intent: elapsed_days beats d, is_eligible beats flag2. Functions should be small, do one thing, and take few parameters. In interviews, decent names cost seconds and buy the interviewer's comprehension — a trade you always take.

title: DRY, with judgment
body: Do not Repeat Yourself targets duplicated KNOWLEDGE — one rule encoded twice will diverge. But premature abstraction of coincidental similarity is its own disease; a little duplication beats the wrong abstraction. Stating both halves shows maturity, not slogan-recital.

title: Clean enough under pressure
body: Interview calibration: helper functions where they clarify, guard clauses over nested ifs, constants over magic numbers — but do not gold-plate. Narrate the debt instead: I would extract this and add validation in production. Judgment displayed beats perfection attempted.

title: Quiz: DRY's real target
body: What does the DRY principle actually warn against duplicating?
payload: {"options": ["Any similar-looking lines", "Knowledge — a business rule or fact encoded in multiple places", "Test code", "Comments"], "correct": "Knowledge — a business rule or fact encoded in multiple places", "explain": "Two copies of one rule WILL drift apart. Coincidentally similar code serving different rules may rightly stay separate — merging it creates the wrong abstraction."}

title: APIs are contracts
body: An API is a promise to callers: endpoints, parameters, types, error shapes. Once published, breaking it breaks THEM — so design deliberately: consistent naming, predictable errors, explicit types, and documentation of behavior including edge cases.

title: Versioning and compatibility
body: Backward-compatible changes — adding optional fields, new endpoints — ship freely. Breaking changes — renaming, removing, retyping — demand a version (v1, v2 in the path or header) and a deprecation window. The additive-changes-are-safe rule is the heart of API evolution.

title: Designing for the future
body: Two habits that age well: return objects rather than bare scalars (room to add fields), and make clients tolerate unknown fields. When an interviewer extends a design question — now add pagination — these habits make the extension a diff instead of a rewrite.

title: Quiz: Safe evolution
body: Which API change is backward-compatible?
payload: {"options": ["Renaming user_id to userId", "Adding a new optional field to the response", "Changing price from cents to dollars", "Removing a deprecated field immediately"], "correct": "Adding a new optional field to the response", "explain": "Additions do not break existing parsers written to tolerate unknown fields. Renames, semantic changes, and removals all break callers and require versioning plus deprecation."}

title: READMEs that work
body: A working README answers four questions fast: what this is, how to run it, how to test it, and how it is structured. The audience is a stranger — or you in six months. Judging by setup friction: if onboarding needs a person, the README failed.

title: Comments explain why
body: Code shows WHAT; comments earn their place explaining WHY: the constraint, the workaround, the link to the incident or spec. Comments restating the code rot into lies as code changes. Self-documenting names first, why-comments second, what-comments never.

title: Docs as interview evidence
body: Your projects ARE your portfolio, and their READMEs are the landing pages interviewers actually open. A crisp README with a demo GIF, run instructions, and an architecture sketch outperforms a bigger project that will not build. Polish the docs before the interview, not after.

title: Quiz: Comment worth keeping
body: Which comment provides lasting value?
payload: {"options": ["// increment i", "// loop over users", "// Retry 3 times: upstream flakes under load, see incident #482", "// TODO fix later"], "correct": "// Retry 3 times: upstream flakes under load, see incident #482", "explain": "It records WHY the code is unusual, with a pointer to evidence — knowledge the code itself cannot express. The others restate the obvious or defer without context."}

title: Client-server and the monolith question
body: Client-server splits presentation from logic and data behind an API. A monolith ships all server logic as one deployable — simple to build, test, and debug; microservices split by capability — independent scaling and deploys, at the price of network complexity and distributed failure modes.

title: Start monolith, split on pain
body: The calibrated intern answer: start with a monolith; extract services when specific pain justifies it — one component needs independent scaling, a team needs deploy autonomy, or a hotspot needs isolation. Reciting microservices-by-default reads as cargo culting, not judgment.

title: The costs people forget
body: Every service boundary converts a function call into a network call: latency, partial failure, retries, serialization, and observability overhead. Naming ONE concrete cost — a checkout that now spans four services needs distributed tracing to debug — grounds the trade-off in reality.

title: Quiz: Splitting justification
body: Which situation most justifies extracting a microservice from a monolith?
payload: {"options": ["The codebase feels large", "One component needs to scale independently of the rest", "Microservices are modern", "The team wants to try Kubernetes"], "correct": "One component needs to scale independently of the rest", "explain": "Independent scaling, deploy cadence, or fault isolation are concrete needs services solve. Size alone is solved by modularity within the monolith — extraction should answer a specific pain."}

title: Load balancers
body: A load balancer spreads requests across server replicas, health-checks them, and routes around failures — the front door of every horizontally scaled design. Strategies: round robin (rotate), least connections (send to the least busy), and hashing for stickiness when needed.

title: Beyond distribution
body: Load balancers also terminate TLS, enable zero-downtime deploys (drain one replica, upgrade, rejoin), and absorb replica failure invisibly. In a design diagram, the LB is what makes replicas count matter — draw it before drawing three app boxes.

title: Stickiness and its smell
body: Session stickiness pins a client to one server — needed only when servers hold local state. The stronger design keeps services stateless (state in a shared store) so ANY server handles ANY request; stickiness is usually a symptom worth designing away.

title: Quiz: Least connections
body: When does least-connections routing beat round robin?
payload: {"options": ["Never — round robin is optimal", "When request costs vary widely, so live load beats blind rotation", "When there is one server", "When requests are all identical"], "correct": "When request costs vary widely, so live load beats blind rotation", "explain": "Round robin assumes equal work per request; heavy requests pile onto unlucky servers. Least connections uses current load as a proxy for actual busyness — better under variance."}

title: Vertical vs horizontal
body: Vertical scaling buys a bigger machine — zero code changes, but a hardware ceiling and a single point of failure. Horizontal scaling adds machines — near-unbounded, fault-tolerant, and the reason statelessness, load balancing, and data partitioning exist as topics.

title: What horizontal demands
body: Adding servers only helps if requests can go anywhere: state must move to shared stores, sessions to Redis or tokens, files to object storage, and the database becomes the next bottleneck — which is where replication and sharding enter. Horizontal scaling is a lifestyle, not a checkbox.

title: Scale the bottleneck
body: The interview discipline: identify WHICH component saturates first — app CPU, database IOPS, bandwidth — and scale THAT. Scaling everything 10x because traffic grew 10x wastes money and misses the point; measurements before machines.

title: Quiz: Horizontal prerequisite
body: What property must application servers have for horizontal scaling behind a load balancer to work cleanly?
payload: {"options": ["Identical hardware", "Statelessness — any server can serve any request", "Shared CPU caches", "A single writer thread"], "correct": "Statelessness — any server can serve any request", "explain": "If a server holds session state locally, requests must return to it, defeating free distribution and failover. Externalize state and replicas become interchangeable."}

title: Cache placement layers
body: Caching happens at every layer: browser (HTTP cache headers), CDN (static assets at the edge), application (Redis or Memcached for hot objects), and database (query and page caches). Saying WHERE you cache and WHAT lives at each layer structures the whole conversation.

title: Cache-aside and eviction
body: Cache-aside is the default pattern: check the cache, miss to the database, write the result back with a TTL. Eviction under memory pressure follows LRU (recency), LFU (frequency), or TTL expiry. Know the trio and default to LRU with a TTL safety net.

title: Invalidation, the hard part
body: The two honest strategies: TTLs bound staleness cheaply; explicit invalidation on write is precise but easy to miss a path. Also name the stampede problem — a hot key expiring floods the database — mitigated by jittered TTLs or single-flight recomputation. Depth here is rare and rewarded.

title: Quiz: Cache-aside flow
body: In cache-aside, what happens on a cache miss?
payload: {"options": ["Return an error", "The application reads the database and populates the cache", "The cache queries the database itself", "The request is dropped"], "correct": "The application reads the database and populates the cache", "explain": "The application owns the flow: miss, fetch from source of truth, write into the cache with a TTL, return. The cache stays a passive key-value store — which is what makes the pattern simple."}

title: Replication
body: Replication copies data across nodes: a primary takes writes, replicas serve reads — multiplying read throughput and surviving node loss. Asynchronous replication introduces LAG: read-your-own-write anomalies where a user's fresh post is missing from a stale replica.

title: Sharding
body: Sharding PARTITIONS data across nodes — each holds a slice, keyed by range or hash of a shard key. It scales writes and storage where replication cannot, but cross-shard queries and transactions get hard, and a bad shard key creates hot spots (all celebrity traffic on one shard).

title: Replication then sharding
body: The escalation to narrate: reads saturate — add replicas and a cache; writes or data size saturate — shard. Replication copies EVERYTHING everywhere; sharding puts DIFFERENT data different places; real systems do both. That one distinction cleanly answers most follow-ups.

title: Quiz: Which problem does sharding solve
body: Replication alone cannot fix which bottleneck?
payload: {"options": ["Read throughput", "Write throughput and total data size", "Replica failure", "Slow queries on small tables"], "correct": "Write throughput and total data size", "explain": "Every replica still applies every write and stores everything. Partitioning across shards divides the write load and the dataset — the problems replication structurally cannot touch."}

title: Message queues
body: A queue decouples producers from consumers: the web request enqueues a job — send email, resize image — and returns fast, while workers process asynchronously at their own pace. The queue absorbs traffic spikes as backlog instead of dropped requests.

title: Delivery semantics
body: Queues typically promise at-least-once delivery: a worker crash after processing but before acknowledging causes redelivery — so handlers must be IDEMPOTENT. Also know the dead-letter queue: where messages that repeatedly fail go for inspection instead of poisoning the stream.

title: When to reach for one
body: The trigger phrases: work that can happen LATER (email, thumbnails, reports), spiky load meeting fixed capacity, or fan-out to multiple consumers (pub-sub). If the user does not need the result in the response, a queue belongs in the diagram.

title: Quiz: Idempotent handlers
body: Why must queue consumers be idempotent under at-least-once delivery?
payload: {"options": ["Queues corrupt messages", "Redelivery after a crash means the same message may be processed twice", "It improves throughput", "Idempotency compresses messages"], "correct": "Redelivery after a crash means the same message may be processed twice", "explain": "An unacknowledged message returns to the queue even if its work completed. Processing must be repeat-safe — deduplication keys or naturally idempotent operations — or duplicates cause damage."}

title: Token bucket
body: Token bucket rate limiting: tokens drip in at the sustained rate, each request spends one, and the bucket's capacity permits short BURSTS above the rate. Two parameters — refill rate and bucket size — and O(1) state per client. It is the default answer for API limits.

title: Window counters
body: Fixed-window counting is simplest but leaks at boundaries — a burst straddling the minute mark doubles the intended limit. Sliding-window approaches (log of timestamps, or weighted adjacent windows) smooth the edge at slightly higher cost. Name the boundary flaw; it is the discriminating detail.

title: Limiting in distributed reality
body: Across many servers, per-node counters undercount globally — a shared store like Redis with atomic increments centralizes the count, trading a network hop per request. Also state the rejection behavior: HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header. Small details, complete answer.

title: Quiz: Burst tolerance
body: Which rate-limiting algorithm explicitly allows short bursts while enforcing a long-run rate?
payload: {"options": ["Fixed window", "Token bucket", "Rejecting all spikes", "Round robin"], "correct": "Token bucket", "explain": "Accumulated tokens (up to bucket capacity) let a quiet client burst briefly; the refill rate caps sustained throughput. Fixed windows have boundary leakage instead of principled burst handling."}

title: Consistent hashing
body: Naive hash-mod-N routing reshuffles nearly ALL keys when a node joins or leaves. Consistent hashing places nodes and keys on a ring; each key belongs to the next node clockwise, so membership changes move only about 1/N of keys. Awareness tier — the ring sentence is the answer.

title: Virtual nodes
body: Real rings give each physical node many VIRTUAL positions, smoothing load imbalance from unlucky ring placement and letting heterogeneous machines take proportional shares. One sentence on virtual nodes upgrades the answer from textbook to practiced.

title: Where the ring lives
body: Name-drop accurately: distributed caches (Memcached clients), Dynamo-style stores (Cassandra, DynamoDB), and load balancers with sticky routing all use consistent hashing to survive membership churn. It appears in interviews wherever cache all this across N servers does.

title: Quiz: The ring's win
body: What is consistent hashing's key advantage over hash-mod-N node assignment?
payload: {"options": ["Faster hashing", "Adding or removing a node remaps only about 1/N of keys instead of nearly all", "It encrypts keys", "It needs no hash function"], "correct": "Adding or removing a node remaps only about 1/N of keys instead of nearly all", "explain": "Mod-N changes the modulus and scrambles everything — a cache-wipe event. On the ring, only keys between the affected node and its neighbor move — churn becomes survivable."}

title: Latency vs throughput
body: Latency is one request's duration; throughput is requests completed per second. They are not reciprocal: batching RAISES throughput while raising latency; a system can be fast per request yet low capacity. Percentiles matter more than averages — p99 is where users suffer.

title: Numbers to carry
body: Rough magnitudes for estimation: memory access ~100 nanoseconds, SSD read ~100 microseconds, same-region network round trip ~1 millisecond, cross-continent ~100 milliseconds. A day is about 86400 seconds — so 1 million requests per day is a mere ~12 per second.

title: Back-of-envelope discipline
body: Estimation questions grade the METHOD: state assumptions (users, actions per user, object sizes), multiply with round numbers, sanity-check the result against known scales. Ten million users posting 100-byte messages daily is ~1 GB per day — storage is trivial, so the bottleneck is elsewhere. That conclusion is the point.

title: Quiz: Daily to per-second
body: About how many requests per second is 10 million requests per day?
payload: {"options": ["~115", "~1150", "~10000", "~12"], "correct": "~115", "explain": "A day is roughly 86400 seconds — call it 100 thousand for arithmetic. 10 million over 100 thousand is about 100-115 per second. Daily numbers always sound scarier than their per-second truth."}

title: Timeouts and retries
body: Reliability's first tools: every remote call gets a TIMEOUT (fail fast instead of hanging) and judicious RETRIES with exponential backoff plus jitter — immediate synchronized retries create thundering herds that finish off a struggling service.

title: Idempotency keys
body: Retries are only safe when repeat execution is harmless. For payments and orders, clients send an IDEMPOTENCY KEY; the server stores results per key and returns the cached outcome on duplicates — charge once, no matter how many retries. This is the canonical reliability interview detail.

title: Failing gracefully
body: Two more named tools: circuit breakers stop calling a failing dependency for a cooldown (protecting both sides), and graceful degradation serves partial functionality — cached recommendations, disabled comments — instead of a hard error. Systems fail; design chooses HOW.

title: Quiz: Charging exactly once
body: A payment request times out and the client retries. What prevents a double charge?
payload: {"options": ["Retrying more slowly", "An idempotency key the server deduplicates on", "TCP guarantees delivery", "Shorter timeouts"], "correct": "An idempotency key the server deduplicates on", "explain": "The timeout hides whether the first attempt succeeded. A unique key per logical operation lets the server recognize the retry and return the original result instead of charging again."}

title: Stateless services
body: A stateless service keeps no request-spanning state in process memory — every request is self-contained, state lives in databases, caches, or tokens. The payoff: any replica serves any request, so scaling is adding boxes and failure is invisible.

title: Stateful realities
body: Some things are irreducibly stateful — databases, WebSocket connections, game sessions, caches. The design move is CONCENTRATING state into purpose-built stateful tiers with their own replication story, keeping the app tier stateless above them.

title: The interview refrain
body: Push state down is the phrase: sessions to Redis or JWTs, files to object storage, counters to the database. When an interviewer asks how your design handles a server dying mid-session, the stateless answer — nothing is lost, the LB reroutes — is the whole point.

title: Quiz: The dead server test
body: In a well-designed stateless app tier, what happens to a user's session when their server instance dies?
payload: {"options": ["The session is lost", "Nothing — session state lives in a shared store, any replica continues serving", "The user must log in again", "The load balancer replays history"], "correct": "Nothing — session state lives in a shared store, any replica continues serving", "explain": "Statelessness means the instance held nothing unique; the next request routes elsewhere and reads the same external state. This resilience is the argument for pushing state down."}

title: URL shortener anatomy
body: The URL shortener is the hello world of design: an API to create (POST long URL, return short code) and redirect (GET code, 301 to target), a key-value-shaped table of code to URL, and a code-generation strategy. Start with the API and data model, not the scaling.

title: Generating short codes
body: Two standard strategies: base62-encode an auto-incrementing ID (short, collision-free, but sequential and guessable) or hash the URL and take a prefix (handle collisions by probing). A 7-character base62 code covers 62 to the 7th — about 3.5 trillion URLs.

title: The read-heavy insight
body: Shorteners are wildly read-heavy — redirects outnumber creations by orders of magnitude — so the design centerpiece is a cache in front of the store and CDN-friendly redirects. Also mention 301 versus 302: permanent redirects cache in browsers, which helps traffic but hides analytics.

title: Quiz: Shortener capacity
body: Roughly how many distinct URLs can 7 base62 characters address?
payload: {"options": ["About 3.5 million", "About 3.5 trillion", "About 62 thousand", "Exactly 7 billion"], "correct": "About 3.5 trillion", "explain": "62 to the 7th power is about 3.5 times 10 to the 12th — comfortably more than any realistic URL volume, which is why 6-8 character codes are the industry norm."}

title: Rate limiter design
body: Design a rate limiter assembles known parts: choose the algorithm (token bucket for burst tolerance), the state store (in-memory per node, or Redis for a global limit), and the response contract (429 plus Retry-After). Clarify FIRST what is limited — per user, per IP, per API key — and at what granularity.

title: The atomicity detail
body: The distributed version's crux is read-modify-write races on the counter: two servers both read 99 of 100 and both admit. Redis answers with atomic INCR or a Lua script doing check-and-decrement in one step. Naming the race and its atomic fix is the senior move.

title: Failure posture
body: State the fail-open versus fail-closed decision: if the limiter's store is down, do you admit traffic (availability, risk of abuse) or reject it (safety, self-inflicted outage)? There is no universal answer — articulating the trade-off IS the answer.

title: Quiz: The limiter race
body: Two app servers simultaneously check a shared counter at 99 of 100 and both admit a request. What fixes this?
payload: {"options": ["Longer TTLs", "An atomic increment-and-check operation in the shared store", "More servers", "Client-side limiting"], "correct": "An atomic increment-and-check operation in the shared store", "explain": "The check and the update must be one indivisible operation — Redis INCR or a Lua script. Separate read-then-write steps always leave a race window."}

title: OO design questions
body: Parking lot, library, vending machine: these test object modeling, not scale. The protocol — extract nouns as candidate classes (Lot, Level, Spot, Vehicle, Ticket), verbs as methods (park, unpark), then walk one scenario end to end assigning responsibilities.

title: Encoding the rules
body: The interesting decisions are where RULES live: spot-vehicle compatibility (a motorcycle fits anywhere, a bus needs five consecutive large spots) belongs in a SpotAssignment strategy, pricing in a Fee calculator — swappable pieces, not if-else jungles inside ParkingLot.

title: Design for the follow-up
body: OO interviews escalate: now add electric charging spots, now add reservations. Interfaces and enums you can EXTEND without editing existing logic — the open-closed principle in action — are what make the follow-up a two-minute addition instead of a rewrite.

title: Quiz: Where pricing lives
body: In parking lot OO design, why put fee calculation in its own strategy class instead of inside ParkingLot?
payload: {"options": ["It runs faster", "Pricing rules change independently — swap strategies without touching lot logic", "ParkingLot cannot do math", "Interviewers require five classes"], "correct": "Pricing rules change independently — swap strategies without touching lot logic", "explain": "Weekend rates, EV surcharges, validation discounts — pricing churns. Isolating it behind an interface makes the inevitable now change the pricing follow-up trivial: open-closed in practice."}

title: Hit counter design
body: Count hits in the last 5 minutes: a queue of timestamps popped when stale is exact but memory grows with traffic; a circular buffer of 300 per-second counters is O(1) memory with one-second granularity. Present both, state the exactness-versus-memory trade.

title: Scaling the counter
body: At high volume, per-node counters aggregate periodically into a store — accepting slight staleness for throughput — and metrics systems go further: pre-aggregated buckets per time window, percentile sketches instead of raw events. Exact-versus-approximate is the recurring axis.

title: The generalization
body: Hit counters generalize into metrics aggregators: counters, gauges, histograms, emitted per window and rolled up over time. If asked to extend, mention downsampling old data (per-second recent, per-hour historical) — the storage insight behind every real metrics system.

title: Quiz: Buffer vs queue
body: For a 5-minute hit counter, what does the 300-slot circular buffer trade away versus a timestamp queue?
payload: {"options": ["Memory bounds", "Exact per-hit precision — counts bucket to one-second granularity", "Thread safety", "The ability to count at all"], "correct": "Exact per-hit precision — counts bucket to one-second granularity", "explain": "The buffer caps memory at 300 integers regardless of traffic but rounds events into second buckets. The queue is exact and unbounded. Naming this trade is the entire question."}

title: Chat flow design
body: A minimal chat: clients hold WebSocket connections to chat servers, messages persist to storage, then fan out to recipients' connections; offline users get the messages on reconnect plus a push notification. Draw send-path and receive-path separately — it keeps the story clean.

title: The routing problem
body: With many chat servers, the sender's server must find the recipient's: a registry mapping user to server (in Redis), or pub-sub where each server subscribes to its users' channels. This who-holds-the-connection problem is the intellectual core of the question.

title: Ordering and delivery
body: Mention per-conversation ordering (sequence numbers or single-writer per conversation), delivery acknowledgments powering sent-delivered-read states, and idempotent message IDs so retries do not duplicate. Three sentences that convert a sketch into a design.

title: Quiz: Finding the recipient
body: In multi-server chat, server A holds the sender and server B holds the recipient. How does the message reach B?
payload: {"options": ["Broadcast to every server", "A registry or pub-sub layer maps the recipient to server B", "The client polls all servers", "It cannot be done"], "correct": "A registry or pub-sub layer maps the recipient to server B", "explain": "Either look up the recipient's connection host in a shared registry and forward, or publish to the recipient's channel that B subscribes to. Broadcast works at toy scale and dies quickly after."}

title: File pipeline shape
body: A file-processing pipeline: ingest (upload or watch a source), enqueue a job per file, workers parse-transform-validate, results land in storage, failures land in a dead-letter queue with alerting. The queue between stages is what makes it scalable and crash-tolerant.

title: Idempotent reprocessing
body: Design every stage to be safely re-runnable: name outputs deterministically per input, upsert rather than insert, checkpoint progress. When — not if — a worker dies mid-file, redelivery just works. Idempotency is the difference between a pipeline and a pile of scripts.

title: Grounding it in experience
body: If you have automated document processing — XML, PDFs, reports — USE that in this answer: real pain points (malformed inputs, partial failures, schema drift) and how you handled them beat textbook diagrams. Interviewers upgrade abstract answers that connect to shipped work.

title: Quiz: The poison file
body: One malformed file crashes its worker repeatedly on redelivery. What design element contains the damage?
payload: {"options": ["Bigger workers", "A dead-letter queue after N failed attempts, with alerting", "Deleting the file silently", "Processing files in one thread"], "correct": "A dead-letter queue after N failed attempts, with alerting", "explain": "Retry limits plus a DLQ quarantine the poison message so the pipeline keeps flowing, while alerting ensures a human investigates. Silent deletion loses data; infinite retries block the queue."}

title: Autocomplete's engine
body: Typeahead is the trie's showcase: descend to the typed prefix, return the top suggestions below it. The optimization that makes it real: cache the top-k completions AT each node during construction, so queries stop descending and read precomputed answers — O(prefix length).

title: Ranking and freshness
body: Suggestions rank by score — frequency, recency, personalization. Updating scores on every keystroke query is too hot; real designs batch-aggregate search logs and rebuild or patch the trie periodically. Separate the serve path (read-only, fast) from the update path (offline, batch).

title: Scale and latency
body: Autocomplete budgets ~100 milliseconds per keystroke: serve from memory, shard the trie by prefix range if it outgrows one machine, and debounce on the client. This question chains data structure, ranking, and systems — practice narrating all three layers in order.

title: Quiz: Fast suggestions
body: What precomputation makes autocomplete queries O(prefix length) instead of O(subtree size)?
payload: {"options": ["Sorting the dictionary", "Storing the top-k completions at every trie node", "Hashing each prefix", "Binary search on words"], "correct": "Storing the top-k completions at every trie node", "explain": "Without caching, each query walks the prefix's entire subtree to collect and rank candidates. With per-node top-k lists, the query descends the prefix and reads the answer — subtree exploration eliminated."}

title: News feed, the two strategies
body: Feed design's central axis: fan-out on WRITE pushes each new post into followers' precomputed feed lists (fast reads, heavy writes) versus fan-out on READ assembling the feed per request (cheap writes, expensive reads). Awareness tier — the axis is the answer.

title: The celebrity problem
body: Fan-out on write breaks on accounts with millions of followers — one post triggers millions of writes. Real systems hybridize: push for normal users, pull for celebrities, merging at read time. Naming the celebrity problem is the recognized shibboleth for this question.

title: Feed answer calibration
body: At intern level, a complete answer: the two fan-out strategies, the hybrid, feeds cached in Redis as sorted lists, ranking as a swappable scoring step. Do not descend into ML ranking — breadth over depth matches the awareness-level ask.

title: Quiz: The celebrity post
body: Why does pure fan-out-on-write fail for an account with 50 million followers?
payload: {"options": ["Followers cannot read that fast", "One post triggers 50 million feed writes — a write amplification explosion", "Timelines cannot store posts", "It violates CAP"], "correct": "One post triggers 50 million feed writes — a write amplification explosion", "explain": "Precomputing every follower's feed multiplies each celebrity post into tens of millions of operations. The hybrid keeps push for normal accounts and pulls celebrity posts at read time."}

title: Scaling your project 100x
