payload: {"options": ["Apply it silently", "Ignore it and merge", "Explain your reasoning and ask whether their concern still applies", "Escalate to the manager immediately"], "correct": "Explain your reasoning and ask whether their concern still applies", "explain": "Reasoned dialogue resolves most disagreements at the clarification step — often one side lacked context. Silent compliance loses real bugs; silent merging breaks the review contract."}

title: Estimating the unknown
body: Scenario: asked to estimate work you have never done. Decompose into pieces, estimate each with explicit confidence, and pad the unknowns — then say the assumptions with the number: two weeks IF the API behaves as documented; the auth part is my biggest unknown.

title: Spike first
body: The professional move for deep uncertainty: a timeboxed SPIKE — one day of investigation on the riskiest unknown — before committing an estimate. Buying information before promising dates converts wild guesses into ranges, and teams universally prefer the day's delay to the blown estimate.

title: Ranges beat points
body: Communicate estimates as ranges tied to unknowns — three days if X holds, up to eight if not — and update the moment an assumption resolves. A revised estimate early is professionalism; the same revision late is a slip. The estimate is a living document, not a vow.

title: Quiz: The un-estimatable task
body: Asked to estimate a task full of unknowns, what is the strongest professional move?
payload: {"options": ["Refuse to estimate", "Give a confident single number", "Timebox a spike on the riskiest unknown, then estimate as a range with stated assumptions", "Double someone else's guess"], "correct": "Timebox a spike on the riskiest unknown, then estimate as a range with stated assumptions", "explain": "Information first, then a range bound to explicit assumptions. It demonstrates that you treat estimation as engineering — reducible uncertainty — rather than fortune telling."}

title: Own every resume line
body: Resume deep-dives probe until they hit the bottom of your knowledge: every project on the page must survive why did you choose that stack, what was the hardest bug, and what would you rebuild differently. If you cannot defend a line at depth, cut it before the interview does.

title: The architecture narration
body: For each flagship project, rehearse a 90-second architecture tour: the components, the data flow, YOUR specific decisions, and one decision you would revisit. Practice drawing it in under a minute — deep-dives frequently move to a whiteboard or shared doc without warning.

title: Decisions, not features
body: Interviewers grade decision quality, not feature lists: why THIS framework over the default, why a rewrite over a patch, what alternative you rejected and on what evidence. For every project, prepare the two or three forks in the road and your reasoning at each.

title: Quiz: The deep-dive rubric
body: In a resume deep-dive, what are interviewers primarily grading?
payload: {"options": ["The number of technologies listed", "The quality of your decisions and depth of ownership on each project", "Project age", "Whether the projects are famous"], "correct": "The quality of your decisions and depth of ownership on each project", "explain": "Anyone can list a stack. Defending choices — alternatives considered, trade-offs weighed, evidence used — is what distinguishes genuine ownership from proximity to a project."}

title: The dollar-result story
body: A quantified business result — an analysis that found six figures, an automation that saved a process — is resume gold, but expect the audit: how the number was computed, what you validated, and who accepted it. Rehearse the calculation's story as carefully as the code's.

title: Defending the number
body: For any claimed metric, prepare three layers: the method (how measured or estimated), the validation (what you cross-checked), and the reception (who acted on it). A number that survives three follow-ups builds enormous credibility; one that wobbles damages the whole resume.

title: Technical work, business frame
body: Data and analysis stories should travel both registers: the SQL or pipeline mechanics for engineers, and the business meaning for everyone else. Practicing the same story at both altitudes prepares you for whichever interviewer type you draw.

title: Quiz: The audited metric
body: You claim an analysis saved a large sum. Which preparation matters most?
payload: {"options": ["Rounding the number up", "Being able to explain how it was computed, validated, and received", "Avoiding specifics", "Citing the tool used"], "correct": "Being able to explain how it was computed, validated, and received", "explain": "Quantified claims invite audits. Method, validation, and reception are the three follow-ups that always come — surviving them converts a bullet point into proof of judgment."}

title: The rewrite story
body: A rewrite or migration project — one language or system replaced by another — is a premium deep-dive target: why the change was justified (not resume-driven development), what the old system did right, and what the transition cost. Defend the decision like an engineer, not an enthusiast.

title: Language-choice defenses
body: If you chose a distinctive language (Rust over C, Go over Python), prepare the honest comparison: the specific property that mattered — memory safety for byte-level parsing, deployment simplicity — plus the cost you paid, like a steep learning curve. The admitted cost is what makes the choice credible.

title: The learning-curve arc
body: Rewrites in unfamiliar technology double as learning stories: name the concept that fought you (ownership and borrowing, async models), how long it took to click, and the moment it paid off. That arc feeds the learning-speed behavioral question from technical evidence.

title: Quiz: Justifying a rewrite
body: Which justification for a rewrite reads strongest in a deep-dive?
payload: {"options": ["The new language was more exciting", "A specific property of the new stack solved a documented pain of the old one, and the transition cost was weighed", "The old code was not mine", "A blog recommended it"], "correct": "A specific property of the new stack solved a documented pain of the old one, and the transition cost was weighed", "explain": "Rewrites are expensive and often wrong; the credible defense names the concrete failing, the property that fixes it, and the acknowledged cost. Enthusiasm-driven rewrites are a known anti-pattern interviewers probe for."}

title: Team project leadership
body: Team projects with a process role — Scrum Master, coordinator, integrator — are behavioral goldmines: describe the day-to-day mechanics (running standups, clearing blockers, negotiating scope) and one process change you made with its measurable effect on the team.

title: API integration stories
body: Projects wiring external APIs (fitness platforms, LLM services, payment providers) invite specific probes: auth flow, rate limits and failure handling, data mapping, and what you did when the API misbehaved. The misbehavior story is where the real engineering shows.

title: Course projects count double
body: School team projects are fully legitimate deep-dive material when you own real decisions in them — often MORE useful than solo work because they carry teamwork, conflict, and process stories in the same package. Prepare them to the same depth as internship work.

title: Quiz: The integration probe
body: When you list an external API integration, which follow-up should you most expect?
payload: {"options": ["The API's pricing", "How you handled its failures — rate limits, outages, malformed responses", "The company's founding year", "Whether you memorized every endpoint"], "correct": "How you handled its failures — rate limits, outages, malformed responses", "explain": "Happy-path integration is tutorial work; failure handling is engineering. Retries, backoff, fallbacks, and validation are where the deep-dive tests whether you built for reality."}

title: Long-arc commitments
body: Multi-year commitments — a robotics team led for years, a coaching role sustained across seasons — evidence something projects cannot: durability. Structure the story as an arc: year one contributor, year two owner, year three teacher. Growth across the arc IS the answer.

title: Hardware-adjacent credibility
body: Robotics and embedded work carry distinctive technical texture: sensor noise, real-time constraints, debugging with limited observability. One concrete story — a sensor integration that fought back — differentiates you from purely web-and-scripts resumes.

title: Teaching as mastery evidence
body: If your long-arc role included training others — new team members, younger students — foreground it: designing onboarding, watching your explanations fail and improve. Teaching stories certify both mastery and communication in one beat, and interviewers weight them heavily.

title: Quiz: The multi-year story
body: What should structure the telling of a 3-year commitment like leading a robotics team?
payload: {"options": ["The final trophy only", "The growth arc: contributor to owner to teacher, with what changed at each stage", "A list of parts used", "The team's full roster"], "correct": "The growth arc: contributor to owner to teacher, with what changed at each stage", "explain": "Duration alone proves attendance; the arc proves development. Increasing scope and responsibility across years is the evidence long commitments uniquely provide."}

title: The hardest-bug drill
body: For EVERY resume project, pre-load the hardest-bug story: symptom, the wrong theories first (honesty makes it real), the diagnostic step that cracked it, the fix, and the prevention. Debugging narratives are the single most-asked deep-dive follow-up — never improvise them.

title: What would you rebuild
body: The what would you do differently question tests growth since the project: name a real regret — a data model that fought every feature, a skipped test layer that cost a weekend — and the principle you extracted. No regrets is a non-answer; a specific one is evidence of taste developing.

title: Trade-offs, defended calmly
body: Expect adversarial-sounding probes: why not just use X, was Y overkill? These test composure and reasoning, not correctness — defend with the constraints you faced at the time, concede where the critique lands, and never get defensive. The tone of the defense is half the grade.

title: Quiz: The regret question
body: Asked what you would rebuild differently in a past project, which answer is strongest?
payload: {"options": ["Nothing — it was solid", "A specific design regret, its concrete cost, and the principle you now apply", "Everything — it was terrible", "A vague mention of more testing"], "correct": "A specific design regret, its concrete cost, and the principle you now apply", "explain": "The question measures growth. A named regret with its price and the extracted lesson shows developing judgment; no regrets reads as no reflection, and everything reads as no ownership."}

title: Questions about the work
body: Your questions-for-them should surface what interns actually experience: what does an intern own end to end here, what does a typical first month look like, how is intern work scoped and reviewed. These questions get useful answers AND signal you think about delivering.

title: Two prepared, one live
body: Bring two prepared questions and generate one from the conversation itself — earlier you mentioned the migration; what makes it hard? The live question proves you listened all session, which lands stronger than any prepared cleverness.

title: Questions reveal you
body: Interviewers read your questions as preferences: asking about code review culture says you care about craft; asking about intern ownership says you want responsibility. Choose questions whose SIGNAL matches what you want them writing in the feedback form after you leave.

title: Quiz: The strongest closer
body: Which closing question tends to land best?
payload: {"options": ["What does your company do?", "One that builds on something the interviewer said earlier in this session", "How much vacation do interns get?", "No questions, to save time"], "correct": "One that builds on something the interviewer said earlier in this session", "explain": "A question rooted in the live conversation proves attention and genuine engagement — unpreparable and therefore credible. Googleable and logistics questions waste the slot."}

title: The success question
body: What separated your best past interns from the rest is the highest-yield question available: it extracts the team's actual evaluation criteria — which you can then act on from day one — and every interviewer has a ready, revealing answer. Ask it in some form every time.

title: Mining the answer
body: Listen actively to the answer: if they say the best interns asked for feedback early or shipped small and often, you have been handed the rubric. Reference it later in host matching or follow-ups — you mentioned X; here is how I already work that way.

title: Variants of the question
body: Rotations of the same probe: what do interns typically struggle with here, what would a great first month look like, how does the team decide an internship was a success. Each yields the same gold — expectations, stated plainly, before you start.

title: Quiz: The rubric extractor
body: Why is what made your best interns successful such a high-value question?
payload: {"options": ["It flatters the interviewer", "It extracts the team's real evaluation criteria, which you can act on from day one", "It runs out the clock", "It is unexpected"], "correct": "It extracts the team's real evaluation criteria, which you can act on from day one", "explain": "The answer is literally the rubric you will be graded by. Few questions convert one minute of interview time into more actionable advantage."}

title: Culture questions that work
body: Useful culture probes are concrete: how does the team make technical decisions when people disagree, what does code review look like here, how do design docs work. Concrete process questions get honest answers; abstract culture questions get brochures.

title: The decision question
body: How are technical decisions made is quietly diagnostic: the answer reveals whether the team runs on design docs, debate, seniority, or data — and whether you would thrive there. It also signals you have worked places where decision-making mattered, which is itself a maturity marker.

title: Ask what they like
body: One personal question humanizes the close: what keeps you on this team, or what has surprised you here. People enjoy answering it, it produces real information about retention and mood, and it ends the session warm — a deliberate final impression.

title: Quiz: Brochure vs honest
body: Which phrasing gets a more honest answer about team culture?
payload: {"options": ["Is the culture good?", "How does the team resolve technical disagreements — docs, debate, data, or seniority?", "Do people like it here?", "Is it fast-paced?"], "correct": "How does the team resolve technical disagreements — docs, debate, data, or seniority?", "explain": "Concrete process questions force concrete answers; abstract culture questions trigger rehearsed positives. The disagreement question also reveals exactly the dynamics you would live inside."}

title: Questions to avoid
body: Skip three categories in interviews: compensation and time off (recruiter topics, wrong audience), anything a search answers (what does the team build — it is on the website), and premature closers (did I pass?). Each burns a high-value slot on a low-value or awkward exchange.

title: Right question, right audience
body: The routing rule: logistics and money to the recruiter, team and technical questions to engineers, growth and evaluation questions to managers or hosts. Asking each audience what only THEY can answer is itself a demonstrated professional skill.

title: Never zero questions
body: No questions is the one guaranteed-bad answer — it reads as no engagement, however tired you are by round three. Keep a reserve question that always works: what made past interns successful here. It is round-proof and interviewer-proof.

title: Quiz: The wasted slot
body: Which question wastes the closing minutes of a technical interview most completely?
payload: {"options": ["What would a great first month look like?", "Something answerable by the company's homepage", "How does code review work on your team?", "What has surprised you about this team?"], "correct": "Something answerable by the company's homepage", "explain": "Googleable questions signal zero preparation — the exact opposite of the intended effect. The slot exists to show engagement and extract information only this person can give."}

title: How host matching works
body: After passing interviews, intern candidates enter host matching: teams with intern projects browse the pool and request calls; a mutual yes seals the placement. It is a market — being easy to place is an advantage you can engineer through flexibility and responsiveness.

title: Survey strategy: stay broad
body: On the project-preferences survey, breadth wins: narrow filters (one city, one product area) shrink the set of teams that can even see you. State genuine interests but avoid hard exclusions — every restriction is a team that never calls, and unmatched candidates simply age out.

title: Interest without exclusion
body: The phrasing move: express pull, not walls — most excited about backend and data problems, open to anything with strong mentorship — rather than only infrastructure teams. Preference-with-openness gets you shown to more teams while still steering toward fit.

title: Quiz: Survey calibration
body: On the host-matching preferences survey, why does marking many restrictions hurt you?
payload: {"options": ["It looks indecisive", "Each restriction removes teams that could have matched you, and unmatched candidates can expire", "Surveys are ignored", "Teams prefer blank surveys"], "correct": "Each restriction removes teams that could have matched you, and unmatched candidates can expire", "explain": "Matching is a two-sided market with a clock. Filters shrink your visible pool; breadth with stated preferences maximizes calls while still signaling direction."}

title: Host call preparation
body: A host call is a two-way fit conversation, not a re-interview — but preparation still wins it: skim the team's product area, form one genuine question about their work, and prepare a two-sentence bridge from something you built to something they do.

title: The bridge sentence
body: The highest-leverage prep artifact: I built a data pipeline handling messy documents — your team's ingestion problems sound like the same shape at real scale. One rehearsed bridge sentence converts a generic call into a this-person-fits call in the host's memory.

title: Enthusiasm decides ties
body: Hosts choose between several qualified candidates; visible, specific enthusiasm about THEIR problem space is often the tiebreak. Generic eagerness is invisible — your excitement should name their domain. End the call by saying directly that the project interests you, if it does.

title: Quiz: Host call prep
body: What is the single most valuable thing to prepare before a host-matching call?
payload: {"options": ["A salary figure", "A specific bridge between your past work and the team's problem space, plus one genuine question", "A rehearsed algorithm", "Nothing — it is informal"], "correct": "A specific bridge between your past work and the team's problem space, plus one genuine question", "explain": "Hosts pick the candidate who already sounds connected to their work. The bridge sentence and an informed question are ten minutes of prep that decide real tiebreaks."}

title: Responsiveness norms
body: During matching season, speed is strategy: teams often contact several candidates and proceed with whoever engages first. Same-day replies to recruiter and host emails — even a holding reply naming when you will respond fully — keep you in every race you are in.

title: Season logistics
body: Treat matching like an on-call rotation: notifications on for the relevant inbox, calendar cleared enough to take a call within a day or two, and your survey answers consistent with what you say live. Slow replies quietly forfeit matches nobody tells you about.

title: After the call
body: Same-day follow-up after a host call — two sentences thanking them and re-stating specific interest — is rare enough to be memorable and costs one minute. If you are NOT interested, saying so promptly is also right: it releases you back into the pool faster.

title: Quiz: The slow reply
body: Why can a three-day email delay during host matching quietly cost a placement?
payload: {"options": ["Recruiters penalize typos", "Teams often engage multiple candidates and proceed with whoever responds first", "Emails expire", "It cannot — timing is irrelevant"], "correct": "Teams often engage multiple candidates and proceed with whoever responds first", "explain": "Matching runs on parallel conversations with a clock. Responsiveness is one of the few fully-controllable advantages in the whole process — same-day replies keep every door open."}

title: Flexibility as an asset
body: Location and team flexibility is a genuine market advantage in matching: candidates open to any office and multiple project areas fit vastly more teams. If you are flexible, SAY it explicitly and early — to the recruiter and on the survey — because it is invisible unless stated.

title: Deploying the advantage
body: Phrase flexibility as enthusiasm, not indifference: I am genuinely open on location — the project and mentorship matter most to me. That framing reads as maturity about what makes an internship valuable, while indifference framing (whatever works) reads as low signal.

title: Flexibility with honesty
body: Only claim flexibility you actually have — accepting a mismatched placement to win the match trades a summer of misery for a matching-season advantage. If one constraint is real, state that one and stay flexible on everything else; a single honest constraint rarely sinks a broad profile.

title: Quiz: Making flexibility count
body: You are genuinely open on location and team. How do you convert that into a matching advantage?
payload: {"options": ["Keep it private to seem selective", "State it explicitly and early, framed as caring most about the project and mentorship", "Say whatever works with a shrug", "List every office as a top preference"], "correct": "State it explicitly and early, framed as caring most about the project and mentorship", "explain": "Flexibility only widens your pool if recruiters and teams KNOW about it. The project-first framing makes openness read as maturity rather than indifference."}
